background_cases: A thief is sentenced to imprisonment for stealing; we judge this just.
A murderer is executed for his crime; this too strikes us as fitting.
A negligent driver who causes an accident is fined; again, this seems appropriate.
definition: Punishment is just if and only if (i) the person punished has committed an offence, (ii) the punishment is imposed by a legitimate authority, and (iii) the severity is proportional to the gravity of the offence.
conditions: person committed an offence
imposed by legitimate authority
severity proportional to gravity
counterexample: A man committed a theft many years ago and has since entirely reformed, becoming a benefactor to his community. He is discovered and punished according to the three conditions. Yet many would doubt this punishment is just.
abductive_insight: The background cases all involved punishment proximate to the offence. The definition dropped the temporal condition, admitting cases where punishment and offence are separated by fundamental personal change.
passage:
Consider the following cases of punishment. A thief is sentenced to imprisonment for stealing; we judge this just. A murderer is executed for his crime; this too strikes us as fitting. A negligent driver who causes an accident is fined; again, this seems appropriate. From such cases, one might extract the following definition: punishment is just if and only if the person punished has committed an offence, the punishment is imposed by a legitimate authority, and the severity of the punishment is proportional to the gravity of the offence.

Yet this analysis, for all its plausibility, may be challenged. Consider a case in which a man committed a crime many years ago — a theft, let us say — and has since reformed entirely, becoming a benefactor to his community. He is discovered and punished according to the three conditions: he did commit the offence, the authority is legitimate, and the punishment is proportional. Yet many would doubt whether this punishment is just, for the man who is punished is in a relevant sense not the man who committed the crime. The background cases from which we drew our definition all involved punishment proximate to the offence; our definition failed to capture this temporal condition, and accordingly admits cases that strain our intuitions about justice.

background_cases: A person who deliberately trips a passer-by is held responsible for the resulting injury.
A woman who knowingly sells spoiled food is held responsible for the illness it causes.
A driver who recklessly speeds through a village is held responsible for striking a pedestrian.
definition: A person is morally responsible for a harmful outcome if and only if (i) their action caused the outcome, (ii) they acted voluntarily, and (iii) they could have acted otherwise.
conditions: action caused outcome
acted voluntarily
could have acted otherwise
counterexample: A man voluntarily fires a gun at a target in a field. Unbeknownst to him, a person is hiding behind the target and is struck by the bullet. His action caused the harm, he acted voluntarily, and he could have refrained. Yet we should hesitate to hold him morally responsible, for he had no reason to anticipate the harm.
abductive_insight: The background cases all involved agents who could foresee, or should have foreseen, the harmful consequence of their actions. The definition dropped the foreseeability condition.
passage:
Consider the following cases of moral responsibility. A person who deliberately trips a passer-by is held responsible for the resulting injury. A woman who knowingly sells spoiled food is held responsible for the illness it causes. A driver who recklessly speeds through a village is held responsible for striking a pedestrian. From these cases one might abstract the following definition: a person is morally responsible for a harmful outcome if and only if his action caused the outcome, he acted voluntarily, and he could have acted otherwise.

But this definition proves too broad. Suppose a man, practising his marksmanship, fires a rifle at a target set up in a remote field. He acts voluntarily, he could have refrained, and his bullet strikes and injures a person who, unknown to the marksman, was concealed behind the target. His action caused the harm. The three conditions are satisfied. Yet we should hesitate to hold him morally responsible in the fullest sense, for the harm was entirely unforeseeable. The background cases from which we drew our definition all involved agents who could foresee, or ought to have foreseen, the harmful consequences of their actions. The definition failed to capture this element of foreseeability, and accordingly admits cases in which responsibility seems attenuated or absent.

background_cases: A marksman aims carefully and kills his target; we blame him for the death.
A thief plans and executes a robbery; we blame him for the theft.
A perjurer gives false testimony deliberately; we blame him for the injustice that follows.
definition: An agent is morally blameworthy for an outcome if and only if (i) the agent performed a voluntary action, (ii) the action caused the harmful outcome, and (iii) the agent intended the outcome or was reckless as to its occurrence.
conditions: agent performed a voluntary action
action caused the harmful outcome
agent intended the outcome or was reckless as to its occurrence
counterexample: Two drivers, equally reckless, run the same red light at the same speed. One passes through without incident because no pedestrian happens to be crossing. The other strikes and kills a pedestrian. Both performed voluntary, reckless actions; but only the second caused a harmful outcome. We judge the second far more blameworthy, though the difference is mere chance.
abductive_insight: The background cases all involved agents whose voluntary actions reliably produced the harmful outcomes. The definition captured agency, causation, and culpable mental state but dropped the implicit assumption that outcome and effort are correlated, leaving it unable to handle cases where identical conduct yields different results by sheer fortune.
passage:
Consider three cases of moral blame. A marksman aims carefully and kills his target; we blame him for the death. A thief plans and executes a robbery; we blame him for the theft. A perjurer gives false testimony deliberately; we blame him for the injustice that follows. From these cases one might extract a definition: an agent is morally blameworthy for an outcome if and only if the agent performed a voluntary action, the action caused the harmful outcome, and the agent intended the outcome or was reckless as to its occurrence.

Yet this analysis is embarrassed by a simple variant. Suppose two drivers, equally reckless, each run the same red light at the same speed on the same road. The first passes through without incident, for no pedestrian happens to be crossing. The second strikes and kills a pedestrian who steps into the road at the fatal moment. Both performed voluntary actions; both were reckless as to the risk. But only the second caused a death. The definition assigns full blame to the second and none — or at most, blame for the recklessness alone — to the first. Yet the only difference between them is a matter of fortune: the presence or absence of a pedestrian at a particular instant. One might observe that the background cases from which the definition was drawn all involved scenarios in which the agent's conduct reliably produced the harmful result — there was no gap between culpable action and harmful consequence for luck to occupy. The definition captured the tight coupling of agency and outcome but failed to accommodate cases in which identical conduct issues in radically different results by sheer contingency.

background_cases: A man repays a debt punctually; we commend him as doing the right thing.
A woman tells a difficult truth when asked; we praise her as honest.
A citizen obeys a just law at some personal inconvenience; we regard him as dutiful.
definition: An action is morally praiseworthy if and only if (i) it conforms to a recognised moral obligation, (ii) the agent performs it from a sense of duty, and (iii) the performance involves overcoming some difficulty or temptation.
conditions: action conforms to a recognised moral obligation
agent performs it from a sense of duty
performance involves overcoming difficulty or temptation
counterexample: A stranger dives into a flooded river to save a drowning child, risking his own life. No recognised obligation requires such extreme self-sacrifice; he acts not from a sense of duty but from spontaneous compassion. Yet his action is praiseworthy to a degree that surpasses the ordinary dutiful acts from which the definition was derived.
abductive_insight: The background cases were all instances of compliance with recognised obligations. The definition captured the praiseworthy as the dutiful-despite-difficulty, but dropped the possibility that praiseworthiness might attach to actions that exceed obligation entirely — the supererogatory.
passage:
Consider three cases of praiseworthy conduct. A man repays a debt punctually, even when he might plausibly have delayed; we commend him. A woman tells a difficult truth when asked, though evasion would have been easier; we praise her honesty. A citizen obeys a just but burdensome law; we regard him as dutiful. From these cases one might extract the following definition: an action is morally praiseworthy if and only if it conforms to a recognised moral obligation, the agent performs it from a sense of duty, and the performance involves overcoming some difficulty or temptation.

Yet consider a case that falls outside the reach of this analysis. A stranger walking along a riverbank sees a child being swept away by floodwaters. Without hesitation he dives into the torrent, at grave risk to his own life, and succeeds in pulling the child to safety. No recognised moral obligation requires a man to risk his life for a stranger's child; no code of conduct prescribes such extreme self-sacrifice. The man acts not from a sense of duty — he has no time for deliberation — but from spontaneous compassion. The first condition is plainly unsatisfied, and the second is doubtful. Yet the action is praiseworthy in the highest degree, surpassing in our moral estimation the punctual repayment of debts and the telling of difficult truths. The background cases from which the definition was drawn were all instances of compliance with obligation; the definition therefore confined praiseworthiness to the sphere of duty and failed to accommodate actions that go beyond what any obligation demands.

background_cases: A neighbour lends her tools freely when asked; the borrower feels grateful.
A teacher gives extra lessons to a struggling pupil without charge; the pupil's parents are grateful.
A stranger gives directions to a lost traveller; the traveller feels a moment of warm appreciation.
definition: Gratitude is the appropriate response to another's action if and only if (i) the other has provided a benefit to oneself, (ii) the provision was voluntary, and (iii) the provision involved some cost or effort on the other's part.
conditions: other has provided a benefit
provision was voluntary
provision involved cost or effort
counterexample: A competitor in a race stumbles and, in falling, inadvertently blocks a rival who would otherwise have overtaken the runner in third place. The third-place runner benefits, and the stumble was voluntary in the sense that no one forced the competitor to run; it involved considerable physical effort. Yet it would be absurd to feel grateful to the fallen competitor, for the benefit was entirely accidental.
abductive_insight: The background cases all involved agents who intended the benefit they conferred. The definition captured voluntariness and cost but omitted the requirement that the benefactor have intended to benefit the recipient — that the benefit not be merely a fortunate by-product of unrelated action.
passage:
Consider three cases in which gratitude seems the natural response. A neighbour lends her tools freely when asked, and the borrower feels grateful. A teacher gives extra lessons to a struggling pupil without charge, and the pupil's parents are grateful. A stranger gives directions to a lost traveller, and the traveller feels a warm moment of appreciation. From these cases one might define the conditions under which gratitude is appropriate: gratitude is the fitting response to another's action if and only if the other has provided a benefit, the provision was voluntary, and it involved some cost or effort on the other's part.

Yet this analysis is challenged by a case of the following sort. In a footrace, a competitor stumbles and, in falling, inadvertently blocks a rival who would otherwise have overtaken the runner in third place. The third-place runner benefits materially — she finishes in a better position than she would otherwise have achieved. The fallen competitor's participation in the race was voluntary, and running a race certainly involves effort. All three conditions are satisfied. Yet it would be absurd to suggest that the third-place runner ought to feel grateful to the woman who stumbled; the benefit was entirely accidental, a by-product of misfortune rather than of any intention to help. The background cases from which the definition was drawn all involved benefactors who intended the benefit they conferred. The definition captured the outward features — voluntariness, effort, benefit — but dropped the requirement of beneficent intent, which is essential if gratitude is to be more than appreciation of fortunate coincidence.

background_cases: A fireman enters a burning building to rescue a trapped child; we call him courageous.
A dissenter speaks against a tyrant at risk of imprisonment; we admire her courage.
A physician volunteers to treat patients during a plague, knowing the risk of contagion; we regard him as brave.
definition: An action is courageous if and only if (i) the agent faces a substantial risk of serious harm, (ii) the agent is aware of the risk, and (iii) the agent acts voluntarily despite the risk.
conditions: agent faces substantial risk of serious harm
agent is aware of the risk
agent acts voluntarily despite the risk
counterexample: A man, on a drunken dare, climbs an unstable construction crane in the dark. He faces a substantial risk of death, is dimly aware of it, and acts voluntarily. All three conditions are satisfied. Yet this is not courage but recklessness — the risk is undertaken for no purpose of any worth.
abductive_insight: The background cases all involved agents who faced risk in pursuit of a morally significant end — saving a life, defending justice, serving the afflicted. The definition abstracted risk, awareness, and voluntariness but dropped the purposive element: that courage is risk borne for a worthy end.
passage:
Consider three cases of what we call courage. A fireman enters a burning building to rescue a trapped child. A political dissenter speaks against a tyrant, knowing she risks imprisonment. A physician volunteers to treat patients during a plague, fully aware that he may contract the disease. In each case, the agent faces grave risk, knows it, and acts freely. From these cases one might extract a definition: an action is courageous if and only if the agent faces a substantial risk of serious harm, is aware of the risk, and acts voluntarily despite it.

But now consider a person who, on a drunken dare at a construction site, scales an unstable crane in the dark of night. He faces a substantial risk of falling to his death; he is aware, at least dimly, of this danger; and he acts of his own free will — no one compels him. The three conditions are met. Yet it would be strange to commend this as courage. It is foolhardiness, bravado, or mere recklessness. What distinguishes this case from those of the fireman, the dissenter, and the physician is the absence of any worthy purpose: the man risks his life for nothing more than the momentary thrill of a dare. The background cases all involved agents who bore risk in service of a morally significant end — the preservation of life, the defence of justice, the care of the afflicted. The definition captured the formal features of risk-taking but failed to include the purposive element that transforms mere risk-taking into courage.

background_cases: A wife forgives her husband for a thoughtless remark after he sincerely apologises; reconciliation follows.
A man forgives a friend who broke a confidence, accepting his remorse as genuine; the friendship is restored.
A shopkeeper forgives a youth who stole from his till after the youth returns the money and expresses regret; the matter is closed.
definition: A person has forgiven a wrongdoer if and only if (i) the person was wronged, (ii) the person has deliberately relinquished resentment towards the wrongdoer, and (iii) the wrongdoer has expressed repentance.
conditions: person was wronged
person has deliberately relinquished resentment
wrongdoer has expressed repentance
counterexample: A man is gravely wronged by a now-deceased friend who never had the opportunity to repent. After long reflection, the man deliberately relinquishes his resentment towards the dead friend. The first two conditions are met, but the third is not — yet we might well say the man has forgiven.
abductive_insight: The background cases all involved living wrongdoers who expressed repentance before forgiveness was granted. The definition made repentance a necessary condition, but this was an artefact of the sample: forgiveness can be extended unilaterally, even towards those who cannot or will not repent.
passage:
Consider three cases of forgiveness. A wife forgives her husband for a thoughtless remark after he sincerely apologises; reconciliation follows. A man forgives a friend who broke a confidence, accepting his remorse as genuine; the friendship is restored. A shopkeeper forgives a youth who stole from his till after the youth returns the money and expresses regret; the matter is closed. From these cases one might define forgiveness thus: a person has forgiven a wrongdoer if and only if the person was wronged, the person has deliberately relinquished resentment towards the wrongdoer, and the wrongdoer has expressed repentance.

Yet consider a case of a different kind. A man has been gravely wronged by a close friend — deceived, let us say, in a matter of great personal importance. Before any reconciliation is possible, the friend dies. The wrongdoer never expressed repentance; the opportunity was foreclosed by death. After long and painful reflection, the wronged man comes to relinquish his resentment deliberately, not from forgetfulness or indifference, but from a considered judgement that the bitterness serves no good. The first two conditions are satisfied — he was wronged, and he has deliberately let go of his resentment. But the third is not: the wrongdoer has not expressed repentance and never can. Yet one might well say that the man has forgiven his dead friend. The background cases all featured living wrongdoers who repented before forgiveness was extended; the definition mistakenly elevated this feature into a necessary condition. Forgiveness, it seems, is fundamentally an alteration in the wronged party's attitude, and while repentance may occasion it, it need not be a precondition.

background_cases: A person who eats moderately, taking only what he needs and finding no difficulty in restraining himself, is temperate.
A woman who drinks wine only in modest quantities, never to excess and without any struggle against desire, is temperate.
A youth who governs his appetites easily and cheerfully, feeling no pull towards indulgence, is temperate.
definition: A person is temperate if and only if (i) they habitually moderates their bodily appetites, (ii) they do so without inner conflict, and (iii) their moderation extends to all appetites equally.
conditions: habitually moderates bodily appetites
does so without inner conflict
moderation extends to all appetites equally
counterexample: A person who is a model of moderation in his eating and drinking — temperate in the fullest sense with respect to food and alcohol — is nevertheless intemperate with respect to sleep, lying abed for twelve hours at a stretch and unable to resist the call of the pillow. His moderation does not extend to all appetites equally; the third condition fails. Yet he is temperate with respect to the appetites that most concern the moral tradition, and it seems wrong to deny him the title altogether.
abductive_insight: The background cases all involved persons whose moderation was comprehensive — they were moderate in all respects. The definition abstracted comprehensiveness as essential. But temperance may be domain-specific: a person may be temperate with respect to some appetites and not others, and the concept admits of partial application in a way the definition does not allow.
passage:
Consider three cases of temperance. A man eats moderately, taking only what he needs and finding no difficulty in restraint: he is temperate. A woman drinks wine only in modest quantities, never to excess and without struggle: she is temperate. A youth governs his appetites easily and cheerfully, feeling no pull towards indulgence: he is temperate. From these cases one might define temperance: a person is temperate if and only if he habitually moderates his bodily appetites, does so without inner conflict, and his moderation extends to all appetites equally.

But this definition, drawn from persons of comprehensive moderation, proves too restrictive. Consider a person who is a model of temperance in his eating and drinking. He eats sparingly, he drinks moderately, he never overindulges, and he does so without inner conflict. Yet he is intemperate with respect to sleep. He lies abed for twelve hours at a stretch; he cannot resist the call of the pillow; he is, in this one domain, wholly without self-governance. His moderation does not extend to all appetites equally; the third condition fails. Yet it seems wrong to deny that the man is temperate. He is temperate with respect to the appetites that the moral tradition has always regarded as most important — food and drink — and his failure in the domain of sleep, while perhaps a deficiency, does not seem to vitiate his temperance altogether. The background cases all involved persons of comprehensive moderation, and the definition mistook this comprehensiveness for a necessary condition. But temperance, it appears, may be domain-specific, and a person may be genuinely temperate in some respects while falling short in others.

background_cases: A person who has stolen from a friend feels shame when the theft is discovered: he has done wrong, others know of it, and he is pained by their knowledge.
A woman who has lied to her employer feels shame when the lie is exposed: she has acted dishonestly, the dishonesty is public, and she suffers for it.
A student who has cheated on an examination feels shame when confronted by his tutor: he has violated a standard, the violation is known, and he experiences distress.
definition: A person feels shame if and only if (i) they have violated a standard they accept, (ii) the violation is known or believed to be known by others, and (iii) their distress is proportional to the seriousness of the violation.
conditions: has violated standard they accept
violation known or believed known by others
distress proportional to seriousness of violation
counterexample: A man who has done nothing wrong — who has violated no standard he accepts — feels intense shame upon losing his employment through no fault of his own when his firm is dissolved. Others learn of his unemployment, and he is agonised by their knowledge of it. The first condition fails: he has violated no standard. The third condition is inapplicable, for there is no violation to which the distress might be proportioned. Yet his shame is real and devastating.
abductive_insight: The background cases all involved shame arising from moral transgression — theft, dishonesty, cheating. The definition abstracted the violation of a standard as essential. But shame may arise from conditions that involve no violation at all — involuntary unemployment, loss of status, misfortune — where the distress is prompted by perceived exposure to the judgement of others rather than by any wrong committed.
passage:
Consider three cases of shame. A person who has stolen from a friend feels shame when the theft is discovered: he has violated a standard he accepts, others know of it, and his distress is proportional to the gravity of his offence. A woman who has lied to her employer feels shame when the lie is exposed: the same pattern obtains. A student who has cheated feels shame when confronted by his tutor: again, the violation is real, the exposure is public, and the distress is commensurate. From these cases one might define shame: a person feels shame if and only if he has violated a standard he accepts, the violation is known or believed to be known by others, and his distress is proportional to the seriousness of the violation.

But this definition, drawn from cases of moral transgression, cannot accommodate shame that arises from circumstances rather than from conduct. Consider a man who has been employed for twenty years at a firm that is dissolved through no fault of his own. He has done nothing wrong; he has violated no standard he accepts or that anyone else accepts. Yet when others learn of his unemployment, he feels intense shame — shame at his condition, shame at being seen to be idle, shame at the pity and awkwardness he reads in the faces of his acquaintances. The first condition fails entirely: there is no violation. The third condition is inapplicable: there is no offence to which the distress might be proportioned. Yet his shame is as real and as painful as any shame occasioned by moral failure. The background cases all involved shame arising from wrongdoing, and the definition abstracted the violation of a standard as the essential trigger. But shame, it appears, may be triggered by perceived deficiency — by falling short of an ideal of status, capacity, or self-sufficiency — without any wrongdoing at all.

background_cases: A judge reduces a sentence upon hearing that the offender has suffered greatly: he exercises mercy by tempering deserved punishment with compassion for suffering.
A father forgives his son's disobedience and withholds the promised punishment: he exercises mercy by setting aside what is deserved in favour of clemency.
A creditor cancels the debt of a debtor who cannot pay: he exercises mercy by relinquishing a claim he is entitled to enforce.
definition: An act is merciful if and only if (i) the agent relinquishes a just claim to impose a burden upon another, (ii) the relinquishment is motivated by sympathy for the other's plight, and (iii) the other is aware of and grateful for the mercy shown.
conditions: agent relinquishes just claim to impose burden
relinquishment motivated by sympathy
other is aware of and grateful for mercy
counterexample: A magistrate secretly reduces a fine imposed upon a destitute widow, recording the lower amount in the official ledger without informing her that the original fine was higher. The widow pays the reduced amount and goes on her way, unaware that any mercy has been shown. She is neither aware of the mercy nor grateful for it. The third condition fails. Yet the magistrate's act is merciful — he has relinquished a just claim out of sympathy for her poverty.
abductive_insight: The background cases all involved mercy that was acknowledged by the recipient — the offender knows the sentence was reduced, the son knows he was forgiven, the debtor knows the debt was cancelled. The definition abstracted the recipient's awareness and gratitude as essential. But mercy may be exercised secretly, and its moral character does not depend upon whether the beneficiary knows of it.
passage:
Consider three cases of mercy. A judge reduces a sentence upon hearing of the offender's suffering: mercy is shown, and the offender is grateful. A father forgives his son's disobedience and withholds punishment: the son knows he has been spared and is grateful. A creditor cancels an unpayable debt: the debtor is relieved and thankful. From these cases one might define mercy: an act is merciful if and only if the agent relinquishes a just claim to impose a burden upon another, the relinquishment is motivated by sympathy for the other's plight, and the other is aware of and grateful for the mercy shown.

But this definition, drawn from cases of acknowledged mercy, cannot accommodate mercy exercised in secret. Consider a magistrate who imposes a fine upon a destitute widow for a minor infraction. The law prescribes a fixed penalty, and the magistrate is entitled to impose it. But moved by sympathy for her circumstances, he secretly reduces the fine, recording the lower amount in the official ledger without informing her that the original amount was higher. The widow pays the reduced fine and departs, wholly unaware that any mercy has been exercised. She is neither aware of the mercy nor grateful for it — she believes she has simply paid what was owed. The third condition fails. Yet the magistrate's act is merciful. He has relinquished a just claim out of genuine sympathy for her plight. The background cases all involved mercy that was visible to the recipient, and the definition abstracted this visibility as essential. But mercy may be exercised in secret, and its moral quality does not depend upon the recipient's awareness.

background_cases: A wealthy man gives a large sum to a hospital for the treatment of the poor: he acts charitably, transferring his own resources to those in need.
A woman donates clothing to a refuge for the destitute: she acts charitably, giving what she possesses to those who lack it.
A farmer sends a portion of his harvest to a neighbouring village struck by famine: he acts charitably, sharing his abundance with the hungry.
definition: An act is charitable if and only if (i) the agent gives from their own surplus, (ii) the recipient is in genuine need, and (iii) the giving diminishes the agent's material resources.
conditions: agent gives from own surplus
recipient in genuine need
giving diminishes agent's material resources
counterexample: A poor widow who has nothing to spare places her last two coins in the collection box for the destitute. She does not give from surplus — she has none. The giving does not diminish a comfortable stock of resources but exhausts her entirely. The first condition fails if 'surplus' is understood strictly. Yet her act is, by widespread moral intuition, the most profoundly charitable of all — she gives not from abundance but from want.
abductive_insight: The background cases all involved agents of relative plenty — a wealthy man, a woman with clothing to spare, a farmer with a harvest. The definition abstracted giving-from-surplus as essential. But the moral tradition has always recognised that the charity of the poor — giving from necessity rather than from abundance — is the highest form of charity, and the definition excludes it.
passage:
Consider three cases of charity. A wealthy man gives a large sum to a hospital for the poor: he gives from his surplus, the recipients are in genuine need, and his material resources are diminished. A woman donates clothing to a refuge: she gives what she can spare, the recipients need it, and her wardrobe is reduced. A farmer shares his harvest with a famine-struck village: he gives from abundance, the villagers need the food, and his stores are lessened. From these cases one might define charity: an act is charitable if and only if the agent gives from his own surplus, the recipient is in genuine need, and the giving diminishes the agent's material resources.

But this definition, drawn from agents of relative plenty, proves too narrow. Consider the figure of the poor widow — a figure celebrated in the moral and religious traditions of many cultures — who places her last two coins in the collection box for the destitute. She has no surplus; the two coins are all she possesses. The giving does not diminish a comfortable stock of resources; it exhausts her entirely. The first condition fails, for she does not give from surplus but from necessity. Yet her act is, by widespread and deeply held moral intuition, the most profoundly charitable of all — more charitable, many would say, than the large donations of the wealthy, for she gives everything she has. The background cases all involved agents who could afford their generosity, and the definition abstracted this affordability as essential. But the moral tradition teaches that charity is at its purest when the giver sacrifices not his surplus but his sustenance.

background_cases: A woman who has broken a neighbour's fence repairs it at her own expense: the wrongdoer restores what she damaged, and the harm is undone.
A thief who has stolen a sum of money returns it with interest: the wrongdoer compensates the victim, and the material loss is made good.
A publisher who has printed a libel issues a public retraction: the wrongdoer addresses the harm to the victim's reputation, and the injury is partly repaired.
definition: An act of reparation is adequate if and only if (i) it restores the victim to the condition they occupied before the wrong, (ii) it is performed voluntarily by the wrongdoer, and (iii) the victim accepts the reparation as sufficient.
conditions: restores victim to pre-wrong condition
performed voluntarily by wrongdoer
victim accepts reparation as sufficient
counterexample: A surgeon's negligence leaves a patient permanently disabled. No amount of compensation can restore the patient to her pre-injury condition — the damage is irreversible. The first condition fails: the victim cannot be restored to her prior state. Yet reparation is still owed, and financial compensation, though it cannot undo the harm, is a genuine reparation that acknowledges the wrong and mitigates its consequences.
abductive_insight: The background cases all involved harms that could be fully undone — a fence can be mended, stolen money returned, a reputation partially restored. The definition abstracted full restoration as essential. But many wrongs — permanent injury, death, psychological trauma — cannot be fully undone, and reparation in such cases consists not in restoration but in acknowledgement, compensation, and mitigation.
passage:
Consider three cases of reparation. A woman who has broken her neighbour's fence repairs it at her own expense: the damage is undone, and the victim is restored to her prior condition. A thief who has stolen money returns it with interest: the financial loss is made good. A publisher who has printed a libel issues a retraction: the harm to reputation is partly repaired. From these cases one might define adequate reparation: an act of reparation is adequate if and only if it restores the victim to the condition she occupied before the wrong, it is performed voluntarily by the wrongdoer, and the victim accepts it as sufficient.

But this definition, drawn from cases in which the harm is reversible, cannot accommodate wrongs that leave permanent damage. Consider a surgeon whose negligence during an operation leaves a patient permanently disabled — unable to walk, unable to work, unable to live as she lived before. No act of reparation can restore this patient to her pre-injury condition. The damage is irreversible; the first condition fails and can never be satisfied. Yet reparation is still owed. Financial compensation, though it cannot undo the disability, is a genuine form of reparation — it acknowledges the wrong, it provides the means for the patient to adjust to her altered circumstances, and it expresses a recognition of the wrongdoer's responsibility. The background cases all involved harms that could be fully reversed, and the definition abstracted full restoration as the standard of adequacy. But for permanent and irreversible injuries, reparation must be reconceived as acknowledgement and mitigation rather than as restoration.

background_cases: A factory owner pays subsistence wages to workers who have no alternative employment: he exploits their lack of options to extract labour at minimal cost.
A landlord charges extortionate rent to tenants who cannot find other housing: he exploits their dependence to extract excessive payment.
A moneylender charges ruinous interest to borrowers in desperate need: he exploits their urgency to profit at their expense.
definition: A transaction is exploitative if and only if (i) one party takes advantage of the other's vulnerability, (ii) the vulnerable party receives less than the fair value of what they provide, and (iii) the vulnerable party would not have agreed to the terms under conditions of equality.
conditions: one party takes advantage of other's vulnerability
vulnerable party receives less than fair value
vulnerable party would not agree under conditions of equality
counterexample: A pharmaceutical company sells a life-saving drug at an enormous profit to patients in wealthy countries who can easily afford the price. The patients are not vulnerable in any economic sense; they are affluent and have alternatives. They would agree to the terms even under conditions of equality, for the drug is worth the price to them. The second condition may hold — the drug costs pennies to manufacture and is sold for thousands — yet the patients are not exploited in the ordinary sense. Meanwhile, the company refuses to sell the same drug at a reduced price to impoverished patients in developing countries who will die without it. The impoverished patients are vulnerable, but no transaction occurs. The definition cannot capture the exploitation that consists in the refusal to transact with those in need.
abductive_insight: The background cases all involved transactions in which the exploited party participated and received something, however inadequate. The definition abstracted the structure of an unfair exchange as essential. But exploitation may also consist in the refusal to engage — in withholding access to essential goods from those who cannot pay — and this form of exploitation involves no transaction at all.
passage:
Consider three cases of exploitation. A factory owner pays subsistence wages to workers with no alternatives: he exploits their lack of options. A landlord charges extortionate rent to trapped tenants: he exploits their dependence. A moneylender charges ruinous interest to desperate borrowers: he exploits their urgency. From these cases one might define exploitation: a transaction is exploitative if and only if one party takes advantage of the other's vulnerability, the vulnerable party receives less than fair value, and the vulnerable party would not have agreed under conditions of equality.

But this definition, which models exploitation upon the unfair exchange, cannot accommodate exploitation by exclusion. Consider a pharmaceutical company that manufactures a life-saving drug at trivial cost and sells it at enormous profit to affluent patients in wealthy countries. The wealthy patients are not exploited in any standard sense: they can afford the price, they have alternatives, and they consent freely. But the company refuses to sell the drug at any price that impoverished patients in developing countries can afford. These patients will die without the drug. They are vulnerable in the most extreme sense. Yet no transaction occurs between them and the company; there is no exchange, fair or unfair, to evaluate. The definition requires a transaction in which the vulnerable party receives less than fair value, but here the vulnerable party receives nothing at all. The background cases all involved exploitative exchanges, and the definition abstracted the structure of unfair exchange as essential. But exploitation may also consist in the withholding of essential goods from those who cannot pay, and this exclusionary form involves no transaction whatsoever.

background_cases: A physician who, after long deliberation, resolves to tell a patient the difficult truth about his condition, because she judges honesty to be the right course, and does so with evident reluctance but firm resolve.
A magistrate who, after weighing the arguments on both sides, sentences a man to prison because he concludes that justice requires it, though he takes no pleasure in the punishment.
A teacher who, after careful reflection, decides to fail a student whose work does not meet the standard, knowing that the student will suffer, but judging that integrity demands it.
definition: A person acts virtuously if and only if (i) the person deliberates about the right course of action, (ii) the person chooses that course on the basis of a correct moral judgement, and (iii) the person acts from a sense of duty rather than from inclination.
conditions: deliberates about the right course
chooses on basis of correct moral judgement
acts from duty rather than inclination
counterexample: A woman of deeply compassionate temperament encounters a lost and frightened child in the street. Without deliberation, without any conscious exercise of moral judgement, and entirely from inclination — from the spontaneous movement of sympathy — she kneels beside the child, speaks gently, and remains with him until his parents are found. She does not act from duty; she acts from love. All three conditions fail, yet her action is virtuous in the fullest sense.
abductive_insight: The background cases all involved persons acting rightly despite contrary inclination, after deliberation, from a sense of duty. The definition abstracted these features as essential to virtuous action. But the compassionate woman reveals that virtue may flow from spontaneous fellow-feeling rather than from deliberation and duty — that the kind heart may be as genuine a source of virtue as the dutiful will, and perhaps a higher one.
passage:
Consider three cases of virtuous action. A physician who, after long deliberation, resolves to tell her patient the difficult truth about his condition, because she judges honesty to be required, and does so with evident reluctance but firm resolve. A magistrate who, after weighing the arguments, sentences a man to imprisonment because justice demands it, though the task is painful to him. A teacher who, after careful thought, fails a student whose work does not meet the standard, knowing the hardship this will cause, but judging that integrity requires it. From these cases one might extract a definition: a person acts virtuously if and only if the person deliberates about the right course, chooses that course on the basis of a correct moral judgement, and acts from a sense of duty rather than from inclination.

But this definition, drawn from cases of reluctant rectitude, proves too narrow when applied to other forms of virtuous action. Consider a woman of deeply compassionate temperament who, walking through a market town, encounters a small child standing alone in the street, lost and frightened. She does not deliberate. No process of moral reasoning precedes her response. She kneels beside the child without a moment's hesitation, speaks to him gently, takes his hand, and remains with him until his parents are found. She acts not from duty but from the spontaneous movement of sympathy — from an immediate and unreflective compassion that is as natural to her as breathing. All three conditions of the definition fail: she did not deliberate, she did not choose on the basis of a conscious moral judgement, and she acted from inclination rather than from duty. Yet her action is virtuous in a deep and important sense. The background cases were all cases in which virtue required the overcoming of contrary inclination through deliberation and resolve. The definition mistook this pattern for the essence of virtue, overlooking the possibility that the most admirable moral actions may spring not from the hard exercise of duty but from a settled disposition of sympathy and love that moves the agent to goodness without the mediation of rational calculation.

background_cases: A factory in which all workers performing the same task receive the same wage, regardless of seniority or personal circumstances.
A school examination in which every student is given the same questions, the same time, and the same marking criteria.
A rationing system during wartime in which every citizen receives the same quantity of bread, meat, and sugar, irrespective of age, occupation, or need.
definition: A distribution or procedure is just if and only if (i) it treats all persons identically, (ii) no person is singled out for special advantage or disadvantage, and (iii) the same standard is applied in every case without exception.
conditions: treats all persons identically
no person singled out for advantage or disadvantage
same standard applied in every case
counterexample: A hospital receiving casualties after an accident practises triage: the most severely injured are treated first, the less severely injured must wait, and those whose injuries are minor are attended to last. Persons are not treated identically; some are singled out for prior attention; the standard varies according to the severity of the injury. All three conditions fail, yet triage is regarded not merely as expedient but as just — it would be unjust to treat a man with a broken finger before a woman who is bleeding to death, merely because he arrived first.
abductive_insight: The background cases were all situations in which identical treatment was appropriate because the persons involved were relevantly similar — workers doing the same job, students sitting the same examination, citizens with the same basic needs. The definition abstracted identical treatment as essential to justice, but the triage case reveals that when persons differ in morally relevant respects — the severity of their need — justice may require differential treatment, and identical treatment would itself be unjust.
passage:
Consider three cases of just procedure. A factory in which all workers performing the same task receive the same wage, with no exceptions for seniority or personal circumstance. A school examination in which every student receives the same questions, the same time, and the same marking criteria. A wartime rationing system in which every citizen receives identical quantities of bread, meat, and sugar. From these cases one might define justice: a distribution or procedure is just if and only if it treats all persons identically, no person is singled out for special advantage or disadvantage, and the same standard is applied in every case without exception.

But this definition, drawn from contexts in which the persons involved were relevantly alike, proves inadequate when applied to situations in which they are relevantly different. Consider a hospital that receives a large number of casualties following an accident. The physicians practise triage: those in immediate danger of death are treated first; those with serious but not life-threatening injuries must wait; those with minor hurts are attended to last. The procedure treats persons very differently indeed. Some are rushed to the operating theatre while others sit in corridors. The standard varies according to the severity of the injury. All three conditions of the definition fail. Yet triage is not merely expedient; it is just. To treat a man with a sprained wrist before a woman whose life is in danger, merely because the man arrived at the hospital first or because identical treatment requires attending to patients in alphabetical order, would be a grotesque injustice. The background cases involved persons who were relevantly similar, and identical treatment was appropriate precisely because of that similarity. The definition mistook a feature of those particular cases for a universal requirement, when in truth justice demands that relevantly different cases be treated differently, and identical treatment in the face of unequal need may itself constitute the deepest form of injustice.

background_cases: A lifeguard who is contracted to watch over swimmers at a public beach and is obligated to rescue anyone in danger.
A doctor who has taken the Hippocratic oath and is obligated to treat patients brought to her care.
A soldier who has sworn an oath of service and is obligated to defend the territory he has been assigned to protect.
definition: A person is under a moral obligation to render aid to a stranger if and only if (i) the person has voluntarily assumed a role that carries a duty of aid, (ii) the person has the training or competence required to render aid effectively, and (iii) the person is on duty or otherwise formally engaged in the relevant role at the time.
conditions: voluntarily assumed role carrying duty of aid
has training or competence to render aid
on duty or formally engaged in role
counterexample: A retired shopkeeper with no medical training and no formal role of any kind is walking through a park when he sees a man collapse, clutching his chest. The shopkeeper has assumed no role that carries a duty of aid, he has no medical competence, and he is not on duty in any capacity. All three conditions fail. Yet it appears that he is under a moral obligation to go to the man, to call for help, to loosen his collar, to do whatever lies within his power — however limited — to assist a fellow human being in distress.
abductive_insight: The background cases all involved persons who had voluntarily entered professional roles with formal duties of aid — a lifeguard, a doctor, a soldier. The definition abstracted role-assumption, competence, and active duty as necessary for the obligation to aid. But the case of the retired shopkeeper reveals that a basic moral obligation to render such aid as one can arises from the bare fact of encountering another human being in acute distress, prior to any assumption of role, any special competence, or any formal engagement.
passage:
Consider three cases of obligation to render aid. A lifeguard contracted to watch over swimmers at a public beach, who is obligated to rescue anyone in danger. A doctor bound by her oath to treat patients brought to her care. A soldier who has sworn to defend the territory assigned to him. From these cases one might extract a definition: a person is under a moral obligation to render aid to a stranger if and only if the person has voluntarily assumed a role that carries a duty of aid, has the training or competence to render aid effectively, and is on duty or otherwise formally engaged in that role at the time.

But this definition, drawn from professional and institutional obligations, proves too restrictive when applied to the moral life as a whole. Consider a retired shopkeeper — a person of no special training, no formal role, and no connection to any institution of aid — who is walking through a public park on a quiet afternoon. He sees a man some yards ahead collapse suddenly to the ground, clutching his chest and gasping for breath. The shopkeeper has assumed no role that carries a duty of aid. He has no medical competence whatever. He is not on duty in any capacity; he is merely taking a walk. All three conditions of the definition fail. Yet it appears beyond serious question that he is under a moral obligation to act. He must go to the man. He must call for assistance. He must do whatever lies within his power, however modest, to help — loosen the man's collar, reassure him, stay with him until help arrives. To walk past, to avert one's gaze, to treat the suffering of a fellow creature as none of one's concern, would be a moral failing of the gravest kind. The background cases were all drawn from professional life, where obligation is tied to role and competence. The definition mistook these institutional features for the ground of all obligation to aid, when in truth the most fundamental obligation arises from nothing more than the encounter between one human being and another in a condition of helpless distress.

background_cases: A court that sentences a burglar to six months' imprisonment for breaking into a dwelling.
A tribunal that orders a fraudster to repay the sum he stole and to serve an additional period of community labour.
A magistrate who fines a man for assaulting his neighbour, the fine being calculated to reflect the severity of the injury inflicted.
definition: A punishment is just if and only if (i) it is imposed upon a person who has been found to have committed a specific, identifiable wrong, (ii) the severity of the punishment corresponds to the gravity of the wrong, and (iii) the punishment is directed solely at the person who committed the wrong.
conditions: imposed upon person found to have committed a specific wrong
severity corresponds to gravity of wrong
directed solely at person who committed wrong
counterexample: Consider a case in which a woman is sentenced to two years' imprisonment for a serious assault. The punishment satisfies all three conditions. But the woman has a wife and three young children who depend entirely upon her earnings. Her imprisonment plunges them into destitution. The punishment is directed solely at the offender, yet in practice it falls with terrible weight upon persons who have committed no wrong at all. The definition has no resources with which to address this difficulty, for it attends only to the relation between the punishment and the offender and takes no account of the collateral suffering visited upon the innocent.
abductive_insight: The background cases all involved offenders whose punishment could be conceived as affecting them alone — the burglar, the fraudster, the assailant. The definition abstracted from these cases the principle that punishment is a transaction between the state and the wrongdoer. But the imprisoned mother reveals that punishment rarely affects the offender alone; it radiates outward to dependants and associates, and a definition of just punishment that ignores these effects is incomplete, for justice must attend not only to the guilt of the offender but to the suffering of the innocent.
passage:
Consider three cases of just punishment. A court sentences a burglar to six months' imprisonment. A tribunal orders a fraudster to repay what he stole and to perform community labour. A magistrate fines a man for assault, calibrating the fine to the severity of the injury. From these cases one might define just punishment: a punishment is just if and only if it is imposed upon a person found to have committed a specific wrong, its severity corresponds to the gravity of that wrong, and it is directed solely at the person who committed the wrong.

But this definition, drawn from cases in which the punishment could be conceived as touching the offender alone, proves inadequate when the punishment's effects extend beyond her. Consider a woman sentenced to two years' imprisonment for a serious assault. She has committed a specific wrong. The sentence corresponds to its gravity. It is directed solely at her. All three conditions are satisfied. But the woman has a wife and three young children who depend entirely upon her earnings. Her imprisonment leaves them without income, without support, and without a mother. They are plunged into hardship through no fault of their own. The definition pronounces the punishment just, for it attends only to the relation between the sentence and the offender, but it is silent about the destitution of the offender's family. The background cases involved offenders who could be imagined as isolated individuals, and the definition abstracted from these a conception of punishment as a bilateral affair between the wrongdoer and the authority that punishes her. It overlooked the plain fact that punishment rarely affects the offender alone. It radiates outward, falling upon spouses, children, parents, and dependants who have committed no offence. A complete account of just punishment must reckon with these collateral consequences, for justice that is blind to the suffering of the innocent is justice of a very imperfect sort.

background_cases: A carpenter who builds a defective staircase through carelessness, and a visitor falls and is injured: the carpenter is responsible because his carelessness caused the harm.
A chemist who mislabels a compound through negligence, and a colleague is poisoned: the chemist is responsible because his negligence caused the harm.
A driver who fails to check his mirrors before changing lanes, causing a collision: the driver is responsible because his inattention caused the accident.
definition: A person is morally responsible for a harm if and only if (i) the person's action or negligence was the proximate cause of the harm, (ii) the person could have foreseen that the harm might result, and (iii) the person had the capacity to act differently.
conditions: action or negligence was proximate cause of harm
could have foreseen harm might result
had capacity to act differently
counterexample: A factory owner operates a chemical works that, unknown to him and to the scientific community of his day, emits a substance that causes a rare illness among the residents of the neighbouring town. The illness manifests only after years of exposure, and no scientist of the period has identified the substance as harmful. The owner's operation of the factory is the proximate cause of the harm, and he had the capacity to cease operations. But he could not have foreseen the harm, for the danger was unknown to anyone. The second condition fails. Yet when the connection between the emissions and the illness is later established, many persons feel that the factory owner bears some measure of responsibility for the suffering his enterprise has caused, even though he could not have anticipated it.
abductive_insight: The background cases all involved harms that were foreseeable — a defective staircase, a mislabelled compound, an unchecked mirror. The definition abstracted foreseeability as essential. But the factory case reveals that responsibility may attach to the causation of serious harm even when that harm was unforeseeable, particularly when the responsible party profited from the activity that produced the harm; strict moral responsibility, like strict legal liability, may obtain where foresight does not.
passage:
Consider three cases of moral responsibility. A carpenter who builds a defective staircase through carelessness, injuring a visitor. A chemist who mislabels a compound through negligence, poisoning a colleague. A driver who changes lanes without checking his mirrors, causing a collision. In each case, the person's carelessness was the proximate cause of the harm, the harm was foreseeable, and the person could have acted with greater care. From these cases one might extract a definition: a person is morally responsible for a harm if and only if the person's action or negligence was the proximate cause of the harm, the person could have foreseen that the harm might result, and the person had the capacity to act differently.

But this definition encounters a difficulty with harms that were unforeseeable at the time they were caused. Suppose that a factory owner in the late nineteenth century operates a chemical works. The factory emits a substance that, unbeknown to the owner and to the entire scientific community of his day, causes a rare and debilitating illness when inhaled over many years. The residents of the neighbouring town fall ill, but the connection between the emissions and the disease is not established until decades later, long after the damage has been done. The owner's operation of the factory was undoubtedly the proximate cause of the harm. He had the capacity to act differently — he could have operated a different enterprise or employed different processes. But he could not have foreseen the harm, for the danger was entirely unknown. The second condition fails. Yet when the causal connection is eventually discovered, there is a powerful moral intuition that the factory owner bears responsibility for the suffering his enterprise inflicted, particularly since he profited handsomely from the very activity that caused the harm. The background cases all involved foreseeable dangers, and the definition abstracted foreseeability as essential. But the factory case suggests that the causation of grave harm may generate responsibility even in the absence of foresight, particularly when the agent benefited from the harmful activity.

background_cases: A patient who, after a thorough explanation of the risks and benefits of a surgical procedure, signs a consent form in the presence of a witness.
A citizen who, upon reaching the age of majority, takes an oath of allegiance and thereby consents to the obligations of citizenship.
A bride and groom who, before an officiant and witnesses, exchange vows and thereby consent to the terms of marriage.
definition: A person's consent to an arrangement is morally binding if and only if (i) the consent is expressed in a single, identifiable act — a signature, an oath, a declaration, (ii) the person is aware at the moment of consent of the specific obligations being undertaken, and (iii) the act of consent is witnessed or otherwise publicly recorded.
conditions: consent expressed in single identifiable act
person aware at moment of consent of specific obligations
act of consent is witnessed or publicly recorded
counterexample: Consider a case in which a man moves to a new town, joins its clubs and associations, participates in its governance, pays its local taxes, sends his children to its schools, and over the course of many years becomes deeply embedded in the life of the community. He has never performed any single act of consent — no oath, no signature, no declaration. He was not aware, at any particular moment, of the totality of obligations he was assuming. No act of consent was witnessed or recorded. All three conditions fail. Yet it appears that he has consented, through the accumulated weight of his participation, to the norms and obligations of the community. His consent is diffused across a thousand acts rather than concentrated in one.
abductive_insight: The background cases all involved discrete ceremonial acts — signing a form, taking an oath, exchanging vows — in which consent was concentrated in a single public moment. The definition abstracted this ceremonial structure as essential. But the case of the man who gradually integrates into a community reveals that consent may be constituted not by a single act but by a pattern of conduct extended over time, in which participation itself becomes the expression of assent.
passage:
Consider three cases of binding consent. A patient who, after hearing a full explanation of risks, signs a consent form in the presence of a witness. A citizen who, upon reaching majority, takes an oath of allegiance and consents to the obligations of citizenship. A bride and groom who exchange vows before an officiant and witnesses. From these cases one might extract a definition: a person's consent to an arrangement is morally binding if and only if it is expressed in a single, identifiable act, the person is aware at the moment of consent of the specific obligations being undertaken, and the act is witnessed or publicly recorded.

But this definition, drawn from ceremonial occasions, proves too narrow when applied to other forms of moral consent. Consider a person who moves to a new town. Over the first months and years he joins its clubs and associations, participates in its public meetings, pays the local taxes that support its schools and roads, sends his children to those schools, and gradually becomes one of the most active members of the community. He takes part in its festivals, contributes to its charities, and sits on its committees. At no single moment does he perform a discrete act of consent. He signs no document pledging adherence to the town's norms. He takes no oath. He is not, at any identifiable moment, aware of the full catalogue of obligations he is assuming, for they accumulate gradually as his involvement deepens. No act of consent is witnessed or recorded. All three conditions of the definition fail. Yet it appears that he has consented, through the accumulated weight of years of participation, to the norms, obligations, and expectations of the community. His consent is not concentrated in a single moment but diffused across a thousand acts of engagement. The background cases were all ceremonial, and the definition mistook the ceremonial form of consent for its essence. It overlooked the possibility that consent may be constituted by a pattern of conduct extended over time, in which sustained participation itself becomes the vehicle of assent.

background_cases: A student who studies with great diligence throughout the term and earns the highest mark in the examination: she deserves the prize because her effort produced the result.
A farmer who labours from dawn to dusk, tending his fields with care, and produces an abundant harvest: he deserves the profits because his toil brought forth the crop.
An athlete who trains rigorously for years and wins the championship: he deserves the medal because his sustained effort achieved the victory.
definition: A person deserves the benefits that flow from an activity if and only if (i) the person exerted sustained effort in the activity, (ii) the effort was the primary cause of the beneficial outcome, and (iii) the person freely chose to exert that effort.
conditions: exerted sustained effort in activity
effort was primary cause of beneficial outcome
freely chose to exert effort
counterexample: A man inherits a plot of land in a region where, unbeknown to him, vast deposits of oil lie beneath the surface. He has exerted no effort towards the discovery of oil, the beneficial outcome is not caused by any effort on his part, and he made no relevant choice — the inheritance came to him by the accident of birth and the provisions of a will. All three conditions fail. Yet there is a widespread and deeply held conviction that the landowner deserves the wealth that flows from his inheritance, or at the very least that he has a legitimate claim to it that others must respect.
abductive_insight: The background cases were all cases in which effort produced a result — studying produced high marks, farming produced crops, training produced victory. The definition abstracted effort, causation, and choice as the grounds of desert. But the case of the inherited oil wealth reveals that desert may attach to ownership and inheritance independently of effort, suggesting that our ordinary conception of desert draws upon multiple and perhaps incompatible sources — effort, ownership, entitlement, and fortune — that no single definition can unify.
passage:
Consider three cases of deserved benefit. A student who studies with great diligence and earns the highest mark: she deserves the prize. A farmer who labours from dawn to dusk and produces an abundant harvest: he deserves the profits. An athlete who trains for years and wins the championship: he deserves the medal. From these cases one might extract a definition: a person deserves the benefits that flow from an activity if and only if the person exerted sustained effort in the activity, the effort was the primary cause of the beneficial outcome, and the person freely chose to exert that effort.

But this definition, drawn from cases in which effort produces a result, encounters a difficulty with benefits that arise from ownership rather than from labour. Consider a person who inherits a plot of land in a remote district. He has done nothing with the land; it lies as his father left it. But beneath the surface, unknown to anyone, there are vast deposits of oil. When the oil is discovered, the man becomes enormously wealthy. He exerted no effort towards this outcome. The wealth was not caused by anything he did; it was caused by a geological accident and the accident of inheritance. He made no relevant choice — the land came to him through a will, and the oil was there long before he was born. All three conditions of the definition fail. Yet there is a widespread and deeply rooted conviction that the landowner has a legitimate claim to the wealth, that it is in some sense his due, and that to deprive him of it would be unjust. The background cases were all cases in which effort and desert were naturally aligned. The definition abstracted this alignment as a universal principle, but the case of the inherited wealth exposes the fact that our ordinary notion of desert draws upon sources beyond effort — upon ownership, upon entitlement, upon the conventions of inheritance — that cannot be reduced to the exertion of labour and the exercise of choice.

background_cases: A soldier who, despite intense fear, holds his position under enemy fire, enduring personal danger to protect his comrades.
A fireman who enters a burning building to rescue a trapped child, facing the risk of death from the collapsing structure.
A sailor who remains at the wheel during a violent storm, refusing to abandon his post though the ship may founder.
definition: A person S possesses the virtue of courage if and only if (i) S faces a situation involving serious physical danger, (ii) S experiences fear in the face of that danger, and (iii) S acts rightly despite their fear, holding firm rather than fleeing.
conditions: S faces a situation involving serious physical danger
S experiences fear in the face of that danger
S acts rightly despite their fear, holding firm rather than fleeing
counterexample: Consider a person who, knowing that he will be scorned and ostracised by his community, speaks publicly in defence of an unpopular truth — that a man condemned by popular opinion is in fact innocent. He faces no physical danger whatsoever; no one threatens his person. But he experiences a fear that is no less real for being social rather than physical: the fear of ridicule, exclusion, and the loss of his standing among those whose regard he values. He acts rightly despite this fear, speaking when silence would have been far easier. His courage is genuine, yet the definition excludes it because the danger he faces is not physical.
abductive_insight: The background cases were all instances of physical courage — the soldier under fire, the fireman in the burning building, the sailor in the storm — in which the danger was bodily and the fear was of injury or death. The definition abstracted physical danger as a necessary condition of courage because it was present in every case examined. But the cases were drawn from a narrow class of situations in which courage is exercised against threats to the body. The case of the man who speaks an unpopular truth reveals that courage may be exercised against threats to reputation, social standing, and livelihood, and that the fear of scorn and ostracism may call upon the same virtue as the fear of death. The definition mistook the particular form of danger present in its background cases for the essence of the situations that call forth courage.
passage:
Consider three cases of courage. A soldier who, despite intense and acknowledged fear, holds his position under enemy fire, enduring the immediate danger of death to protect his comrades. A fireman who enters a burning building to rescue a child, knowing that the roof may collapse upon him. A sailor who remains at the helm during a violent storm, refusing to abandon his post though the vessel may be lost. These cases share certain features: in each, the agent faces serious physical danger; in each, he experiences fear; in each, he acts rightly despite his fear, holding firm rather than fleeing. From these cases one might extract a definition: a person possesses the virtue of courage if and only if he faces a situation involving serious physical danger, experiences fear in the face of that danger, and acts rightly despite his fear.

But one must consider the man who speaks an unpopular truth. Suppose that in a small community a man has been condemned by popular opinion — let us say he has been accused of a dishonourable act, and the accusation has been accepted by nearly everyone. One man, having investigated the matter, is convinced that the accused is innocent. He knows that if he speaks in the man's defence he will be scorned by his neighbours, excluded from the society he has enjoyed all his life, and regarded as a fool or a traitor to the community's judgement. No one threatens his person; the danger he faces is entirely social. Yet the fear he experiences is real and powerful — the fear of ridicule, ostracism, and the loss of the regard of those whose opinion he values. He speaks nevertheless, publicly and clearly, because he believes the truth requires it. His courage is of a different kind from that of the soldier or the fireman, but it is courage. The definition, drawn from cases in which the danger was physical and the fear was of bodily harm, failed to accommodate the courage that is exercised against social danger — against the threat of scorn, exclusion, and the destruction of one's standing among one's fellows. It mistook the physical form of danger, which happened to be present in every background case, for an essential feature of the situations that call forth the virtue of courage.

background_cases: A physician who has taken the Hippocratic oath is obligated to treat the sick, having voluntarily assumed the duties of his profession.
A barrister who has accepted a brief is obligated to represent his client zealously, having voluntarily entered into the professional relationship.
A trustee who has accepted the management of a trust fund is obligated to administer it prudently, having voluntarily assumed the fiduciary responsibility.
definition: A person S has a moral obligation to perform an action A if and only if (i) S has voluntarily entered into a role or relationship that carries the duty to perform A, (ii) S was aware, at the time of entering the role, that it carried the duty to perform A, and (iii) S has the competence and resources to perform A.
conditions: S has voluntarily entered into a role or relationship that carries the duty to perform A
S was aware, at the time of entering the role, that the duty attached to it
S has the competence and resources to perform A
counterexample: Consider a person who, walking along a deserted canal, sees a stranger's child drowning in the water. He has entered into no role or relationship that carries a duty to rescue the child. He was not aware, at any time, of any duty attaching to any such role, for no role is in question. He may or may not have the competence to swim. Yet he has a moral obligation to attempt a rescue — or at the very least to seek assistance — for the child's life is in immediate peril and he is the only person present. The obligation arises not from any voluntary undertaking but from the bare fact of the child's desperate need and his capacity to help.
abductive_insight: The background cases were all instances of obligation arising from voluntarily assumed professional roles — the physician's oath, the barrister's brief, the trustee's acceptance. The definition abstracted voluntary role-assumption, awareness of attendant duties, and competence as the essential conditions of moral obligation. But the cases were drawn from the domain of professional and fiduciary relationships, in which obligation is indeed grounded in voluntary undertaking. The drowning child reveals that moral obligation may arise from circumstances that no one has chosen and no role has created — from the sheer conjunction of another's urgent need and one's capacity to help. The definition mistook the source of obligation in professional life for the source of obligation as such.
passage:
Consider three cases of moral obligation. A physician, having taken the oath of his profession, is obligated to treat the sick who come to him; he voluntarily assumed the role and was aware of its duties. A barrister, having accepted a brief, is obligated to represent his client with diligence and zeal; he entered the professional relationship knowingly. A trustee, having accepted the management of a fund, is obligated to administer it with prudence and fidelity; the duty was attached to the role, and he assumed the role freely. From these cases one might extract a definition: a person has a moral obligation to perform an action if and only if he has voluntarily entered into a role carrying the relevant duty, was aware at the time of entering the role that the duty attached to it, and has the competence and resources to perform the action.

But one must consider the case of the drowning child. A man walks along a deserted canal on a winter afternoon. He has no particular business; he is taking the air. He sees, some twenty yards ahead, a young child who has fallen into the water and is struggling to stay afloat. There is no one else in sight. The man has entered into no role or relationship that carries a duty to rescue children from canals. He is not a lifeguard, not a physician, not a parent of this child. He was aware of no duty attaching to any such role, for no role is in question. He is an ordinary citizen going about his ordinary affairs. Yet he has a moral obligation to help — to wade into the water if he can, to throw his coat or a branch if he cannot swim, to shout for assistance if nothing else is possible. The obligation arises unbidden, from the bare conjunction of the child's mortal peril and the man's presence as the sole potential rescuer. The definition, drawn from cases in which obligation was grounded in the voluntary assumption of professional roles, failed to accommodate the obligations that spring from circumstances rather than from choices — from the fact that one is present, one is able, and another human being is in desperate need. It mistook the mechanism by which obligations arise in professional life for the only mechanism by which moral obligations can arise at all.

background_cases: A person of full age and sound mind agrees, after careful deliberation, to sell his house to a buyer at a price both parties consider fair.
A woman, having read and understood the terms of an employment contract, signs the document freely and without pressure.
Two merchants, each represented by competent counsel, negotiate the terms of a partnership and assent to the final agreement after prolonged discussion.
definition: An agreement between parties is morally valid if and only if (i) each party is a competent adult capable of understanding the terms, (ii) each party has been afforded adequate time for deliberation, and (iii) no party is in a position of significantly unequal bargaining power relative to the other.
conditions: each party is a competent adult capable of understanding the terms
each party has been afforded adequate time for deliberation
no party is in a position of significantly unequal bargaining power
counterexample: Consider a desperately ill man who agrees to undergo a dangerous surgical procedure. The surgeon explains the risks clearly; the man is a competent adult who understands the terms perfectly. He has been given time to deliberate.
