But the bargaining power is profoundly unequal: the surgeon possesses the skill and knowledge upon which the man's life depends, and the man has no real alternative. He agrees not because the terms are fair in any ordinary sense but because the only alternative is death. Yet the agreement is morally valid, and the surgeon is justified in proceeding. The inequality of bargaining power does not vitiate the consent, because the alternative to agreement is not merely disadvantageous but catastrophic.
abductive_insight: The background cases were all commercial or contractual agreements between parties of roughly comparable standing — a house sale, an employment contract, a partnership — in which approximate equality of bargaining power was a reasonable background condition. The definition abstracted this equality as essential to the moral validity of any agreement. But the case of the desperately ill patient reveals that an agreement may be morally valid even when the inequality of bargaining power is extreme, provided that the party in the weaker position has no acceptable alternative and the terms offered are not exploitative given the circumstances. The definition mistook a feature of fair commercial transactions for a necessary condition of all morally valid agreements, overlooking the domain of agreements made under necessity, where inequality of position is an ineliminable feature of the situation and does not by itself invalidate the consent.
passage:
Consider three agreements. A person of full age and sound mind agrees to sell his house at a price that both he and the buyer consider fair, after careful reflection. A woman reads an employment contract, understands its terms, and signs it freely, without any pressure from her prospective employer. Two merchants, each assisted by competent counsel, negotiate the terms of a business partnership over several weeks and assent to the final agreement. From these cases one might extract a definition: an agreement between parties is morally valid if and only if each party is a competent adult capable of understanding the terms, each party has been afforded adequate time for deliberation, and no party occupies a position of significantly unequal bargaining power relative to the other. The three cases satisfy all three conditions.

But one must consider the case of the desperately ill patient. A man is gravely ill — let us say he has a condition that will kill him within weeks unless a difficult and dangerous surgical procedure is performed. A surgeon explains the procedure: the risks are severe, the recovery will be long and painful, and the outcome is uncertain. The man understands the explanation perfectly; he is a competent adult of sound mind. He has been given time to reflect. But the bargaining power between the two parties is profoundly unequal. The surgeon possesses the skill and knowledge upon which the man's life depends; the man possesses nothing that the surgeon needs. The man agrees to the procedure not because the terms are fair in any commercial sense but because the only alternative is death. The third condition of the definition fails conspicuously — the inequality of bargaining power could scarcely be greater. Yet the agreement is morally valid. The surgeon is justified in performing the operation, and the man's consent, though given from a position of desperation, is genuine. The definition, drawn from cases of commercial agreement between parties of roughly equal standing, mistook the approximate equality of bargaining power that characterises fair market transactions for a necessary condition of all morally valid agreements, failing to account for agreements made under necessity, where extreme inequality of position is an ineliminable feature of the situation.

background_cases: A thief who steals a loaf of bread is sentenced to a short term of imprisonment — a punishment clearly less severe than the punishment for armed robbery.
A man convicted of assault is sentenced to a longer term of imprisonment than the bread-thief — a punishment proportioned to the greater severity of his offence.
A murderer is sentenced to the harshest punishment available — a punishment reflecting the supreme gravity of the crime of taking a human life.
definition: A system of punishments is just if and only if (i) the severity of each punishment is strictly proportional to the gravity of the offence, (ii) the scale of punishments forms a strict linear ordering from least to most severe, and (iii) identical offences receive identical punishments without exception.
conditions: the severity of each punishment is strictly proportional to the gravity of the offence
the scale of punishments forms a strict linear ordering
identical offences receive identical punishments without exception
counterexample: Consider two men who commit the same offence — the theft of a sum of money from an employer. The facts of the crime are identical. But the first man stole because he wished to enrich himself at another's expense, whilst the second stole because his child was gravely ill and he could not afford the physician's fee. A just tribunal might well impose a lighter punishment upon the second man, recognising that the circumstances, though they do not excuse the crime, mitigate its moral gravity. The third condition — that identical offences receive identical punishments — is violated, yet the differentiation is an expression of justice, not a departure from it.
abductive_insight: The background cases were all instances in which the offence could be characterised simply and the appropriate punishment determined by the gravity of the act alone — theft, assault, murder — without attention to the circumstances or motivations of the offender. The definition abstracted strict proportionality, linear ordering, and identical treatment of identical offences as the marks of a just system. But the cases were drawn from a simplified picture of criminal justice in which each offence was considered in isolation from the character and circumstances of the offender. The case of the two thieves reveals that justice may require attention to the particular circumstances of the offender — his motivations, his personal situation, the pressures under which he acted — and that identical offences may justly receive different punishments when the circumstances differ. The definition mistook the abstract schema of proportionality for the whole of penal justice, neglecting the role of mitigation and individualised judgement.
passage:
Consider three cases of punishment. A thief who steals a loaf of bread receives a short term of imprisonment — a punishment plainly less severe than what would be imposed for a more serious crime. A man convicted of assault receives a longer sentence, proportioned to the greater gravity of his offence. A murderer receives the harshest penalty the law affords, reflecting the supreme seriousness of the act of taking a human life. From these cases one might extract a definition: a system of punishments is just if and only if the severity of each punishment is strictly proportional to the gravity of the offence, the punishments form a strict linear ordering from least to most severe, and identical offences receive identical punishments without exception.

But one must consider the case of two men who commit the same crime under different circumstances. Two clerks, employed by the same firm, each steal the same sum of money from the till. The facts of the offence are identical in every material respect. But the first clerk stole because he wished to live beyond his means — to furnish himself with luxuries that his salary would not support. The second clerk stole because his young child was gravely ill, the physician demanded payment before he would treat the child, and the clerk had exhausted every legitimate means of obtaining the money. A just tribunal, confronted with these two cases, might well impose a lighter punishment upon the second man. The offence is the same, but the circumstances are profoundly different, and the moral gravity of an act performed in desperation to save a child's life is not the same as the moral gravity of the identical act performed from avarice. The third condition of the definition is violated: identical offences receive different punishments. Yet the differentiation is not an injustice but an expression of justice — a recognition that the moral quality of an action depends not only upon what was done but upon why it was done and under what pressures. The definition, drawn from cases in which the offence was considered in the abstract, mistook the schema of strict proportionality for the whole of penal justice, overlooking the indispensable role of mitigation and individualised judgement.

background_cases: A landowner's right to exclude trespassers from his property — a right he exercises by building a fence and posting a notice.
A creditor's right to repayment of a debt — a right he exercises by presenting his claim to the debtor and, if necessary, to a court.
An author's right to the profits from his published work — a right he exercises by collecting royalties through his publisher.
definition: A person S has a right to X if and only if (i) S can exercise the right by performing a specific action, (ii) S can waive the right voluntarily, and (iii) there exists a correlative duty upon some identifiable person or persons to respect or fulfil the right.
conditions: S can exercise the right by performing a specific action
S can waive the right voluntarily
there exists a correlative duty upon some identifiable person or persons
counterexample: Consider the right of every person not to be tortured. This right cannot be exercised by performing any specific action; it is not an active entitlement but a passive protection. It cannot be waived voluntarily — or at least, the claim that a person can waive his right not to be tortured is morally and legally contested in the strongest possible terms. And the correlative duty falls not upon some identifiable person or persons but upon everyone without exception: every person, every government, every institution has a duty not to torture. Yet the right not to be tortured is among the most fundamental of all moral rights.
abductive_insight: The background cases were all instances of active, waivable rights held against identifiable duty-bearers — the right to exclude, the right to repayment, the right to royalties. The definition abstracted exercisability, waivability, and identifiable correlative duty-bearers as essential features of rights. But the cases were drawn from the domain of property and contract, in which rights are indeed actively exercised, may be voluntarily surrendered, and are held against specific persons. The right not to be tortured reveals that a right may be a passive protection rather than an active entitlement, may be non-waivable, and may impose duties upon all persons rather than upon identifiable individuals. The definition mistook the features of proprietary and contractual rights for the features of rights as such.
passage:
Consider three rights. A landowner's right to exclude trespassers from his property — a right he exercises by erecting a fence and posting a notice, and which he could waive by inviting the public to cross his land. A creditor's right to repayment of a debt — a right he exercises by presenting his claim and could waive by forgiving the debt. An author's right to the profits from his published work — a right he exercises by collecting royalties and could waive by releasing the work into the public domain. From these cases one might extract a definition: a person has a right to something if and only if he can exercise the right by performing a specific action, he can waive the right voluntarily, and there exists a correlative duty upon some identifiable person or persons to respect or fulfil it.

But one must consider the right of every person not to be tortured. This right is affirmed by virtually every moral tradition and is regarded as among the most fundamental claims that any human being can assert. Yet it does not fit the definition. It cannot be exercised by performing any specific action; it is not an entitlement that the rightholder actively claims but a protection that surrounds him whether he invokes it or not. It cannot be waived voluntarily; the suggestion that a person might consent to being tortured, and that this consent would extinguish the right, is met with deep moral resistance, and many would hold that the right persists even against the wishes of the rightholder. And the correlative duty falls not upon some identifiable person or group of persons but upon everyone — upon every individual, every government, every institution, without exception. The duty not to torture is universal, and the right exists against the world. The definition, drawn from proprietary and contractual rights that are actively exercised, voluntarily waivable, and held against identifiable persons, mistook these features for the essential characteristics of rights as such, and thereby failed to accommodate the class of fundamental, non-waivable rights that impose universal duties and exist as passive protections rather than active entitlements.

background_cases: A person who habitually gives money to the poor, moved by genuine compassion and without any expectation of reward, is regarded as possessing the virtue of generosity; his actions flow from a stable disposition of character directed towards the good of others.

A soldier who stands his ground in battle, fully aware of the danger and choosing to face it rather than flee, is regarded as possessing the virtue of courage; his steadfastness flows from a settled disposition exercised in the service of a worthy cause.

A judge who decides cases impartially, resisting the temptation to favour his friends or punish his enemies, is regarded as possessing the virtue of justice; his impartiality flows from a stable disposition directed towards the fair treatment of all.
definition: A character trait T is a virtue if and only if (i) T is a stable disposition of the agent's character, (ii) T is directed towards the good — that is, the exercise of T aims at what is genuinely beneficial or noble, and (iii) the exercise of T is pleasant to the agent — the virtuous person takes delight in acting well.
conditions: T is a stable disposition of character
T is directed towards the good
the exercise of T is pleasant to the agent
counterexample: Consider a person who has struggled all his life with a violent temper. Through years of effort, he has cultivated the habit of restraining his anger, but the restraint is never pleasant. Every time he holds his tongue or unclenches his fist, he feels the powerful pull of his contrary inclination, and the act of self-mastery is painful to him. He does not delight in his own moderation; he endures it. His trait of self-restraint is a stable disposition directed towards the good, but its exercise is not pleasant to him. The third condition fails, yet his self-restraint seems to be a virtue of the highest order — perhaps more admirable, not less, for the suffering it costs him.
abductive_insight: The background cases were all instances of virtue exercised by persons of naturally harmonious character — the compassionate man who delights in giving, the brave soldier who finds satisfaction in standing firm, the impartial judge who takes pleasure in fairness. The definition abstracted the pleasantness of virtuous action as essential to virtue. But the cases were drawn from persons whose natural temperament was already aligned with virtue, and the definition mistook the ease and pleasure that accompany virtue in fortunate natures for a necessary feature of virtue itself. The case of the irascible man reveals that virtue may be exercised against the grain of one's natural temperament, and that the painful self-mastery required in such cases may be more virtuous, not less, than the effortless goodness of the naturally well-disposed.
passage:
Consider three cases that seem to exhibit the essential features of virtue. A person who gives generously to the poor does so from a stable disposition of compassion, directed towards the genuine good of those he helps, and he takes evident delight in his own generosity; the giving brings him pleasure. A soldier who stands his ground in battle does so from a settled disposition of courage, directed towards the defence of something worthy, and he finds satisfaction in his own steadfastness; the facing of danger, though perilous, is not disagreeable to him. A judge who decides cases impartially does so from a stable disposition of fairness, directed towards the just treatment of all who come before him, and he takes pleasure in the exercise of his judicial virtue; the rendering of just decisions is gratifying to him. From these cases one might extract a definition: a character trait is a virtue if and only if it is a stable disposition directed towards the good, and its exercise is pleasant to the agent.

But one must consider a person of a very different temperament. He is irascible by nature — quick to anger, prone to violent outbursts, and inclined to retaliate with disproportionate force against the slightest provocation. Through years of painful effort, he has cultivated the habit of restraining his anger. He holds his tongue when insulted; he unclenches his fists when provoked; he walks away from confrontations that his nature urges him to escalate. His self-restraint is a stable disposition; it is no passing resolution but a settled feature of his character, maintained through long practice. It is directed towards the good; the restraint of unjust anger is a genuine moral achievement. Yet its exercise is not pleasant to him. Every act of self-restraint is a small battle against his own inclinations. He feels the anger surging within him and must wrestle it down by sheer force of will. He does not delight in his own moderation; he endures it, as a man endures a chronic pain. The third condition of the definition fails. Yet his self-restraint appears to be a virtue — and, upon reflection, perhaps a more admirable virtue than the easy good temper of a person who is naturally mild and who restrains himself without effort because there is nothing to restrain. The background cases were drawn from persons of fortunate temperament, in whom virtue and pleasure coincided because nature had already done much of the work. The definition mistook this coincidence for a necessary connexion, overlooking the profound moral significance of virtue achieved in the teeth of contrary inclination.

background_cases: Two labourers perform the same work for the same employer, each contributing the same effort and skill, and each receives the same wage; the distribution is regarded as just because equal contributions are equally rewarded.

Two students sit the same examination, one performs better than the other, and the better performer receives a higher mark; the distribution of marks is regarded as just because it reflects a difference in achievement.

Two claimants dispute the ownership of a parcel of land, one produces a valid deed of purchase and the other does not, and the court awards the land to the holder of the deed; the decision is regarded as just because it respects the established entitlement.
definition: A distribution of benefits and burdens is just if and only if (i) the distribution is proportional to the contributions, achievements, or entitlements of the parties, (ii) the basis for distribution is applied consistently — like cases are treated alike, and (iii) the distribution is the result of a procedure that all parties have had an opportunity to contest.
conditions: the distribution is proportional to contributions, achievements, or entitlements
the basis is applied consistently — like cases treated alike
the distribution results from a procedure open to contestation
counterexample: Consider the distribution of food within a family. A mother divides the evening meal among her children, giving more to the child who is hungrier and less to the child who has already eaten a large lunch. The distribution is not proportional to contribution or achievement — the children have contributed nothing in proportion to what they receive. It is not applied on a basis of treating like cases alike, for the children are treated differently precisely because their needs differ. And the distribution is not the result of a procedure open to contestation; the mother simply decides, and the children accept. All three conditions fail. Yet the distribution is paradigmatically just — it is a distribution according to need, and its justice is immediately apparent.
abductive_insight: The background cases — equal wages for equal work, higher marks for better performance, property awarded by deed — were all drawn from the domain of public, institutional distribution, where the relevant criteria are contribution, achievement, and formal entitlement. The definition abstracted these criteria as exhaustive of the grounds of just distribution. But the cases were drawn from a narrow class of distributional contexts — labour markets, examinations, courts of law — and the definition mistook the features of institutional justice for the features of justice as such. The case of the family meal reveals that justice may operate according to need rather than contribution, may treat different cases differently precisely because their differences are morally relevant, and may dispense with formal procedures of contestation. The definition confused the public and institutional with the universal.
passage:
Consider three cases of apparently just distribution. Two labourers perform the same work for the same employer, each contributing the same effort and skill, and each receives the same wage; the justice of the arrangement lies in the proportionality of reward to contribution. Two students sit the same examination, one performs better than the other, and the better performer receives a higher mark; the justice of the arrangement lies in the correspondence between achievement and recognition. Two claimants dispute the ownership of a parcel of land, and the court awards it to the claimant who produces a valid deed of purchase; the justice of the decision lies in the respect accorded to established entitlement. From these cases one might extract a definition: a distribution of benefits and burdens is just if and only if it is proportional to contributions, achievements, or entitlements, the basis for distribution is applied consistently, and the distribution results from a procedure that all parties have had an opportunity to contest.

But one must consider the distribution of food within a family. A mother divides the evening meal among her three children. One child is growing rapidly and is ravenous; another has been ill and has little appetite; a third ate a large lunch and is not hungry. The mother gives more to the hungry child, less to the others, adjusting the portions to the needs of each. This distribution is not proportional to contribution, for the children have contributed nothing to the preparation of the meal. It is not proportional to achievement, for no achievement is in question. It is not proportional to entitlement in any formal sense, for the children hold no deeds or claims. The basis of distribution — need — is applied inconsistently in the sense that like cases are not treated alike; the children receive different portions precisely because their needs differ, and the differences are what make the distribution just. And the distribution does not result from any procedure open to contestation; the mother simply decides, guided by her knowledge of her children's needs, and the children accept her decision without formal recourse. All three conditions of the definition fail. Yet the distribution is paradigmatically just. Its justice lies in the fact that it responds to the genuine needs of the children — that it gives more where more is needed and less where less is needed. The background cases, drawn from the institutional contexts of wages, examinations, and legal proceedings, led the definition to identify justice with proportionality to contribution and formal procedural propriety, overlooking the vast domain of distributional justice that is governed by the principle of need.

background_cases: A man deliberates about whether to tell a lie or speak the truth, chooses to lie, and is held responsible for the deception; his responsibility rests upon his having freely chosen the dishonest course when an honest alternative was available.

A woman considers whether to keep a found purse or return it to its owner, chooses to keep it, and is held responsible for the appropriation; her responsibility rests upon her having freely chosen to keep it when she could have returned it.

A student decides to cheat on an examination rather than study, is caught, and is held responsible for the academic dishonesty; his responsibility rests upon his having freely chosen to cheat when the option of honest preparation was open to him.
definition: An agent A is morally responsible for an action X if and only if (i) A chose X from among alternative courses of action — that is, A could have done otherwise, (ii) A was aware of the nature and likely consequences of X at the time of choosing, and (iii) A's choice was not the product of external compulsion — no other agent forced A to perform X.
conditions: A chose X from among alternatives — A could have done otherwise
A was aware of the nature and likely consequences of X
A's choice was not the product of external compulsion
counterexample: Consider a person who is addicted to a powerful narcotic. He knows that taking the drug is destroying his health and his relationships. He is aware of the nature and consequences of his action. No other person compels him to take the drug; no one holds a weapon to his head or threatens him with punishment if he abstains. In a sense, he 'chooses' to take the drug — he lifts it to his lips and swallows. Yet his addiction has so thoroughly corrupted his will that the alternative of abstention, though logically available, is not genuinely open to him. The craving overwhelms his capacity for self-governance in a manner that external compulsion would not. All three conditions of the definition are ostensibly satisfied, yet one hesitates to hold him fully morally responsible.
abductive_insight: The background cases — the deliberate liar, the purse-keeper, the cheating student — were all instances in which the agent's will was functioning normally and the alternatives were genuinely accessible. The definition abstracted the features of these cases — the availability of alternatives, awareness, and the absence of external compulsion — as sufficient conditions for moral responsibility. But the cases were drawn from the domain of agents whose capacity for self-governance was intact, and the definition failed to consider the possibility that internal compulsion — addiction, obsession, pathological impulse — might undermine responsibility as effectively as external force. The case of the addict reveals that the mere absence of external compulsion does not guarantee that the agent's will is free in the sense that moral responsibility requires. The definition confused the absence of external coercion with genuine freedom of will.
passage:
Consider three cases in which an agent is plainly morally responsible for his action. A man deliberates about whether to tell a lie or to speak the truth. Both options are open to him; he is subject to no external pressure. He chooses to lie, fully aware that he is deceiving another person. His responsibility seems beyond question. A woman finds a purse in the street. She knows whose it is and could easily return it. She chooses to keep it, aware that she is appropriating another's property. Her responsibility seems equally clear. A student considers whether to cheat on an examination. He could study honestly; the option is available. He chooses to cheat, knowing the consequences if he is caught. His responsibility is not in doubt. From these cases one might extract a definition: an agent is morally responsible for an action if and only if he chose it from among alternatives, was aware of its nature and likely consequences, and was not externally compelled.

But one must consider a person who is addicted to a powerful narcotic substance. He knows that the drug is destroying his health — his physician has told him so in the plainest terms. He is aware that it is ruining his relationships; his husband has left him and his children no longer speak to him. He understands the nature and consequences of his action with perfect clarity. No one compels him to take the drug. No one holds a weapon to his head, no one threatens him with punishment if he abstains, no one so much as encourages him to continue. In a formal sense, he chooses to take the drug — he lifts it to his lips, he swallows. The alternative of abstention is logically available; he could, in principle, refrain. Yet his addiction has so thoroughly corrupted his capacity for self-governance that the alternative, though logically present, is not genuinely accessible to him. The craving is not an external force but an internal one, and it overwhelms his will with a power that no external compulsion could match. All three conditions of the definition are ostensibly satisfied — he chose, he was aware, he was not externally compelled — yet one hesitates to assign him the full measure of moral responsibility that attaches to the deliberate liar or the calculating cheat. The background cases were drawn from agents whose wills were functioning normally, for whom the alternatives were not merely logically available but genuinely accessible. The definition abstracted the absence of external compulsion as sufficient for freedom, overlooking the possibility that internal compulsion — addiction, obsession, pathological craving — may compromise the agent's freedom as effectively as any external force, rendering the formal availability of alternatives an empty formality.

background_cases: A buyer and a seller meet in a marketplace; the buyer inspects the goods, the seller states a price, the buyer agrees, and the exchange takes place; valid consent is given because both parties act freely and with full knowledge of the terms.

A patient is informed by her physician of the risks and benefits of a surgical procedure, considers the information, and agrees to undergo the operation; valid consent is given because the patient acts freely and with full understanding.

Two nations negotiate a treaty; each side is represented by authorised agents, the terms are openly discussed, and both parties sign the agreement; valid consent is given because each nation acts through its chosen representatives with full knowledge of the provisions.
definition: A person P has given valid consent to an arrangement X if and only if (i) P is a competent adult — that is, P has reached the age of majority and possesses the rational capacities necessary for deliberation, (ii) P has been provided with all material information regarding X, and (iii) P has agreed to X freely — that is, without coercion or duress.
conditions: P is a competent adult with rational capacities for deliberation
P has been provided with all material information regarding X
P has agreed to X freely, without coercion or duress
counterexample: Consider a worker in a remote company town where there is only one employer, one landlord, and one general store, all owned by the same individual. The worker is offered employment at a very low wage under harsh conditions. He is a competent adult; he has been provided with all material information about the terms of employment; and no one coerces him or threatens him with violence if he refuses. He agrees to the terms. All three conditions are satisfied. Yet one is reluctant to say that valid consent has been given, for the worker has no genuine alternative. If he refuses the employment, he has no other means of livelihood, no other place to live, and no realistic prospect of leaving the town. His agreement, though formally free and fully informed, is extracted from a position of such structural dependency that it lacks the quality of genuine voluntariness.
abductive_insight: The background cases — the marketplace exchange, the informed patient, the treaty negotiation — were all instances in which the consenting parties had genuine alternatives available. The buyer could have refused the goods and purchased elsewhere; the patient could have sought a second opinion or declined the procedure; the nations could have walked away from the table. The definition abstracted freedom from coercion and the provision of information as sufficient for valid consent, but it overlooked the structural conditions that make genuine freedom possible. The case of the company-town worker reveals that consent may be formally free and fully informed yet substantively unfree, because the consenting party lacks any genuine alternative. The definition confused the formal absence of coercion with the substantive presence of real choice.
passage:
Consider three cases of apparently valid consent. In a marketplace, a buyer inspects goods, is told the price, and agrees to the purchase. He is a competent adult, fully informed of the terms, and free from coercion. His consent is valid, and the exchange is legitimate. A patient is told by her physician of the risks and benefits of a surgical procedure. She considers the information carefully, asks questions, and agrees to undergo the operation. She is a competent adult, fully informed, and subject to no duress. Her consent is valid. Two nations negotiate a treaty through authorised representatives. The terms are openly discussed, each side is free to walk away, and both parties sign. Consent is validly given on both sides. From these cases one might extract a definition: valid consent requires a competent adult, the provision of all material information, and freedom from coercion or duress.

But one must consider the case of a worker in a remote company town. The town is owned, in its entirety, by a single individual who controls the only factory, the only housing, and the only general store. The worker is offered employment at a low wage under harsh conditions. He is a competent adult; his rational capacities are unimpaired. He has been provided with all material information about the terms of his employment — the wage, the hours, the dangers of the work. No one coerces him. No one threatens him with violence or punishment if he refuses. He considers the offer and agrees. All three conditions of the definition are satisfied. Yet his consent seems hollow. If he refuses the employment, he has nowhere else to work, nowhere else to live, and no realistic prospect of leaving the town, for he has no savings and the nearest alternative settlement is a hundred miles distant. His agreement, though formally free and fully informed, is extracted from a position of utter dependency. He consents because the only alternative to consent is destitution. The background cases — the marketplace, the consulting room, the treaty table — were all situations in which the consenting parties possessed genuine alternatives. The buyer could purchase elsewhere; the patient could seek another physician; the nations could walk away. The definition abstracted freedom from coercion as sufficient for valid consent, but the cases from which it was drawn were cases in which genuine alternatives were available, and the definition failed to recognise that freedom from coercion without the availability of genuine alternatives is a merely formal freedom, insufficient to ground the moral force that valid consent is meant to carry.

background_cases: A craftsman who spends years mastering his trade and produces work of exceptional quality deserves the esteem of his community; his desert is grounded in his voluntary effort and the excellence of his achievement.

A scholar who devotes decades to the study of a difficult subject and makes an important discovery deserves recognition and reward; her desert is grounded in her sustained intellectual labour and its fruitful outcome.
An athlete who trains rigorously and wins a competition deserves the prize; his desert is grounded in his disciplined effort and his demonstrated superiority in the contest.
definition: A person P deserves reward R if and only if (i) P has exerted voluntary effort in pursuit of a goal, (ii) P's effort has resulted in an achievement that is recognised as valuable by competent judges, and (iii) R is proportional to the magnitude of P's achievement.
conditions: P has exerted voluntary effort in pursuit of a goal
P's effort has resulted in a valuable achievement recognised by competent judges
R is proportional to the magnitude of P's achievement
counterexample: Consider a person who has suffered a grave injustice — a person who has been wrongfully imprisoned for twenty years and is at last exonerated and released. He has exerted no voluntary effort towards the goal of compensation; he has simply endured a wrong. His case involves no achievement that is recognised as valuable by competent judges; it involves, rather, the recognition of a wrong that was done to him. Yet he deserves compensation — substantial compensation, one might say — for the years of his life that were taken from him. His desert is grounded not in effort or achievement but in the wrong he has suffered and the debt that justice owes him.
abductive_insight: The background cases — the craftsman, the scholar, the athlete — were all instances of desert grounded in voluntary effort and recognised achievement. The definition abstracted these features as essential to desert. But the cases were drawn from the narrow domain of meritorious accomplishment, and the definition mistook the features of that domain for the features of desert as such. The case of the wrongfully imprisoned man reveals that desert may be grounded in suffering and injustice rather than in effort and achievement. What grounds his claim to compensation is not anything he has done but something that has been done to him. The definition confused one species of desert — merit-based desert — with the genus, overlooking the possibility that desert may arise from wrongful deprivation as well as from praiseworthy accomplishment.
passage:
Consider three cases that appear to reveal the essential features of desert. A craftsman who spends years mastering his trade and produces work of exceptional quality deserves the esteem of his peers; his desert is rooted in his voluntary effort and the excellence of his product. A scholar who devotes decades to the study of a recondite subject and arrives at an important discovery deserves recognition; her desert is rooted in her sustained intellectual labour and its fruitful outcome. An athlete who trains with great discipline and wins a competition deserves the prize; his desert is rooted in his effort and his demonstrated superiority. From these cases one might extract a definition: a person deserves a reward if and only if he has exerted voluntary effort in pursuit of a goal, his effort has resulted in a valuable achievement recognised by competent judges, and the reward is proportional to the magnitude of his achievement.

But one must consider a very different case. A man is wrongfully convicted of a crime he did not commit and is imprisoned for twenty years. During those years he suffers the deprivation of his liberty, the destruction of his career, the dissolution of his family, and the daily indignities of incarceration. He is at last exonerated when new evidence comes to light, and he is released. He has exerted no voluntary effort towards the goal of compensation; he has simply endured, year after year, a wrong that was not of his making. His case involves no achievement that might be recognised as valuable by competent judges; it involves, rather, the belated recognition of an injustice. Yet he deserves compensation. He deserves it not as a reward for effort or a recognition of achievement but as a reparation for wrong — as the partial discharge of a debt that justice owes to those it has injured. His desert is grounded in the gravity of the injustice he has suffered and the magnitude of the loss he has sustained. The background cases were drawn from the domain of meritorious accomplishment — craftsmanship, scholarship, athletic competition — and the definition abstracted effort and achievement as the essential grounds of desert. But these cases represented only one species of desert, and the definition mistook the features of that species for the features of the genus. The wrongfully imprisoned man reveals that desert may arise from wrongful deprivation as well as from praiseworthy accomplishment, and that the moral demand for compensation is as genuine a form of desert as the demand for reward.

background_cases: A soldier who overcomes his fear of death to rescue a wounded comrade under fire is called courageous; he acts despite danger, from a settled character, and for a good end.

A firefighter who enters a collapsing building to save a child, knowing the risks and accepting them out of a sense of duty, is called courageous; she acts in the face of peril, from a reliable disposition, and for a worthy purpose.

A political dissident who speaks publicly against a tyrannical regime, knowing that imprisonment or worse may follow, is called courageous; he faces danger steadily, from a stable trait of character, and in the service of a cause he judges good.

A physician who travels to a region afflicted by a deadly epidemic, knowing the risk of infection, is called courageous; she acts in the face of mortal danger, from a settled commitment, and for the end of relieving suffering.
definition: A person P possesses the virtue of courage if and only if (i) P acts in the face of serious danger or difficulty, (ii) P does so from a settled disposition of character rather than on impulse or by accident, and (iii) P acts for the sake of an end that P judges to be good and worth the risk.
conditions: P acts in the face of serious danger or difficulty
P does so from a settled disposition of character
P acts for the sake of an end judged good and worth the risk
counterexample: Consider a mountaineer who, having been diagnosed with a condition that renders him physiologically incapable of experiencing fear — a rare neurological deficit — undertakes the most dangerous ascents with perfect equanimity. He climbs because he judges the achievement to be good, and he does so from a settled disposition: his behaviour is consistent and reliable. He satisfies all three conditions. Yet one might hesitate to call this courage. The mountaineer does not overcome anything; there is no inner resistance to be mastered, no fear to be faced and conquered. His steadiness in the face of danger is not a moral achievement but a neurological peculiarity. The background cases all involved agents who felt the pull of fear or self-preservation and overcame it through strength of character. The definition has abstracted the outward pattern — steady action in the face of danger, from a settled disposition, for a good end — without capturing the inner dimension: the presence of fear or apprehension that the courageous agent must master.
abductive_insight: The background cases — the soldier, the firefighter, the dissident, the physician — were all instances in which the agent experienced fear or apprehension and overcame it. The definition abstracted the visible features of these cases — action in the face of danger, stable character, good ends — but dropped the feature that gave the virtue its distinctive moral character: the mastery of fear. The mountaineer who cannot feel fear satisfies the outward conditions but lacks the inner struggle that, in the background cases, was the very essence of the courage on display. The definition confused the observable marks of courage with the moral achievement that underlies them.
passage:
Consider four cases in which courage is attributed. A soldier overcomes his fear of death to rescue a wounded comrade under heavy fire. A firefighter enters a collapsing building to save a child, knowing that the structure may give way at any moment. A political dissident speaks publicly against a tyrannical regime, aware that imprisonment or death may follow. A physician travels to a region afflicted by a deadly epidemic, accepting the risk of infection for the sake of relieving suffering. In each case the agent acts in the face of serious danger, does so from a settled disposition of character, and acts for the sake of an end judged good and worth the risk. From these cases one might extract a definition: courage consists in acting in the face of danger, from a settled character, for a good end.

But one must consider the case of a mountaineer who has a rare neurological condition that renders him physiologically incapable of experiencing fear. He undertakes the most perilous ascents — sheer rock faces, exposed ridges in severe weather, ice-falls where a single misstep means death — with perfect composure. He judges the achievement of reaching the summit to be a good worth the risk, and he pursues it with unwavering consistency, ascent after ascent, year after year. His behaviour proceeds from a settled disposition; it is neither impulsive nor accidental. He satisfies all three conditions of the definition. Yet we should hesitate to attribute courage to him. He does not overcome fear, for he does not experience it. His calmness in the face of danger is not a victory of character over natural inclination but a consequence of a neurological deficit. The soldier, the firefighter, the dissident, the physician — each of them felt fear, felt the powerful pull of self-preservation, and mastered it through strength of will and commitment to the good. The definition abstracted the outward marks of their behaviour — the action in the face of danger, the settled character, the good end — but omitted the inner experience that gave their behaviour its distinctive moral quality. The mountaineer reveals that the definition has captured the surface of courage whilst missing its depth: the presence of fear or apprehension that must be acknowledged and overcome, the inner struggle that distinguishes genuine courage from mere fearlessness.

background_cases: In a small community, each family cultivates its own plot of land and retains the harvest; the distribution of food reflects each family's labour and skill, and no one complains of injustice.
In a guild of craftsmen, each member is paid according to the quality and quantity of his work; the more skilled and industrious craftsman earns more, and the arrangement is accepted as fair.
In a school, prizes are awarded to the students who perform best in the examinations; the distribution of honours reflects individual merit, and the arrangement is regarded as just.
definition: A distribution of goods or honours is just if and only if (i) the distribution reflects individual merit — effort, skill, or achievement, (ii) every person has had an equal opportunity to earn the goods or honours by exercising effort and skill, and (iii) no external interference has distorted the relationship between merit and reward.
conditions: the distribution reflects individual merit
every person has had equal opportunity
no external interference has distorted the merit-reward relationship
counterexample: Consider a society in which two children are born on the same day. One is born to a wealthy family that provides excellent nutrition, private tutoring, access to books, and encouragement at every turn. The other is born to a family living in poverty, with no access to adequate food, no books in the home, and no opportunity for education beyond the most rudimentary. Both children are equally talented at birth. By the time they reach adulthood, the first child has acquired skills and qualifications that the second child — through no fault of her own — has not. A distribution of positions and rewards based on merit, skill, and achievement will favour the first child over the second. The distribution appears to reflect individual merit, and no external interference — no bribery, no corruption, no rigging of the competition — has distorted the outcome. But the two children did not have equal opportunity; their starting positions were vastly unequal, and the 'merit' of the first child is in large part the product of advantages that the second child was denied. Condition (ii) fails, though in a manner that the definition's framers did not anticipate: equal formal opportunity is not equal real opportunity when the conditions for the development of merit are themselves unequally distributed.
abductive_insight: The background cases — the farming community, the guild, the school — were all situations in which the participants started from roughly equal positions and in which effort and skill were the primary determinants of outcome. The definition abstracted merit and equal opportunity as essential to justice. But the background cases concealed a crucial assumption: that the conditions for the development of merit — nutrition, education, encouragement — were themselves equally available. When this assumption is removed, the definition's notion of 'equal opportunity' is revealed as formal rather than substantive, and a distribution that appears to reflect merit may in fact reflect the unequal distribution of the conditions necessary for the development of merit.
passage:
Consider three cases in which a distribution of goods or honours is regarded as just. In a small farming community, each family cultivates its own plot and retains the harvest. Those who work harder and more skilfully reap more, and the distribution reflects effort and ability; the arrangement is accepted by all as fair. In a guild of craftsmen, each member is paid according to the quality and quantity of his work. The more skilful and industrious artisan earns more than the less skilful, and the arrangement is regarded as just by the members of the guild. In a school, prizes are awarded to the students who perform best in the examinations. The distribution of honours reflects individual achievement, and the arrangement is accepted as fair by students and teachers alike. From these cases one might extract a definition: a distribution is just if and only if it reflects individual merit, every person has had equal opportunity, and no external interference has distorted the relationship between merit and reward.

But one must consider a society in which two children are born on the same day. The first is born to a wealthy family. She is well nourished from infancy, provided with private tutors, surrounded by books, and encouraged at every turn to develop her talents. The second is born to a family living in poverty. She is inadequately fed, has no access to books, and receives only the most rudimentary education. Both children are equally gifted at birth — let us stipulate that their native abilities are identical. By the time they reach adulthood, however, the first child has acquired skills, qualifications, and accomplishments that the second has not. A distribution of positions and rewards based upon merit will favour the first over the second. No bribery has occurred, no corruption, no rigging of the competition. The distribution appears to reflect individual merit, and no external interference has distorted the outcome. But the two children did not have equal opportunity in any meaningful sense. The first child's 'merit' is, in substantial part, the product of advantages — nutrition, education, encouragement — that were denied to the second. The formal opportunity was equal: both could, in principle, sit the same examinations. But the real opportunity — the opportunity to develop the capacities that the examinations test — was grossly unequal. The background cases all involved participants who started from roughly comparable positions, and the definition tacitly assumed that the conditions for the development of merit were themselves equally available. The two children reveal that this assumption was doing essential but unacknowledged work, and that a definition of justice that rests upon merit and equal opportunity must reckon with the question of how merit is itself produced.

background_cases: A student who studies diligently for months, sacrificing leisure and sleep, and earns the highest marks in a difficult examination deserves the academic prize; her achievement is the product of sustained effort.
A farmer who toils from dawn to dusk, clearing land, planting crops, and tending them through drought and storm, deserves the harvest he reaps; his yield is the fruit of his labour.
An athlete who trains for years with single-minded dedication, enduring pain and fatigue, and wins a race deserves the medal; her victory is the reward of extraordinary effort.
definition: A person P deserves a reward R for an achievement A if and only if (i) A is the product of P's sustained and voluntary effort, (ii) P sacrificed other goods — time, comfort, pleasure — in order to achieve A, and (iii) A is a genuine accomplishment, not the result of mere luck or external advantage.
conditions: A is the product of P's sustained and voluntary effort
P sacrificed other goods to achieve A
A is a genuine accomplishment, not the result of luck or external advantage
counterexample: Consider two pianists who are competing for a prize. The first has practised for thousands of hours, has sacrificed social life, leisure, and sleep, and plays with technical accuracy and emotional commitment. The second, a prodigy of extraordinary natural talent, has practised far less but plays with an effortless brilliance that surpasses the first pianist in every respect. The second pianist's performance is, in part, the product of a gift — an innate musical capacity — that she did nothing to earn. Her achievement is the result, to a significant degree, of natural endowment rather than effort or sacrifice. Condition (i) is only partially satisfied, and condition (iii) is compromised by the role of natural talent. Yet it appears that the second pianist deserves the prize if her performance is superior. Desert, it seems, may be grounded not only in effort but also in the exercise of a capacity that, though unearned, is genuinely the agent's own.
abductive_insight: The background cases — the student, the farmer, the athlete — were all instances in which effort and sacrifice were the salient features of the achievement, and in which the role of natural talent or external advantage was either minimal or unmentioned. The definition abstracted effort and sacrifice as the essential grounds of desert. But the background cases concealed the contribution of natural capacity — the student's intelligence, the farmer's physical strength, the athlete's physiological endowment — and the definition mistook the most visible component of desert for the whole. The prodigy reveals that desert may be grounded in the exercise of a capacity as well as in the effort expended, and that a definition which demands effort and sacrifice as sufficient conditions will be unable to account for the desert of those whose achievements rest upon gifts they did not earn.
passage:
Consider three cases in which desert is attributed. A student studies diligently for months, sacrificing leisure and sleep, and earns the highest marks in a demanding examination. A farmer toils from dawn to dusk, clearing land, planting crops, and tending them through adverse conditions, and reaps a bountiful harvest. An athlete trains for years with unwavering dedication, enduring pain and exhaustion, and wins a championship race. In each case, the achievement is the product of sustained effort, the achiever has sacrificed other goods in pursuit of the goal, and the accomplishment is genuine — not the result of mere luck. From these cases one might extract a definition: a person deserves a reward for an achievement if and only if the achievement is the product of sustained effort, the person sacrificed other goods in order to achieve it, and the achievement is not the result of luck or external advantage.

But one must consider the case of two pianists competing for a prize. The first has practised for thousands of hours over many years. She has sacrificed her social life, her leisure, her sleep. She plays with precision, with technical command, and with genuine musical feeling. Her performance is, by any standard, excellent. The second pianist is a prodigy of extraordinary natural gift. She has practised far less — a fraction of the hours the first pianist has devoted — yet she plays with an effortless brilliance that leaves audiences astonished. Her phrasing is more subtle, her tone more luminous, her interpretive insight more penetrating. Her superiority is acknowledged by every competent judge. The second pianist's achievement is, in substantial part, the product of a natural endowment that she did nothing to earn. She was born with an extraordinary musical capacity — a gift of nature, not of effort. She has practised, to be sure, but the ratio of achievement to effort is vastly higher than in the case of the first pianist. Her accomplishment is not, in the sense the definition intends, the product of sustained effort and sacrifice; it is the exercise of a capacity that was given to her, not earned by her. The definition, extracted from cases in which effort was the salient feature, treated effort as the essential ground of desert. But the background cases concealed the role of natural capacity — the student's intelligence, the farmer's strength, the athlete's physiology — and the definition mistook the most conspicuous element for the whole. The prodigy reveals that desert may attach to the exercise of a genuine capacity, even when that capacity is a gift of nature rather than a product of effort.

background_cases: A thief who steals from a market stall is caught, tried, and sentenced to a period of imprisonment; the punishment is proportionate to the offence, and the community regards it as just.
A woman who assaults a stranger in the street is arrested, convicted, and fined; the fine is proportionate to the harm done, and the punishment is accepted as appropriate.

A fraudster who deceives elderly persons out of their savings is convicted and imprisoned for a substantial term; the severity of the sentence reflects the gravity of the offence and the vulnerability of the victims.
definition: A punishment is just if and only if (i) the offender has been found guilty of a specific wrong through a fair process of adjudication, (ii) the punishment is proportionate to the gravity of the offence, and (iii) the punishment is of the same kind as the harm done — deprivation of liberty for offences against liberty, deprivation of property for offences against property, and so forth.
conditions: the offender has been found guilty through a fair process
the punishment is proportionate to the gravity of the offence
the punishment is of the same kind as the harm done
counterexample: Consider a woman convicted of perjury — that is, of lying under oath in a court of law. She has been found guilty through a fair trial. The punishment imposed — a term of imprisonment — is proportionate to the gravity of the offence. But perjury is an offence against truth, against the integrity of judicial proceedings, and it is not clear that any punishment can be 'of the same kind' as the harm done. The harm is not a deprivation of liberty or of property but a corruption of the process by which justice is administered. Imprisonment is not a deprivation of truth; a fine is not a restoration of judicial integrity. Condition (iii) cannot be satisfied, for there is no punishment that mirrors the nature of the harm. Yet the punishment is just — the offender has been fairly tried, and the sentence is proportionate. The definition's requirement of kind-matching excludes just punishments for offences whose harm is not of a kind that can be matched by any available sanction.
abductive_insight: The background cases — the thief, the assailant, the fraudster — involved offences whose harm was of a tangible, readily categorisable kind: loss of property, physical injury, loss of savings. In each case, the punishment could be construed as 'matching' the harm in kind: deprivation of liberty for the violent offender, deprivation of property for the property offender. The definition abstracted this pattern of matching as essential to just punishment. But the background cases were drawn from the domain of offences with concrete, material harms, and the definition mistook the matchability of harm and punishment in those cases for a necessary condition of just punishment as such. The perjurer reveals that many offences — offences against truth, against public order, against the integrity of institutions — inflict harms that cannot be matched in kind by any available punishment, and that the justice of a punishment may rest upon proportionality and fair process without any requirement of kind-matching.
passage:
Consider three cases in which punishment is regarded as just. A thief who steals from a market stall is caught, tried through proper judicial process, and sentenced to imprisonment; the sentence is proportionate to the offence, and the punishment — deprivation of liberty — seems, in a loose sense, to match the kind of harm done, inasmuch as the thief deprived another of property and is herself deprived of something valuable. A woman who assaults a stranger is convicted and fined; the fine is proportionate, and the deprivation of property imposed upon the offender mirrors, in a fashion, the suffering inflicted upon the victim. A fraudster who cheats elderly persons of their savings is imprisoned; the severity of the sentence reflects the gravity of the offence and the vulnerability of those wronged. From these cases one might extract a definition: a punishment is just if the offender has been fairly convicted, the punishment is proportionate, and the punishment is of the same kind as the harm done.

But one must consider the case of a woman convicted of perjury. She has lied under oath in a court of law, giving false testimony that has corrupted the proceedings and led to the wrongful conviction of an innocent party. She is tried fairly and convicted. A proportionate sentence of imprisonment is imposed. But the harm done by perjury is not a deprivation of liberty or of property. It is a corruption of truth, an assault upon the integrity of judicial proceedings, a betrayal of the solemn obligation that the oath imposes. What punishment could be 'of the same kind' as this harm? Imprisonment deprives the offender of liberty, but it does not restore truth or repair the integrity of the judicial process. A fine deprives the offender of money, but the harm of perjury is not financial. There is no punishment that mirrors, in kind, the nature of the wrong. Condition (iii) cannot be satisfied. Yet the punishment is just — the trial was fair, the conviction was sound, and the sentence is proportionate to the gravity of the offence. The background cases involved offences with tangible, material harms that could be loosely matched by available sanctions, and the definition abstracted this matchability as a necessary condition. The perjurer reveals that many of the most serious offences — offences against truth, against institutional integrity, against public trust — inflict harms of a kind that no available punishment can mirror, and that the justice of a punishment may consist in proportionality and fair process alone, without any requirement that the punishment resemble the harm in kind.

background_cases: Aristotle's temperate man abstains from excessive pleasure not because he lacks appetite but because he has trained himself to desire only what is fitting; his temperance is a settled state issuing from deliberate habituation and reflective choice.

The courageous soldier who advances under fire does so not from ignorance of danger but from a reasoned judgement that the cause is worth the risk; his courage is the product of both experience and deliberation.
The just magistrate who renders an impartial verdict does so not from indifference to the parties but from a commitment to fairness that he has cultivated over years of practice and reflection.

The generous benefactor who gives freely does so not from a compulsive need to be liked but from a considered view that those in need deserve assistance; her generosity is the expression of a stable and reasoned disposition.
definition: A character trait T is a virtue if and only if (i) T is a stable disposition to act in ways appropriate to the circumstances, (ii) the actions flowing from T are generally beneficial to others or to the community, and (iii) the agent who possesses T acts from T voluntarily rather than under compulsion.
conditions: T is a stable disposition to act appropriately
The actions flowing from T are generally beneficial
The agent acts from T voluntarily
counterexample: Consider a person who has undergone a programme of behavioural conditioning designed to eliminate aggressive impulses. The conditioning has been entirely successful: he is now stably disposed to respond to provocation with mildness and forbearance. His mild behaviour is beneficial to those around him. And he acts voluntarily in the sense that no one forces him on any given occasion — the responses are his own. All three conditions are satisfied. Yet his mildness is not the product of deliberation or reflective endorsement; it is the result of a process that bypassed his rational agency entirely. He did not choose to become mild; mildness was installed in him, much as a habit might be installed in an animal by repeated reinforcement.
abductive_insight: The background cases all involved agents whose virtuous dispositions arose from a process of deliberate habituation guided by reflection — agents who understood why the trait was good and who cultivated it for reasons they could articulate. The definition captures the stability and beneficial character of virtue but drops the requirement that the disposition be the product of the agent's own rational engagement with the question of how to live.
passage:
If one attends to the cases that most clearly exemplify virtue, a common pattern is discernible. The temperate man has trained himself to desire appropriately; the courageous soldier has reflected upon the value of what he defends; the just magistrate has cultivated impartiality through years of deliberate practice; the generous benefactor gives from a considered commitment to the welfare of others. In each case, the virtuous agent possesses a stable disposition that issues in beneficial action, and that disposition is exercised voluntarily. One might extract from these cases the definition: a trait is a virtue if and only if it is a stable disposition to act appropriately, the actions it produces are beneficial, and the agent acts voluntarily.

Yet this definition, though it captures the surface features of the paradigm cases, misses something that a closer inspection reveals to be essential. Consider a person who has undergone a programme of behavioural conditioning that has eliminated his aggressive impulses and replaced them with a stable disposition to respond to provocation with mildness. His behaviour is beneficial; his responses are voluntary in the minimal sense that no one compels him on any particular occasion. All three conditions are satisfied. But his mildness was not cultivated through his own reflection or choice; it was produced by a process that circumvented his rational agency. He did not deliberate about the value of gentleness and choose to pursue it; gentleness was imposed upon him, much as a trick is taught to an animal. The background cases, by contrast, involved agents who understood the reasons for their dispositions and who had shaped their characters through a process of reflective self-formation. The definition has captured the product of virtue — stable, beneficial, voluntary action — but has lost sight of its genesis: the requirement that the disposition arise from the agent's own rational engagement with the question of what kind of person he ought to become.

background_cases: When a community distributes the harvest according to the labour each member has contributed, and every member regards the distribution as fair, the arrangement is called just because it rewards contribution and is endorsed by those it affects.
When a court awards damages to a person wrongfully injured by another, the award is regarded as just because it restores, so far as money can, the balance that the wrong disrupted.

When a society provides free education to all children regardless of their parents' wealth, the provision is regarded as just because it ensures that no child's prospects are determined solely by the accident of birth.
definition: A social arrangement S is just if and only if (i) S distributes benefits and burdens according to a consistent and publicly known rule, (ii) the rule treats relevantly similar persons in the same way, and (iii) the rule was adopted through a procedure in which all affected parties had an equal voice.
conditions: S distributes according to a consistent and publicly known rule
The rule treats relevantly similar persons alike
The rule was adopted through a procedure giving all an equal voice
counterexample: Consider a society that distributes medical care according to a rule — first come, first served — that is consistent, publicly known, and treats everyone alike. The rule was adopted through a fully democratic process in which all citizens participated equally. All three conditions are met. Yet in practice, the rule systematically disadvantages those who, owing to disability, lack of transportation, or the demands of caregiving, are unable to arrive early at the clinic. The rule is formally equal but substantively unequal, and the disadvantaged parties, though they had an equal voice in adopting it, lacked the practical capacity to foresee or articulate its disparate impact.
abductive_insight: The background cases involved arrangements whose rules not only applied equally in form but produced outcomes that were substantively fair in light of the different circumstances of those affected. The definition captures formal equality and procedural legitimacy but drops the requirement that the rule produce substantively equitable outcomes, taking account of the diverse situations of those to whom it applies.
passage:
When one surveys the arrangements that are most confidently called just, a pattern emerges. The community that distributes the harvest according to labour rewards contribution; the court that awards damages restores a balance disrupted by wrongdoing; the society that provides universal education ensures that children are not penalised for their parents' poverty. In each case, there is a consistent and publicly known rule, the rule treats relevantly similar persons alike, and the arrangement enjoys the endorsement of those it governs. One might distil from these cases the following definition: a social arrangement is just if and only if it distributes benefits and burdens according to a consistent, publicly known rule that treats similar persons alike and was adopted through a procedure giving all an equal voice.

Yet this definition, though it captures the formal and procedural dimensions of the paradigm cases, overlooks a feature that was present in each of them. Consider a society that distributes medical care on the principle of first come, first served. The rule is consistent and publicly known; it treats everyone alike — no one is given priority on the basis of wealth, status, or connexion; and it was adopted through a fully democratic procedure. All three conditions are satisfied. But in practice, those who are disabled, those who lack transportation, and those whose responsibilities as carers prevent them from arriving early are systematically excluded from timely care. The rule, though formally equal, operates in a context of substantive inequality that renders its formal equality illusory. The background cases were not merely formally equal; they were substantively responsive to the different circumstances of those affected. The distribution of the harvest according to labour took account of differences in contribution; the provision of universal education took account of differences in parental wealth. The definition has captured the form of justice — consistency, equality, procedural legitimacy — but has dropped its substance: the requirement that a just rule not merely apply equally but produce outcomes that are fair in light of the actual, diverse circumstances of those whom it governs.

background_cases: A strong swimmer who sees a child drowning in shallow water is ordinarily said to be obligated to attempt a rescue, because the cost is negligible and the harm of inaction is severe.
A physician who encounters a person in cardiac arrest in a public place is ordinarily said to be obligated to render aid, because her training equips her to help at minimal risk to herself.
A bystander who witnesses a robbery and can summon help at no personal risk by telephoning the police is ordinarily said to be obligated to do so.
A parent who can prevent her child from running into traffic by calling out is obligated to do so, because the effort is trivial and the consequences of silence are catastrophic.
definition: A person P is morally obligated to perform an action X if and only if (i) P is aware that another person Q faces a serious threat of harm, (ii) P is capable of performing X at a cost that is not disproportionate to the harm that Q faces, and (iii) no other person is better positioned to perform X.
conditions: P is aware that Q faces serious harm
P can perform X at a cost not disproportionate to Q's harm
No other person is better positioned to perform X
counterexample: Consider a wealthy person who is aware that thousands of people in a distant country face starvation. She is capable of donating a sum that, relative to her wealth, would not be disproportionate to the harm the starving people face. And no other person is obviously better positioned to help — many could help, but none stands out as especially well placed. All three conditions are satisfied, yet the obligation the definition generates is vast and indeterminate: it would require the wealthy person to give away most of her fortune to anyone whose need she becomes aware of, and it would do so repeatedly and indefinitely. The conditions yield an obligation that, if taken seriously, would leave the agent with nothing and still not exhaust the claims upon her.
abductive_insight: The background cases all involved specific, proximate situations in which a particular person was uniquely or especially well positioned to prevent a determinate harm to a specific individual. The definition abstracts away the proximity and specificity of the relationship, generating obligations to anyone of whose need the agent becomes aware, regardless of distance, number, or the indefiniteness of the demand.
passage:
The cases in which moral obligation is most clearly present share a common structure. The swimmer and the drowning child, the physician and the cardiac arrest, the bystander and the robbery, the parent and the child running into traffic — in each, a specific person is confronted with a specific threat of harm to a proximate individual, and the cost of intervention is negligible. From these cases, one might extract the definition: a person is obligated to act if she is aware that another faces serious harm, she can act at a cost not disproportionate to that harm, and no one else is better positioned to help.

Yet this definition, abstracted from the local and specific circumstances of the paradigm cases, generates obligations of a scope that may give us pause. Consider a wealthy person who reads in the newspaper that thousands of people in a distant country face starvation. She is aware of the harm; she can donate a sum that is small relative to her fortune and thus not disproportionate to the gravity of starvation; and no single other person is identifiably better positioned to help — the obligation falls on everyone and therefore on no one in particular. All three conditions are satisfied, and the definition yields the conclusion that she is obligated to give. But the obligation does not end with one donation. Tomorrow she will learn of another famine, another epidemic, another disaster, and the conditions will again be satisfied. Taken to its logical conclusion, the definition would require her to divest herself of nearly everything she owns. The background cases involved determinate, proximate encounters between specific individuals; the definition has generalised away the proximity and specificity, producing an account in which any awareness of any serious harm anywhere generates an obligation to act. What is missing is a condition attending to the specificity of the relationship between the agent and the person in need — a condition that limits obligation to situations of the kind exemplified by the background cases rather than extending it to every instance of human suffering of which the agent is aware.

background_cases: When a court sentences a burglar to imprisonment, the punishment is regarded as legitimate in part because the burglar chose to break into the house, knowing that burglary is prohibited and punishable.
When a school disciplines a student for cheating on an examination, the discipline is regarded as fair because the student knew the rules and chose to violate them.
When a professional body suspends a doctor for malpractice, the suspension is regarded as justified because the doctor was trained in the relevant standards and chose to depart from them.
definition: An imposition of suffering S upon a person P constitutes legitimate punishment if and only if (i) P has been found, through a fair procedure, to have committed a prohibited act, (ii) S has been imposed by an authority with recognised jurisdiction, and (iii) S is proportionate to the gravity of the act.
conditions: P has been found through a fair procedure to have committed a prohibited act
S is imposed by an authority with jurisdiction
S is proportionate to the gravity of the act
counterexample: Consider a person who, owing to a severe mental illness of which he was entirely unaware, commits an act of vandalism. He is tried through a scrupulously fair procedure and found to have committed the prohibited act. The punishment — a fine — is imposed by a court of competent jurisdiction and is proportionate to the offence. All three conditions are met. Yet the man was unable, at the time of the act, to understand what he was doing or to conform his conduct to the law. He did not choose to vandalise; his illness drove him to it. The background cases all involved agents who acted with awareness and choice — the burglar who knew burglary was wrong, the student who chose to cheat, the doctor who departed from standards he understood.
