The definition has captured the procedural and institutional dimensions of punishment but has dropped the condition of moral agency — the requirement that the person punished was capable of choosing otherwise.
abductive_insight: The background cases all involved agents who possessed the capacity to understand the prohibition and to choose whether to comply. The definition captures the fairness of the procedure, the authority of the tribunal, and the proportionality of the sanction, but it drops the requirement that the person punished was a responsible moral agent at the time of the act — that he was capable of understanding the rule and of choosing to obey it.
passage:
The cases that most clearly exemplify legitimate punishment share a common structure. The burglar chose to commit the burglary, knowing it was prohibited. The cheating student chose to violate a rule he understood. The negligent physician departed from standards in which she had been trained. In each case, the person punished had the capacity to understand the rule and to choose whether to comply, and a fair procedure established that the rule had been violated. One might extract from these cases the definition: punishment is legitimate if the person has been found through a fair procedure to have committed a prohibited act, the punishment is imposed by a competent authority, and it is proportionate to the act.

Yet this definition, though it captures the procedural and institutional elements of the paradigm cases, misses a feature that was quietly present in each of them. Consider a person who, owing to a severe mental illness, commits an act of vandalism. He is tried through a fair procedure and found to have committed the act. The fine imposed is proportionate and is levied by a court of competent jurisdiction. All three conditions are satisfied. But the man was, at the time of the act, incapable of understanding what he was doing. He did not choose to vandalise any more than a sleepwalker chooses to walk. The background cases all involved agents who acted with awareness and choice — agents who could have complied with the rule but chose not to. The definition has captured everything about the process and the sanction but has dropped the condition that made punishment apt in the first place: that the person punished was a moral agent, capable of understanding the prohibition and of conforming his conduct to it. Without this condition, the definition legitimises the punishment of persons who could not, in any meaningful sense, have acted otherwise.

background_cases: A soldier who repeatedly chooses to face danger in battle, not from recklessness but from a considered judgement that the cause is worth the risk, is said to possess the virtue of courage; his disposition is stable, exercised in response to reasons, and directed towards a worthy end.

A judge who consistently resists the temptation to favour her friends in legal proceedings, acting instead from a settled commitment to impartiality, is said to possess the virtue of justice; her disposition is stable, reason-responsive, and oriented towards fair treatment of all parties.

A physician who habitually tells patients the truth about their condition, even when the truth is unwelcome, is said to possess the virtue of honesty; his disposition is stable, exercised in response to the relevant moral considerations, and directed towards the good of transparency.
definition: A character trait T is a virtue if and only if (i) T is a stable disposition to act in a certain manner across relevantly similar situations, (ii) T is exercised in response to the agent's recognition of relevant reasons for so acting, and (iii) the exercise of T tends to promote the good of the agent or of those affected by the agent's conduct.
conditions: T is a stable disposition to act in a certain manner across relevantly similar situations
T is exercised in response to the agent's recognition of relevant reasons for so acting
The exercise of T tends to promote the good of the agent or of those affected by the agent's conduct
counterexample: Consider a person who has a stable disposition to give money to beggars whenever she encounters them. She does so in response to her recognition that they are in need — a relevant reason — and her giving tends to promote their good. All three conditions are satisfied. Yet suppose she gives indiscriminately, without regard to whether the recipient is genuinely destitute or merely exploiting her generosity, and without considering whether her resources might be better directed elsewhere. Her disposition, though stable, reason-responsive, and beneficial in individual instances, lacks the discriminating judgement that separates virtue from mere habitual benevolence.
abductive_insight: The background cases share a feature absent from the counterexample: in each, the agent exercises practical wisdom — the capacity to discern the appropriate occasion, manner, and degree of the virtuous response. What distinguishes genuine virtue from mere habitual beneficence is not stability, reason-responsiveness, or beneficial tendency alone, but the integration of these qualities with sound practical judgement.
passage:
One might begin by observing a pattern across cases that are commonly regarded as instances of genuine virtue. The soldier who faces danger from considered judgement, the judge who resists partiality from a settled commitment to fairness, and the physician who speaks truthfully from a recognition that patients deserve candour — each displays a stable disposition exercised in response to relevant reasons and directed towards a recognisable good. From these cases, one might extract a definition: a character trait is a virtue if and only if it is a stable disposition to act in a certain manner, exercised in response to the agent's recognition of relevant reasons, and tending to promote the good of those affected.

Yet one might construct a case which reveals that these three conditions, while individually necessary, are not jointly sufficient. Consider a woman who possesses a stable disposition to give money to beggars whenever she encounters them. She gives in response to her recognition of their need — a relevant reason — and her giving tends to promote their immediate welfare. All three conditions appear satisfied. Yet she gives with no regard for whether the recipient is genuinely destitute or merely exploiting her generosity, no consideration of whether alternative forms of assistance might be more effective, and no sensitivity to the limits of her own resources. Her disposition, though stable, reason-responsive, and locally beneficial, lacks the discriminating judgement that the soldier, the judge, and the physician each exhibit. What one might infer, by examining the respects in which the background cases differ from this problematic case, is that genuine virtue requires not merely a stable, reason-responsive, and beneficial disposition, but the integration of that disposition with practical wisdom — the capacity to discern the right occasion, the right manner, and the right degree of the response. Without this integrating capacity, a disposition that satisfies the stated conditions may degenerate into a mechanical habit that mimics virtue without embodying it.

background_cases: In a small farming community, each family contributes seed and labour to a cooperative harvest. The produce is divided in proportion to each family's contribution of seed and labour, and all regard the distribution as fair.

A group of scholars collaborates on a research project. The authorship credit is distributed according to each scholar's intellectual contribution to the work, with the principal investigator receiving first authorship. The arrangement is widely considered just.

A partnership of artisans divides the proceeds of a joint exhibition in accordance with the number and quality of the works each contributed. None objects to the division, and external observers agree that it is equitable.
definition: A distribution of goods among cooperating parties is just if and only if (i) the distribution is determined by a rule that is agreed upon in advance by all parties, (ii) the rule allocates shares in proportion to each party's contribution, and (iii) the rule is applied consistently and without favouritism.
conditions: The distribution is determined by a rule agreed upon in advance by all parties
The rule allocates shares in proportion to each party's contribution
The rule is applied consistently and without favouritism
counterexample: Suppose four workers agree in advance to divide the proceeds of a joint venture in proportion to their respective labour contributions, and the rule is applied consistently. However, one of the four workers is disabled and, despite exerting maximal effort, is able to contribute substantially less than the others. The proportional rule, applied strictly, would award her a negligible share. All three conditions are met, yet many would regard the outcome as unjust, since it takes no account of the worker's lesser capacity and the effort she has expended relative to her abilities.
abductive_insight: The background cases all involve parties of roughly comparable capacity, so that proportional contribution tracks effort and desert reasonably well. When capacities diverge significantly, proportionality to contribution ceases to reflect the morally relevant features of the situation, and a just distribution must incorporate sensitivity to the differing capacities of the parties involved.
passage:
One might observe that in the cases commonly cited as paradigms of just distribution among cooperators, a clear pattern emerges. In the farming cooperative, the research collaboration, and the artisans' partnership, the distribution of benefits is determined by a rule that is agreed upon in advance, allocates shares in proportion to contribution, and is applied consistently without favouritism. From this pattern, one might extract a definition: a distribution of goods among cooperating parties is just if and only if it is governed by a pre-agreed rule that allocates proportionally to contribution and is applied without favouritism.

Yet one might construct a case which reveals a limitation in this analysis. Suppose four workers agree in advance to divide the proceeds of a venture in proportion to their respective labour contributions. One of the four is disabled and, despite exerting the greatest effort relative to her capacity, contributes substantially less in absolute terms than her able-bodied colleagues. The rule is applied consistently: her share is negligible. All three conditions are satisfied. Yet it appears to many that the outcome is unjust, for it ignores the morally significant fact that the disabled worker has exerted maximal effort given her capacities. What the background cases have in common, and what the counterexample lacks, is a rough parity of capacity among the participants. When capacities are broadly similar, proportional contribution serves as a tolerably good proxy for effort and desert. But when capacities diverge sharply, proportionality to absolute contribution ceases to track the considerations that make a distribution fair. The definition, it appears, requires supplementation by a condition that is sensitive to the differing capacities of the parties, so that the distribution reflects not merely what each has contributed in absolute terms but what each has contributed relative to what each is able to contribute.

background_cases: A lifeguard on duty notices a swimmer in distress. She is trained, equipped, and positioned to effect a rescue at minimal risk. She is universally regarded as obligated to intervene.

A parent observes that his young child is about to touch a hot stove. He is standing within arm's reach and can prevent the injury with a trivial effort. Nearly everyone would say he is obligated to act.
A nurse in a hospital observes that a patient's intravenous line has become disconnected. She can reattach it in seconds and at no personal cost. She is clearly obligated to do so.
definition: An agent A has a moral obligation to prevent harm H to a person Q if and only if (i) A is aware that Q faces an imminent risk of H, (ii) A possesses the ability to prevent H without incurring disproportionate cost, and (iii) A stands in a special relationship to Q — such as that of professional, parent, or designated guardian — that generates a duty of care.
conditions: A is aware that Q faces an imminent risk of H
A possesses the ability to prevent H without incurring disproportionate cost
A stands in a special relationship to Q that generates a duty of care
counterexample: Suppose a competent swimmer, out for a morning walk, passes a pond in which a stranger's child is drowning. The swimmer is aware of the imminent risk, can effect a rescue at negligible personal cost, but stands in no special relationship to the child — she is not the child's parent, guardian, teacher, or professional carer. The definition, strictly applied, generates no obligation, since condition (iii) is not met. Yet most persons would insist that the swimmer is morally obligated to rescue the child.
abductive_insight: The background cases all involve agents in special relationships — lifeguard, parent, nurse — which made the relationship condition seem necessary. But the counterexample demonstrates that the moral obligation to prevent easily avoidable serious harm does not depend on a pre-existing special relationship; the sheer vulnerability of the person at risk and the triviality of the cost to the agent are sufficient to generate the obligation.
passage:
One might begin by noting a pattern in cases that are universally regarded as instances of moral obligation to prevent harm. The lifeguard who sees a swimmer in distress, the parent who observes his child reaching for a hot stove, and the nurse who notices a disconnected intravenous line are each aware of an imminent risk, each possesses the ability to intervene at minimal cost, and each stands in a special relationship to the endangered person — a relationship that generates a recognised duty of care. From these cases, one might extract a definition: an agent has a moral obligation to prevent harm if and only if the agent is aware of an imminent risk, can prevent it without disproportionate cost, and stands in a special relationship to the person at risk.

Yet one might construct a case which reveals that the third condition captures a feature of the background cases that is not, in fact, essential to the obligation. Suppose a competent swimmer, on a morning walk, passes a shallow pond in which a stranger's child is drowning. She is fully aware of the danger, and she can effect a rescue at negligible personal cost — the pond is shallow, the bank is stable, and no obstacle prevents her from wading in. But she stands in no special relationship to the child: she is not the child's parent, guardian, teacher, or any sort of professional carer. If we apply the definition strictly, no obligation arises, since the third condition is unsatisfied. Yet nearly every person of normal moral sensibility would insist that the swimmer is obligated to rescue the child. What the background cases share, and what led to the inclusion of the special-relationship condition, is the presence of an institutional or familial bond that makes the duty of care obvious and uncontroversial. But the counterexample suggests that this bond is a sufficient rather than a necessary condition for the obligation. The true ground of the obligation, it appears, is the combination of awareness, ability, and the triviality of the cost relative to the gravity of the harm — conditions that obtain regardless of whether any special relationship exists.

background_cases: A court sentences a burglar to a term of imprisonment proportionate to his offence, with the aim of deterring future burglaries and expressing communal condemnation of the act. The punishment is imposed after a fair trial and is widely regarded as legitimate.

A school imposes a suspension upon a student who has repeatedly disrupted classes, after a disciplinary hearing in which the student was given an opportunity to respond to the charges. The suspension is intended to correct the student's behaviour and to maintain an orderly environment. It is regarded as a fair exercise of disciplinary authority.

A sports league imposes a fine upon a player who has deliberately fouled an opponent, following a review by an independent panel. The fine is proportionate, aimed at deterring future fouls, and is accepted as a legitimate sanction within the rules of the game.
definition: An imposition of a penalty S upon a person P is a legitimate exercise of punitive authority if and only if (i) P has been found, through a fair procedure, to have committed a specified wrong, (ii) S is imposed by an authority that has been granted the power to punish by the relevant institutional framework, and (iii) S is proportionate to the severity of the wrong committed.
conditions: P has been found, through a fair procedure, to have committed a specified wrong
S is imposed by an authority granted the power to punish by the relevant institutional framework
S is proportionate to the severity of the wrong committed
counterexample: Consider a military tribunal that, following a scrupulously fair procedure, finds a soldier guilty of desertion in wartime. The tribunal, which is duly authorised by the military code, sentences the soldier to execution — a penalty that the tribunal regards as proportionate to the offence under the relevant code. All three conditions are met. Yet the soldier deserted because he was ordered to participate in the massacre of unarmed civilians, and his desertion was the only means available to him of refusing to commit an atrocity. Many would hold that punishing him is illegitimate, notwithstanding the satisfaction of all stated conditions.
abductive_insight: The background cases all involve wrongs that are genuinely wrongful — burglary, persistent disruption, deliberate fouling — so the question of whether the underlying conduct was truly blameworthy does not arise. The counterexample reveals that the definition presupposes the moral legitimacy of the rule that has been violated, and that punishment for the breach of an unjust directive is not rendered legitimate merely by procedural fairness, institutional authority, and proportionality.
passage:
One might begin by observing that in paradigmatic cases of legitimate punishment — the sentencing of a burglar by a court, the suspension of a disruptive student by a school, the fining of a deliberately fouling athlete by a sports league — three features recur. In each case, the person punished has been found through a fair procedure to have committed a specified wrong. In each case, the penalty is imposed by an authority that derives its punitive power from the relevant institutional framework. And in each case, the penalty is proportionate to the wrong. From these cases, one might extract a definition: an imposition of a penalty is a legitimate exercise of punitive authority if and only if the person has been fairly found to have committed a specified wrong, the authority is duly empowered, and the penalty is proportionate.

Yet one might construct a case which reveals a gap in this analysis. Consider a military tribunal that, after a fair hearing, finds a soldier guilty of desertion in wartime and sentences him to death — a penalty regarded as proportionate under the military code. The tribunal is duly constituted and empowered. All conditions are met. But the soldier deserted because he was ordered to participate in the massacre of unarmed civilians, and desertion was his only means of refusing to take part. His act, though formally a violation of the military code, was morally commendable — an act of conscience in the face of an atrocious command. To punish him seems not merely harsh but illegitimate. What the background cases have in common, and what the counterexample lacks, is the genuine wrongfulness of the underlying conduct. The burglar's burglary, the student's disruption, and the player's deliberate foul are all genuinely wrongful acts. The soldier's desertion, by contrast, is the refusal to commit an atrocity. The definition, it appears, presupposes that the rule which has been violated is itself morally legitimate, and fails to recognise that punishment for the breach of an unjust directive cannot be rendered legitimate by procedural fairness and proportionality alone.

background_cases: The courage of a soldier who overcomes his fear to charge a fortified position is recognised as virtuous because the soldier not only acts rightly but feels the pull of danger, deliberates, and chooses to act despite it — his courage involves the governance of fear by practical reason.

The temperance of a person who restrains his appetite at a feast, despite genuine desire for excess, is recognised as virtuous because the restraint is exercised by the agent's own rational judgement against the force of inclination.

The generosity of a benefactor who gives from her own resources to relieve the suffering of others is recognised as virtuous because she is moved by compassion, reflects upon the needs of others, and acts from a settled disposition to give what is appropriate.

The patience of a teacher who endures the frustrations of slow learners without irritation is recognised as virtuous because the teacher's equanimity arises from a reflective appreciation of the learners' difficulties, not from mere indifference.
definition: A character trait T is a virtue if and only if (i) T is a stable disposition to act in certain ways, (ii) the actions produced by T promote the well-being of the agent and others, and (iii) the exercise of T is accompanied by the appropriate emotional responses.
conditions: T is a stable disposition to act in certain ways
The actions produced by T promote well-being
The exercise of T is accompanied by appropriate emotions
counterexample: Consider a person who has been raised from infancy under a programme of strict behavioural conditioning, designed to produce unfailing honesty. She never tells a lie, her honesty is perfectly stable, it promotes the well-being of those around her, and she feels the appropriate satisfaction in truth-telling. All three conditions are satisfied. Yet she has never reflected upon the value of honesty, never deliberated about whether to lie or not — her behaviour is the product of conditioning rather than of rational self-governance. One might hesitate to call her honesty a virtue in the fullest sense.
abductive_insight: The background cases all involve agents whose virtuous conduct flows from rational deliberation and reflective endorsement of the trait in question. The definition captures the behavioural and affective dimensions of virtue but omits the rational dimension — the requirement that the agent exercise reflective governance over the disposition rather than merely exhibiting it as a product of conditioning or habituation.
passage:
When one surveys the character traits most confidently identified as virtues, a pattern emerges that rewards careful attention. The soldier's courage involves not merely the act of charging but the felt pull of danger, the deliberation about whether to advance, and the governance of fear by practical reason. The temperate person does not merely refrain from excess; he experiences the temptation, reflects upon it, and restrains himself by the exercise of judgement. The generous benefactor is moved by compassion but gives from a settled disposition that has been shaped by reflection upon what is appropriate. The patient teacher's equanimity arises not from emotional blankness but from a reflective appreciation of the difficulties facing her pupils.

One might attempt to codify these observations as follows: a character trait is a virtue if and only if it is a stable disposition to act in certain ways, the actions promote well-being, and the exercise of the trait is accompanied by the appropriate emotional responses. This definition captures the behavioural regularity, the beneficial orientation, and the affective dimension of the paradigm cases. Yet a case which reveals its insufficiency arises when we consider a person whose apparently virtuous disposition has been produced entirely by external conditioning. Imagine a child raised under a programme of behavioural modification so thorough that she never tells a lie, feels the appropriate satisfaction in honesty, and promotes the well-being of those who depend upon her truthfulness. Her honesty is stable, beneficial, and affectively appropriate — all three conditions are met. Yet she has never reflected upon the value of honesty, never weighed the reasons for and against deception, never exercised the kind of rational self-governance that characterises the agents in the background cases. The soldier overcame fear by deliberation; the temperate person restrained appetite by judgement; the generous benefactor gave from a disposition shaped by reflection. In each background case, rational agency was the wellspring of the virtuous conduct. The definition captures the outward form of virtue — stable dispositions, good effects, appropriate feelings — but omits the rational dimension that distinguishes virtue from mere good behaviour.

background_cases: The distribution of medical supplies during a wartime shortage according to the severity of patients' injuries is regarded as just because it allocates resources according to a morally relevant criterion — need — and the criterion is applied impartially.

The allocation of university places by competitive examination is regarded as just because it distributes a scarce good according to a morally relevant criterion — academic merit — applied without regard to the candidates' social origins.

The distribution of criminal sentences according to the severity of the offence is regarded as just because it matches the magnitude of the response to the magnitude of the wrong, applying a uniform standard to all offenders.
definition: A distribution of goods or burdens is just if and only if (i) it is governed by a criterion that is relevant to the good or burden being distributed, (ii) the criterion is applied impartially — like cases are treated alike, and (iii) the process of distribution is transparent.
conditions: The distribution is governed by a morally relevant criterion
The criterion is applied impartially
The process is transparent
counterexample: Consider a society that distributes access to clean drinking water according to a transparent criterion: distance from the water source. Those living closer receive more water; those farther away receive less. The criterion — proximity — is applied impartially, and the process is transparent. One might argue that proximity is a relevant criterion, since it reflects the practical costs of distribution. All three conditions appear satisfied. Yet the distribution seems unjust if those living farthest from the source are members of a social group that was forcibly relocated to the periphery by the ruling class. The apparent relevance and impartiality of the criterion conceals a prior injustice that the distribution perpetuates.
abductive_insight: The background cases all involve distributions in which the criterion of allocation is not merely relevant and impartially applied but is itself untainted by prior injustice. The definition captures formal impartiality and transparency but does not require that the criterion be free from the distorting influence of antecedent wrongs, permitting distributions that launder historical injustice through a formally neutral mechanism.
passage:
The distributions most securely regarded as just in the received philosophical tradition share a common structure that invites formalisation. The allocation of medical supplies by severity of need is governed by a criterion — need — that is plainly relevant to the good being distributed. The criterion is applied impartially: the general and the private soldier receive treatment according to the gravity of their injuries, not their rank. The process is transparent: the triage principles are publicly known. The allocation of university places by examination is similarly governed by a relevant criterion applied without favouritism in an open process. The distribution of criminal sentences matches the severity of punishment to the gravity of the offence by a publicly known standard.

One might codify these observations as follows: a distribution is just if and only if it is governed by a morally relevant criterion, the criterion is applied impartially, and the process is transparent. This definition captures the formal structure of the paradigm cases admirably. Yet a case which reveals its insufficiency arises when the criterion, though apparently relevant and impartially applied, has been shaped by prior injustice. Consider a society in which access to clean water is distributed by proximity to the sole water source. The criterion is applied impartially — every household at a given distance receives the same allocation — and the process is transparent. Proximity might even seem relevant, since transportation costs increase with distance. All three conditions are formally met. But suppose the households farthest from the source are those of a social group that was forcibly relocated to the periphery by the ruling class a generation ago. The criterion of proximity, apparently neutral, in fact perpetuates the consequences of a deliberate act of oppression. The background cases all involved criteria that were not merely relevant and impartially applied but untainted by antecedent wrongs. The definition captures formal justice but misses what one might call substantive justice — the requirement that the distributional criterion itself be free from the distorting influence of prior injustice.

background_cases: A lifeguard's obligation to rescue a drowning swimmer arises because the lifeguard has voluntarily accepted a position that creates a special relationship with those under her care, and the cost of performing the rescue is commensurate with the obligations of that role.
A parent's obligation to feed and shelter a child arises from the special relationship created by parenthood — a relationship that generates duties of care far exceeding those owed to strangers.
A doctor's obligation to treat a patient in his care arises from the voluntary assumption of a professional role that creates a special relationship and corresponding duties.
A ship captain's obligation to ensure the safety of passengers arises from the special relationship created by the captain's acceptance of responsibility for those aboard.
definition: A person P has a moral obligation to perform action A for the benefit of person Q if and only if (i) P stands in a special relationship to Q, (ii) A is within P's power, and (iii) the cost to P of performing A is not disproportionate to the benefit Q would receive.
conditions: P stands in a special relationship to Q
A is within P's power
The cost to P is not disproportionate to the benefit to Q
counterexample: Consider a competent swimmer walking past a shallow pond in which a stranger's child is drowning. The swimmer has no special relationship to the child — she is not a lifeguard, not a parent, not a teacher, not a professional of any kind with duties toward this child. Yet she can save the child's life by wading into the pond at the cost of wet clothing. The second and third conditions are met — the rescue is within her power and the cost is trivial relative to the benefit — but the first condition fails. Nevertheless, the obligation to rescue seems overwhelming.
abductive_insight: The background cases all involve obligations grounded in special relationships — lifeguard and swimmer, parent and child, doctor and patient. The definition abstracts the relational ground from these cases and treats it as a necessary condition. But the shallow-pond case shows that the moral pull of the second and third conditions — capacity and proportionality — can generate an obligation even in the absence of any antecedent special relationship, suggesting that the relational condition is characteristic of one important class of obligations but not necessary for all.
passage:
When one examines the moral obligations most readily affirmed in ordinary moral thought, a pattern emerges. The lifeguard is obligated to rescue the drowning swimmer because she has voluntarily accepted a role that places her in a special relationship with those she supervises. The parent is obligated to care for the child because the parent-child relationship generates duties of extraordinary weight. The doctor is obligated to treat the patient because the professional relationship creates obligations of fidelity and care. The ship captain is responsible for the passengers because acceptance of the captaincy creates a bond of responsibility. In each case, a special relationship between the parties is the ground of the obligation.

One might attempt to generalise from these cases as follows: a person has a moral obligation to benefit another if she stands in a special relationship to that person, the action is within her power, and the cost is not disproportionate to the benefit. This definition captures the paradigm cases elegantly. Yet a case which reveals its insufficiency is the case of the stranger at the shallow pond. A competent swimmer is walking past a pond in which a small child — a complete stranger — is drowning. She bears no special relationship to the child: she is not a lifeguard, not a relative, not a teacher, not a professional with any duty of care. The first condition fails. Yet the rescue is within her power, and the cost — wet clothing, a few minutes of discomfort — is trivial in comparison to the child's life. The moral obligation seems undeniable, and its force does not depend upon any antecedent relationship. The background cases, by featuring obligations rooted in special relationships, may have led the definition to mistake a frequent ground of obligation for a necessary one. The shallow-pond case suggests that the conjunction of capacity and gross disproportion between cost and benefit can generate obligations that arise not from relationships but from the sheer moral weight of the situation.

background_cases: A merchant's promise to deliver goods by a specified date binds because the promisee has relied upon the promise in planning his affairs, and the merchant was in a position to fulfil the promise at the time it was made.
A friend's promise to collect another from the railway station binds because the promisee has foregone alternative arrangements in reliance upon the promise.

A contractor's promise to complete building work by a specified date binds because the client has entered into further commitments — leases, purchases of furniture, arrangements with tenants — in reliance upon the promise.
definition: A promise by S to R to perform action A is morally binding if and only if (i) S has communicated to R an intention to perform A, (ii) R has relied upon S's communication in a material way, and (iii) S was capable of performing A at the time the promise was made.
conditions: S communicated an intention to perform A
R relied upon the communication in a material way
S was capable of performing A at the time of promising
counterexample: Consider a promise made to a dying friend. A man at his friend's deathbed promises that he will tend the friend's grave each year on the anniversary of his death. The friend dies moments later. There is no material reliance — the friend is dead and cannot have relied upon the promise in any way that affects his affairs. The second condition fails. Yet most persons of moral seriousness would regard the promise as binding; to break it would seem a betrayal of trust and a dishonour to the dead.
abductive_insight: The background cases all involve promisees who materially relied upon the promise — they altered their plans, foregone alternatives, or entered into further commitments. The definition abstracts this reliance as a necessary condition. But the deathbed case reveals that a promise may bind even when reliance is impossible, suggesting that the moral force of promising has a source independent of reliance — perhaps in the solemnity of the commitment itself or in the relationship between promisor and promisee.
passage:
The promises most frequently adduced in philosophical discussions of obligation share a recognisable feature. The merchant's promise to deliver goods binds because the buyer has ordered his affairs around the expected delivery. The friend's promise to provide transport binds because the promisee has foregone other means of getting home. The contractor's promise to finish work by a certain date binds because the client has entered into leases and purchases in reliance upon the completion. In each case, the promisee has materially relied upon the promise, and it is this reliance that gives the promise its moral weight.

One might codify these observations by defining a morally binding promise as one in which the promisor has communicated an intention, the promisee has materially relied upon it, and the promisor was capable of performance. This definition captures the paradigm cases well. Yet a case which reveals its insufficiency is the promise made at a friend's deathbed. A man promises his dying friend that he will tend the friend's grave on each anniversary of his death. The friend dies moments later. There is no possibility of material reliance: the friend is dead, and nothing in his worldly affairs has been altered by the promise. The second condition fails entirely. Yet the promise seems deeply binding. To neglect the grave, to treat the promise as a mere form of words uttered to comfort a dying man, would strike most persons as a moral failing of some gravity. The background cases all grounded the binding force of promises in reliance, but the deathbed case suggests that reliance, though a powerful source of obligation, is not the only one. The very act of promising — the solemnity of the commitment, the trust reposed in the promisor, the relationship between the parties — may generate obligations that persist even when no reliance is possible.

background_cases: A driver who recklessly runs a red light and injures a pedestrian is morally responsible for the injury because the driver performed a voluntary action (running the light), the action directly caused the harm, and the driver could foresee that running red lights endangers pedestrians.

A factory supervisor who knowingly orders the use of unsafe machinery, resulting in a worker's injury, is morally responsible because the supervisor voluntarily issued the order, the order caused the harm, and the supervisor foresaw the danger.

A pharmacist who carelessly dispenses the wrong medication, causing a patient to fall ill, is morally responsible because the pharmacist performed a voluntary act (filling the prescription), the act caused the harm, and the pharmacist could foresee that errors in dispensing are dangerous.
definition: An agent A is morally responsible for a harmful outcome O if and only if (i) A performed a voluntary action that was a proximate cause of O, (ii) A could reasonably foresee that the action might produce harmful consequences, and (iii) A was not acting under compulsion or duress.
conditions: A performed a voluntary action that proximately caused O
A could reasonably foresee harmful consequences
A was not acting under compulsion or duress
counterexample: Consider the manager of a textile mill in Manchester in the 1830s who employs children as young as eight to tend the looms. The manager acts voluntarily, can foresee that the work is injurious to the children's health, and is under no compulsion — other mills in the district have begun to employ only older workers. All three conditions are met, and one would not hesitate to say the manager is morally responsible. But now consider a different manager of the same period who employs children under precisely the same conditions, yet does so because every mill in the district does the same, because the prevailing moral opinion of the day holds child labour to be both natural and beneficial, and because no law prohibits it. This manager, too, satisfies all three conditions — the action is voluntary, the foreseeable harms are the same, and no one compels him. Yet one might hesitate to assign the same degree of moral responsibility, for this manager's moral understanding was shaped by a social context that uniformly endorsed the practice.
abductive_insight: The background cases all involve agents whose moral culpability is clear because the wrongfulness of their actions — reckless driving, knowingly using unsafe machinery, careless dispensing — is recognised by the prevailing moral standards of their community. The definition captures voluntariness, foreseeability, and freedom from compulsion but omits the question of whether the agent had access to moral resources — prevailing norms, available criticism, alternative models of conduct — that would have enabled recognition of the action's wrongfulness.
passage:
When one examines the cases most straightforwardly cited as instances of moral responsibility, a pattern emerges that the standard definition does not fully capture. The reckless driver, the negligent supervisor, the careless pharmacist — each acts voluntarily, foresees the risk of harm, and is free from compulsion. But each also acts in a context in which the wrongfulness of the action is clearly recognised: traffic laws condemn reckless driving, safety regulations condemn the use of defective machinery, and professional standards condemn careless dispensing. The moral environment, so to speak, provides the agent with the resources to recognise that the action is wrong.

One might codify these cases by defining moral responsibility as arising whenever a voluntary, uncompelled agent performs an action whose harmful consequences are reasonably foreseeable. This definition accommodates the paradigm cases. Yet a case which reveals its insufficiency may be drawn from the history of industrial labour. Consider the manager of a textile mill in Manchester in the 1830s who employs children as young as eight to tend the looms. The work is dangerous: the children are maimed by the machinery, stunted by the long hours, and sickened by the dust. The manager acts voluntarily. He can foresee these harms — he sees them daily. He is under no compulsion; other mills in the district have begun to limit the employment of young children. All three conditions are met, and one would rightly hold him responsible. But now consider a different manager, in a district where every mill employs children, where the prevailing moral opinion — shared by clergy, magistrates, and parents alike — holds that child labour is not merely acceptable but positively beneficial, instilling discipline and preventing idleness. This manager, too, acts voluntarily and foresees the physical costs. Yet his moral understanding has been formed in a community that uniformly endorses the practice. He lacks the moral resources — the dissenting voices, the alternative models — that would enable him to see his action as wrong. The definition pronounces both managers equally responsible, yet one might observe that the moral situation of the second is importantly different from that of the first, and that a satisfactory account of responsibility must attend to whether the agent had access to the moral understanding that would have made the wrongfulness of the action recognisable.

background_cases: A friend who promises to meet you at a certain time and place is bound by the promise because she has voluntarily undertaken an obligation, you have relied upon the undertaking in planning your day, and the meeting is within her power to perform.

A tradesman who promises to complete repairs by a fixed date is bound because he freely made the commitment, the client has made arrangements in reliance upon it, and the work is within his competence.

A physician who promises to attend a patient at a scheduled appointment is bound because the promise was voluntary, the patient has relied upon it by forgoing other medical consultations, and attendance is within the physician's power.

A solicitor who promises to file documents before a deadline is bound because the promise was freely given, the client relies upon it, and filing is a routine professional act within the solicitor's capacity.
definition: A person P is morally obligated to keep a promise to Q if and only if (i) P voluntarily undertook the obligation by making the promise, (ii) Q relied upon the promise in shaping Q's subsequent conduct, and (iii) the performance of the promise is within P's power.
conditions: P voluntarily made the promise
Q relied upon the promise
Performance is within P's power
counterexample: Consider a person who promises his dying father that he will never sell the family estate. The promise is voluntary, sincerely given, and the father relies upon it in the sense that it brings him peace in his final hours. Years later, the estate has become a financial burden, the man's own children are suffering for want of resources that the sale would provide, and the neighbourhood has changed so fundamentally that the estate serves no purpose the father could have envisaged. The man can keep the promise — it is within his power — and his father relied upon it. All three conditions are met. Yet one might hesitate to say the man is morally obligated to keep the promise, for the circumstances have changed so dramatically that the promise no longer serves the purpose for which it was extracted.
abductive_insight: The background cases all involve promises made in circumstances that remain substantially unchanged at the time of performance: the friend's meeting, the tradesman's repairs, the physician's appointment, and the solicitor's filing all take place in conditions relevantly similar to those that obtained when the promise was made. The definition captures voluntariness, reliance, and capacity but does not address whether the circumstances presupposed by the promise have changed so fundamentally as to undermine the moral force of the commitment.
passage:
The cases most commonly cited in defence of the moral obligation to keep promises share a feature that deserves explicit attention. The friend who promises to meet you, the tradesman who promises to complete repairs, the physician who promises to attend an appointment, the solicitor who promises to file documents — in each case, the promise is made against a background of circumstances that are expected to persist until performance is due. The friend expects to be in the city; the tradesman expects the materials to be available; the physician expects to be in health; the solicitor expects the courts to be open. The moral force of the promise presupposes a certain stability of conditions.

One might define the obligation to keep a promise by requiring that it was voluntarily given, that the promisee relied upon it, and that performance remains within the promisor's power. This definition captures the paradigm cases admirably. Yet a case which reveals its insufficiency involves a promise made in circumstances that have since been transformed beyond recognition. A man promises his dying father that he will never sell the family estate — a promise given voluntarily, relied upon by the father in his final hours, and well within the son's power to keep. Years pass. The estate, once the centre of a prosperous agricultural community, is now surrounded by industrial development. Its maintenance has become a crippling financial burden. The man's own children are in want of resources that the sale of the estate would provide. The purpose the father envisaged — the preservation of a family seat in a thriving countryside — has been defeated by circumstances no one could have anticipated. The man can keep the promise, and his father relied upon it. All three conditions are satisfied. Yet one might observe that the moral force of the promise has been undermined by the transformation of the very circumstances that gave it meaning. The obligation to keep a promise, it appears, depends not only on voluntariness, reliance, and capacity, but on the continued existence of the conditions that the promise presupposed — conditions that the definition does not mention.

background_cases: The distribution of water in a besieged city is just when each person receives an equal share, because all have equal need and no one has a prior claim to a larger portion; equality of distribution matches equality of need.

The distribution of prizes in an athletic competition is just when they are awarded to the fastest, the strongest, or the most skilful, because the purpose of the competition is to recognise and reward athletic excellence; distribution according to merit matches the nature of the activity.

The distribution of places in a lifeboat is just when priority is given to children and those unable to swim, because the urgency of the need and the vulnerability of the recipients demand that those in greatest danger be served first; distribution according to need matches the gravity of the situation.
definition: A distribution of goods among a group of persons is just if and only if (i) the goods are distributed according to a consistent principle, (ii) the principle is applied impartially — without favouritism or discrimination — to all members of the group, and (iii) the principle is one that the members of the group could reasonably accept.
conditions: Goods are distributed according to a consistent principle
The principle is applied impartially
The principle is one the members could reasonably accept
counterexample: Consider a colonial administration that distributes land to settlers according to a consistent principle — each settler receives a plot of equal size — applied impartially to all settlers without favouritism, and the settlers unanimously accept the principle as fair. All three conditions are satisfied. Yet the land being distributed was seized from an indigenous population that was not consulted, was not included in the group among whom the distribution was made, and whose prior claims were simply disregarded. The distribution is just by the definition's lights, because the definition considers only the members of the group among whom distribution occurs and the principle by which it is conducted.
abductive_insight: The background cases — the besieged city, the competition, the lifeboat — all involve distributions among groups whose membership is itself uncontroversial: the inhabitants of the city, the entrants in the competition, the passengers on the ship. The justice of the distribution is assessed only within the group. The definition captures consistency, impartiality, and reasonable acceptability but omits the prior question of whether the group among whom distribution occurs has been constituted justly — whether all those with legitimate claims have been included and whether the goods being distributed were legitimately available for distribution.
passage:
When one examines the distributions most readily acknowledged as just, a feature emerges that the standard analysis passes over in silence. The equal distribution of water in a besieged city is just because all inhabitants have equal need. The distribution of prizes to the best athletes is just because merit is the appropriate criterion for competitions. The prioritisation of children in a lifeboat is just because vulnerability demands precedence. In each case, the justice of the distribution is assessed among a group whose membership is uncontested — the inhabitants, the competitors, the passengers — and the goods distributed are uncontroversially available.

One might codify these cases by defining a just distribution as one that follows a consistent, impartially applied principle that the members of the group could reasonably accept. This captures the paradigm cases. Yet a case which reveals its insufficiency may be drawn from colonial history. A colonial administration distributes land to settlers according to a scrupulously consistent principle: each settler receives a plot of equal size. The principle is applied impartially — no settler is favoured over another. The settlers, assembled in a meeting, unanimously accept the principle as fair. All three conditions are met. But the land being distributed was taken from an indigenous population that was excluded from the process entirely. The indigenous people were not counted among the group, their prior claims were not considered, and no principle — however consistent, impartial, or acceptable to the settlers — can render just a distribution of goods that were unjustly acquired and distributed among a group that was unjustly constituted. One might observe that justice in distribution presupposes the justice of the prior arrangements — the legitimacy of the group's composition and the legitimacy of its claim to the goods — and that a definition attending only to the internal fairness of the distributional principle cannot capture this prior requirement.

background_cases: A neighbour who shovels the snow from an elderly person's path acts benevolently because the action is motivated by concern for the elderly person's welfare, is performed without expectation of return, and produces a clear benefit for the recipient.

A stranger who helps a lost child find her parents acts benevolently because the action springs from concern for the child's well-being, is performed at some inconvenience to the stranger, and results in the child's reunion with her family.

A teacher who stays after hours to help a struggling student acts benevolently because the action is motivated by care for the student's progress, is performed without additional compensation, and contributes to the student's improvement.
definition: An action X performed by A toward B is benevolent if and only if (i) X is motivated by genuine concern for B's welfare, (ii) X produces a benefit for B, and (iii) A performs X without expectation of reciprocal benefit.
conditions: X is motivated by genuine concern for B's welfare
X produces a benefit for B
A acts without expectation of reciprocal benefit
counterexample: Consider a wealthy Victorian philanthropist who, motivated by genuine concern for the welfare of the poor, establishes a workhouse in which the destitute are housed, fed, and given employment. He expects no return for himself. The workhouse does produce a benefit — the inmates are no longer starving on the streets. All three conditions are met. Yet the regime of the workhouse is so severe, so degrading in its regimentation and its separation of families, that the recipients themselves regard it as a punishment rather than a benefit. The philanthropist's concern is genuine, but he has imposed his conception of welfare upon the recipients without regard to their own understanding of what would benefit them.
abductive_insight: The background cases — the shovelled path, the found child, the after-hours tutoring — all involve beneficent actions whose character as benefits is uncontroversial from the recipient's own perspective: the elderly person wants the path cleared, the child wants her parents, the student wants to improve. The definition captures motivation, benefit, and disinterestedness but omits the requirement that the benefit be one the recipient would recognise as such, rather than one imposed according to the benefactor's own conception of the recipient's good.
passage:
The actions most securely identified as benevolent share a feature that rewards careful examination. The neighbour who clears the snow, the stranger who reunites the lost child, the teacher who stays to help the struggling student — in each case, the action is not only motivated by concern and productive of benefit but is an action whose beneficial character the recipient acknowledges. The elderly person is glad of the cleared path. The child is relieved to find her parents. The student is grateful for the extra help. The benefactor's conception of what is good for the recipient coincides with the recipient's own.

One might define benevolence as action motivated by genuine concern for another's welfare, productive of benefit, and performed without expectation of return. This captures the paradigm cases. Yet a case which reveals its insufficiency may be found in the history of Victorian philanthropy. A wealthy benefactor, moved by sincere concern for the welfare of the destitute, establishes a workhouse. The inmates are housed, fed, and given employment — they are no longer starving in the streets. The benefactor expects nothing in return. All three conditions are satisfied. Yet the regime of the workhouse is severe beyond anything the inmates would have chosen: families are separated, daily life is regimented to the hour, and the conditions of labour are exhausting. The inmates regard the workhouse not as a benefit but as a humiliation, and many prefer the streets to its regime. The philanthropist's concern is genuine, and the action does produce a benefit of a sort — survival — but the benefit is defined entirely by the philanthropist's own conception of the recipients' good, imposed without consultation. One might observe that genuine benevolence requires not only that the benefactor intend the recipient's welfare but that the benefit be one the recipient would recognise as such, and that actions which impose the benefactor's conception of welfare upon unwilling recipients partake more of paternalism than of benevolence.

background_cases: A physician who promises to attend a patient at a scheduled appointment has a duty to keep the appointment because she has voluntarily undertaken the obligation, the patient has relied upon it, and the performance is within her power; the duty is grounded in a specific, identifiable relationship between the physician and the patient.

A solicitor who agrees to represent a client in a legal proceeding has a duty to prepare the case with diligence because the solicitor has voluntarily assumed the obligation, the client has relied upon the solicitor's undertaking, and the work is within the solicitor's competence; the duty arises from the particular professional relationship.

A teacher who agrees to supervise a pupil's examination has a duty to be present and attentive because the teacher has undertaken the obligation voluntarily, the pupil relies upon the supervision, and the teacher is capable of providing it; the duty is rooted in the defined institutional relationship between teacher and pupil.
definition: A person P has a moral duty to perform an action X if and only if (i) P has voluntarily undertaken an obligation to perform X, (ii) another identifiable person Q has relied upon P's undertaking, and (iii) the performance of X is within P's power.
conditions: P has voluntarily undertaken the obligation
An identifiable person Q has relied upon the undertaking
Performance is within P's power
counterexample: Consider a bystander who happens upon a child drowning in a shallow pond. The bystander has made no promise, entered no professional relationship, and undertaken no voluntary obligation toward the child. No identifiable person has relied upon the bystander's doing anything. Yet the bystander can save the child at negligible personal cost — she need only wade in and pull the child from the water. It appears that the bystander has a duty to act, and that her failure to do so would be a serious moral failing, notwithstanding the absence of any voluntary undertaking or relationship of reliance.
abductive_insight: The background cases all involve duties that arise from voluntary undertakings within specific relationships — physician and patient, solicitor and client, teacher and pupil. The definition captures this relational structure. Yet the drowning-child case reveals a duty that exists independently of any prior relationship or voluntary commitment. What generates the duty in this case is the conjunction of the bystander's capacity to prevent grave harm and the triviality of the cost to her of doing so. The definition, by grounding duty exclusively in voluntary undertaking and reliance, cannot accommodate duties that arise from the sheer capacity to prevent serious harm at minimal cost.
passage:
When one examines the most familiar examples of moral duty, a relational pattern emerges. The physician owes her duty to her patient because she has voluntarily entered the professional relationship and the patient has relied upon her. The solicitor owes his duty to his client for the same reasons. The teacher owes her duty to her pupil because she has accepted the institutional role and the pupil depends upon her. In each case, the duty presupposes a specific relationship in which one party has voluntarily assumed an obligation and another has relied upon it.

One might codify this pattern by the following definition: a person has a moral duty to perform an action if and only if she has voluntarily undertaken the obligation, another identifiable person has relied upon the undertaking, and the performance is within her power. This definition captures the relational structure of the paradigmatic duties. Yet a case which reveals its insufficiency arises when one considers the duty of rescue. A bystander happens upon a child drowning in a shallow pond. The bystander has made no promise, entered no professional relationship, and undertaken no obligation of any kind toward the child. No one has relied upon the bystander's acting — indeed, no one is aware of the bystander's presence. Yet the bystander can save the child by wading into the pond at negligible personal cost. It appears that she has a duty to do so, and that her failure to act would be morally culpable in the highest degree. The background cases all involve duties grounded in voluntary undertaking and reliance, and the definition is faithful to their structure. But the drowning-child case reveals a duty that has no such grounding. What generates this duty is the bystander's capacity to prevent a grave harm at trivial cost — a feature entirely absent from the definition's requirements. One might observe that the definition mistakes one important source of moral duty for the only source, and that a complete account must recognise duties that arise not from what one has promised but from what one can prevent.

background_cases: A factory owner who pays two workers different wages for the same work, performed with equal skill and diligence, commits an injustice because the workers' contributions are equal and the distribution of rewards should reflect equality of contribution; the injustice consists in treating relevantly similar cases differently.

A university that admits two applicants with identical qualifications but rejects a third with the same qualifications commits an injustice because all three are relevantly alike, and consistency demands that like cases be treated alike; the injustice consists in the arbitrary differentiation of equals.

A judge who sentences two offenders convicted of the same crime to markedly different penalties, in the absence of any relevant difference in their circumstances, commits an injustice because the cases are relevantly identical and the principle of equal treatment requires equal sentences; the injustice consists in the inconsistent application of a standard.
definition: A distribution of benefits or burdens is just if and only if (i) relevantly similar cases are treated alike, (ii) any difference in treatment corresponds to a relevant difference in the cases, and (iii) the criteria of relevance are applied consistently across all cases.
conditions: Relevantly similar cases are treated alike
Differences in treatment reflect relevant differences
Criteria of relevance are consistently applied
counterexample: Consider a society in which all citizens are taxed at precisely the same rate, all receive the same public services, and all are subject to the same laws applied with perfect consistency. Relevantly similar cases are treated alike in every domain; differences in treatment (there are none) correspond to relevant differences (there are none); and the criteria are applied with exemplary consistency. All three conditions are satisfied. Yet suppose this society contains citizens of vastly different means — some possessing enormous wealth, others living in destitution — and that the uniform tax rate, while formally equal, imposes a negligible burden on the wealthy and a crushing burden on the poor. The distribution satisfies the formal requirements of the definition but produces a state of affairs that most persons would regard as profoundly unjust.
abductive_insight: The background cases all involve injustices that consist in the arbitrary differentiation of equals — paying different wages for equal work, admitting some qualified applicants while rejecting others, sentencing identical offenders differently. The definition captures this formal requirement of consistency. Yet the taxation case reveals that formal consistency can coexist with substantive injustice. A distribution may treat every case alike and yet be unjust because it fails to attend to morally relevant differences in the situations of those affected — differences in need, vulnerability, or capacity that a purely formal definition cannot register.
passage:
When one surveys the cases most readily identified as instances of injustice, a common structure presents itself. The factory owner who pays unequal wages for equal work, the university that admits some qualified applicants while rejecting others with identical credentials, the judge who sentences similar offenders differently — in each case, the injustice consists in the failure to treat relevantly similar cases alike. The principle of formal equality — that like cases be treated in like manner — appears to capture the essence of justice.

One might codify this principle by defining a just distribution as one in which relevantly similar cases are treated alike, differences in treatment correspond to relevant differences, and the criteria of relevance are consistently applied. This definition has the virtue of precision and the authority of a long philosophical tradition. Yet a case which reveals its insufficiency may be constructed by imagining a society that satisfies all three conditions perfectly. Every citizen pays the same tax rate. Every citizen receives the same public services. Every citizen is subject to the same laws, administered with impeccable consistency. No case is treated differently from any relevantly similar case. Yet suppose this formally equal society contains citizens of vastly different means. A flat tax of twenty per cent imposes a trivial sacrifice upon the very wealthy and an intolerable burden upon those who can barely afford food and shelter. The distribution is formally just — every case is treated alike — but substantively unjust, for it ignores the morally significant differences in the situations of the persons affected. The background cases all involve injustices of inconsistency; the taxation case reveals an injustice of a different kind — the injustice of formal equality applied without regard to the material circumstances that determine its actual impact. One might observe that justice requires not merely that like cases be treated alike but that the criteria of likeness be sensitive to the substantive conditions of the persons affected.

background_cases: A patient who agrees to a surgical procedure after being fully informed of its risks, benefits, and alternatives gives morally valid consent because the agreement is voluntary, the patient has capacity, and the relevant information has been disclosed; the consent is genuine because the patient's will is unimpeded and her understanding is complete.

A research subject who volunteers for a clinical trial after reading a detailed consent form and discussing the study with the investigator gives morally valid consent because the agreement is freely given, the subject has full mental capacity, and the nature, risks, and purposes of the research have been explained; the consent is genuine because no relevant information has been withheld.

A tenant who signs a lease after reviewing its terms, asking questions, and comparing offers from other landlords gives morally valid consent because the agreement is voluntary, the tenant has capacity, and the terms have been fully disclosed; the consent is genuine because the tenant enters the agreement with open eyes.
definition: A person P gives morally valid consent to an arrangement A if and only if (i) P agrees to A voluntarily and without coercion, (ii) P has the mental capacity to understand the nature and consequences of A, and (iii) P has been provided with all information material to the decision.
conditions: P agrees voluntarily and without coercion
P has mental capacity
P has been given all material information
counterexample: Consider a migrant worker who is offered employment at a wage far below the prevailing rate, under conditions that local workers would refuse. The worker agrees voluntarily — no one threatens or coerces him. He has full mental capacity and understands the terms of employment. He has been given all material information: the wage, the hours, the conditions. All three conditions are satisfied. Yet the worker's consent is given against a background of extreme economic necessity: he has no other means of supporting his family, no legal right to seek better employment, and no practical possibility of refusing. His agreement, though formally voluntary and fully informed, is extracted by circumstances that leave him no real choice.
abductive_insight: The background cases all involve parties whose consent is given in conditions of genuine choice — the patient could decline surgery, the research subject could withdraw, the tenant could rent elsewhere. The definition captures the formal conditions of valid consent but does not attend to the background conditions that make genuine choice possible. The migrant worker case reveals that consent may satisfy all three conditions and yet be morally deficient because the consenting party, though technically free and fully informed, faces a set of circumstances so constrained that the agreement amounts to submission rather than choice.
passage:
The cases most readily accepted as instances of morally valid consent share a feature that deserves explicit attention. The patient who consents to surgery does so in a setting where she could decline and seek a second opinion. The research subject who volunteers for a trial does so in a setting where withdrawal carries no penalty. The tenant who signs a lease does so in a market where alternative accommodation is available. In each case, consent is given not merely voluntarily and with understanding but against a background of genuine alternatives.

One might codify the conditions of valid consent by requiring voluntariness, mental capacity, and full disclosure of material information. This definition captures the formal requirements of the paradigmatic cases. Yet a case which reveals its insufficiency arises when the consenting party, though formally free and fully informed, faces circumstances that eliminate genuine choice. Consider a migrant worker offered employment at a wage far below the prevailing rate, under conditions of considerable hardship. The worker agrees voluntarily — no one holds a weapon to his head or threatens him with violence. He understands the terms completely. He has been told the wage, the hours, the living conditions. All three conditions are satisfied. Yet the worker consents because he has no other means of feeding his family. He has no legal right to seek employment elsewhere, no savings to fall back upon, and no realistic prospect of refusal. His consent, though voluntary in the formal sense, is extracted by a desperation that reduces his alternatives to one. The background cases all involve consent given in conditions of genuine choice; the migrant worker's consent is given in conditions that make refusal tantamount to starvation. One might observe that morally valid consent requires not only the absence of direct coercion but the presence of a reasonable range of alternatives, a condition the definition does not mention.

background_cases: A thief who is sentenced to imprisonment for stealing from his neighbours is justly punished because he committed a wrongful act, the sentence is proportionate to the offence, and the punishment serves to deter others from similar conduct; the punishment addresses both the wrong done and the prevention of future wrongs.

A fraudster who is fined for deceiving her investors is justly punished because she committed a culpable wrong, the fine is proportionate to the harm inflicted, and the penalty serves to express the community's condemnation of dishonesty in commercial dealings; the punishment addresses both desert and denunciation.

A vandal who is required to perform community service for defacing public property is justly punished because the offence was voluntary, the sentence is proportionate, and the requirement to serve the community he has harmed restores something of what was lost; the punishment addresses desert, proportionality, and a measure of reparation.
definition: The punishment of a wrongdoer W is morally justified if and only if (i) W has committed a culpable wrongful act, (ii) the punishment is proportionate to the gravity of the wrong, and (iii) the punishment serves a legitimate purpose — deterrence, denunciation, or reparation.
conditions: W committed a culpable wrong
The punishment is proportionate
The punishment serves a legitimate purpose
counterexample: Consider a person who, in his youth, committed a serious assault upon another person. He was never apprehended. Thirty years have passed. He has reformed his character entirely: he has lived an exemplary life, devoted himself to charitable work, and made private restitution to his victim, who has long since forgiven him. The community in which he now lives regards him as a model citizen. He is now identified by new forensic methods and brought before a court. He committed a culpable wrong; a proportionate sentence can be devised; and the punishment would serve the legitimate purpose of denunciation. All three conditions are met. Yet one might hesitate to punish a person who has, by his own unaided efforts, accomplished everything that punishment is designed to achieve — reformation, reparation, and the re-establishment of right conduct.
abductive_insight: The background cases all involve wrongdoers who stand before the court unreformed and unreconstructed — the thief is still a thief, the fraudster a fraudster, the vandal a vandal — and in each case the punishment serves purposes that remain unachieved at the time of sentencing. The definition captures the conditions that make punishment appropriate in such cases. Yet the reformed offender case reveals that the purposes of punishment — deterrence, denunciation, reparation, and reformation — may already have been accomplished before the state intervenes, and that imposing punishment in such circumstances serves no purpose beyond the mechanical satisfaction of the definition's conditions.
passage:
When one examines the cases most commonly cited in defence of the moral justification of punishment, a temporal feature emerges. The thief is punished while he is still a thief — his character is unreformed, his conduct unrepented, and the purposes of punishment are prospective. The fraudster is fined while the community's condemnation remains unspoken. The vandal is sentenced while the damage to the community remains unrepaired. In each case, the punishment serves purposes that, at the time of sentencing, remain to be achieved.

One might codify this pattern by defining morally justified punishment as the proportionate penalisation of a culpable wrongdoer in service of a legitimate purpose such as deterrence, denunciation, or reparation. This definition accommodates the paradigmatic cases with ease. Yet a case which reveals its insufficiency arises when the legitimate purposes of punishment have already been achieved before the state intervenes. Suppose a young man commits a serious assault and escapes apprehension. Thirty years pass. During those years, he undergoes a profound moral transformation. He devotes himself to charitable work, makes private restitution to his victim, and lives an exemplary life. His victim has forgiven him. His community regards him as a person of conspicuous virtue. He is now identified by modern forensic techniques and brought before a court. The wrong was culpable; a proportionate sentence can be determined; and the punishment would serve the denunciatory purpose of expressing the community's condemnation. All three conditions are met. Yet one might observe that every purpose punishment could serve has already been accomplished. The man is reformed; reparation has been made; the victim has been reconciled. To punish him now is to impose suffering in service of purposes already achieved. The background cases all involve wrongdoers whose transformation remains to be effected; the present case involves a wrongdoer who has effected his own transformation, and the definition, by attending only to culpability, proportionality, and purpose, cannot distinguish between the two situations.

background_cases: Aristotle's account of the courageous man holds that courage consists not in the absence of fear but in the mastery of fear through rational deliberation; the courageous soldier feels fear but acts rightly despite it, and this capacity is the fruit of repeated practice in confronting danger.

The temperance of the moderate drinker, in Aristotle's view, is not mere abstention but a disposition cultivated through habit to take pleasure in the right amount; the temperate man enjoys food and drink without excess because he has trained his appetites to conform to reason.

The just person, as characterised in the Nicomachean Ethics, does not merely perform just actions by accident or compulsion but does so from a settled state of character that has been developed through habituation and reflective practice over the course of a life.
definition: A character trait T is a virtue if and only if (i) T is a stable disposition to act well in a particular domain of human life, (ii) the exercise of T is characteristically accompanied by the appropriate emotional response, and (iii) T has been cultivated through habitual practice and rational reflection.
conditions: T is a stable disposition to act well in a particular domain of human life
The exercise of T is characteristically accompanied by the appropriate emotional response
T has been cultivated through habitual practice and rational reflection
counterexample: Consider a person who, owing to a pharmacological intervention, acquires a perfectly stable disposition to act generously. The drug produces not only generous behaviour but the appropriate emotional accompaniments — genuine sympathy, pleasure in giving.
