The disposition is stable and operates in the relevant domain of human life. All three conditions appear to be satisfied. Yet the person has never practised generosity or reflected upon it; the disposition was produced by a pill rather than by the slow labour of moral cultivation.
abductive_insight: The background cases share a feature that the pharmacological case lacks: in each, the virtuous disposition was forged through the agent's own sustained effort — through confronting fear repeatedly, moderating appetite over time, performing just actions until justice became second nature. What distinguishes genuine virtue from its pharmacological simulacrum is not the behavioural output or even the accompanying emotion but the process by which the disposition came to be: a process of voluntary, effortful self-formation that the definition's third condition gestures toward but does not fully secure against technological circumvention.
passage:
When one examines the paradigmatic cases of virtue recognised in the philosophical tradition, a pattern emerges that goes beyond the mere possession of a stable and emotionally appropriate disposition. Aristotle's courageous man does not merely act bravely; he has become brave through the repeated experience of confronting danger and mastering his fear. The temperate person has not merely arrived at moderate appetites; he has cultivated moderation through a prolonged discipline of habit and reason. The just person does not perform just actions by happy accident; his justice is the product of a lifetime of practice and reflection.

One might attempt to codify this pattern with the following definition: a character trait is a virtue if it is a stable disposition to act well in a domain of human life, is accompanied by the appropriate emotional response, and has been cultivated through habitual practice and rational reflection. This definition captures the Aristotelian emphasis on stability, emotional aptness, and habituation. Yet a case which reveals its insufficiency may be imagined. Suppose a pharmacological advance produces a pill that, when ingested, installs a perfectly stable disposition to act generously. The person who takes the pill feels genuine sympathy, takes real pleasure in giving, and acts generously in all the relevant situations. The disposition is stable, the emotions are appropriate, and one might even say that a form of habituation has occurred — the pill has habituated the neural pathways. Yet no one would wish to say that the person has thereby acquired the virtue of generosity. What the background cases possess and the pharmacological case lacks is the element of voluntary, effortful self-formation — the slow and sometimes painful process by which the agent shapes his own character through choice. It appears that the definition, by requiring habituation without specifying that it be the agent's own deliberate achievement, cannot distinguish virtues that are genuinely earned from those that are merely installed.

background_cases: In Plato's Republic, the just city assigns to each citizen the role for which his nature best suits him; the guardian guards, the craftsman crafts, and justice consists in each part performing its proper function without encroaching upon the functions of others.

Aristotle's account of distributive justice in the Nicomachean Ethics holds that goods are to be distributed in proportion to merit, where merit is understood relative to the purpose of the distribution; political office is distributed according to political virtue, not wealth.

The medieval just price doctrine held that a fair exchange is one in which the price reflects the intrinsic worth of the good and the labour required to produce it, so that neither party is enriched at the other's expense through the exploitation of necessity or ignorance.
definition: A distribution of goods D is just if and only if (i) D allocates goods according to a principle that the affected parties would accept as fair, (ii) D treats relevantly similar cases alike, and (iii) D does not result from force, fraud, or manipulation.
conditions: D allocates goods according to a principle that the affected parties would accept as fair
D treats relevantly similar cases alike
D does not result from force, fraud, or manipulation
counterexample: Consider a society that distributes all medical resources by lottery. The lottery principle is one that the affected parties accept as fair — everyone has an equal chance. Relevantly similar cases are treated alike — all persons, regardless of need, receive the same probability of treatment. No force or fraud is involved. All three conditions are met. Yet suppose that under this system, a person with a trivial ailment receives treatment while a person with a life-threatening condition does not. One might observe that the distribution, though procedurally fair, is substantively unjust, for it ignores the morally relevant difference in the urgency of need.
abductive_insight: The background cases reveal that paradigmatic conceptions of justice do not merely require procedural fairness and consistency but embed a substantive criterion of relevance — fitness for function in Plato, merit relative to purpose in Aristotle, intrinsic worth and labour in the just price tradition. The lottery satisfies procedural conditions but lacks any substantive principle that tracks the morally relevant features of the situation. What distinguishes genuine justice from mere procedural fairness is the presence of a distributive criterion that is sensitive to the relevant differences among the persons and circumstances involved.
passage:
When one surveys the most influential accounts of distributive justice in the philosophical tradition, a common feature emerges that transcends mere procedural regularity. Plato's just city assigns roles according to natural aptitude. Aristotle distributes goods in proportion to the merit relevant to the purpose of the distribution. The medieval doctrine of the just price requires that exchanges reflect the intrinsic worth of goods and the labour embodied in them. In each case, the distributive principle is substantively connected to a feature of the objects or persons involved — aptitude, merit, worth.

One might attempt to characterise justice more abstractly: a distribution is just if it follows a principle the affected parties would accept as fair, treats similar cases alike, and does not result from force, fraud, or manipulation. This characterisation captures the procedural and formal dimensions of justice. Yet a case which reveals its insufficiency arises when one considers a purely random distribution of medical resources. Suppose a society allocates hospital beds, surgical procedures, and medicines by lottery. Every citizen has an equal chance. Relevantly similar cases — in the sense that all citizens are treated identically — are treated alike. No force or fraud is employed. The principle of random allocation is accepted by the affected parties as fair, since no one is favoured. Yet under this system, a person with a minor bruise may receive a hospital bed while a person with a ruptured appendix is turned away. The distribution, though procedurally impeccable, is substantively unjust. What the background cases possess and the lottery lacks is a distributive criterion sensitive to the morally relevant differences among the recipients — their needs, their merits, their capacities. It appears that the definition, by attending only to procedural fairness and formal consistency, cannot distinguish just distributions from those that are merely impartial.

background_cases: A physician who encounters an injured stranger by the roadside is widely regarded as having a moral obligation to render assistance, for the physician possesses special skills that equip him to prevent serious harm at relatively little cost to himself.

A lifeguard stationed at a public beach is regarded as having a moral obligation to rescue a drowning swimmer, not merely because of the urgency of the situation but because the lifeguard has undertaken a specific role that creates a duty of rescue.

A parent is regarded as having a moral obligation to feed and shelter her child, not simply because the child will suffer without provision but because the parent stands in a special relationship of care and dependence that generates duties beyond those owed to strangers.
definition: An agent A has a moral obligation to perform action X if and only if (i) failure to perform X would result in serious harm to another, (ii) performing X is within A's power, and (iii) A can perform X without incurring a disproportionate sacrifice.
conditions: Failure to perform X would result in serious harm to another
Performing X is within A's power
A can perform X without incurring a disproportionate sacrifice
counterexample: Consider a wealthy bystander who witnesses a stranger's house engulfed in flames. A fire brigade has been summoned and will arrive shortly. The bystander could contribute money toward rebuilding costs — an action well within his power that would impose no disproportionate sacrifice. Failure to contribute will result in the stranger's financial ruin, which is a serious harm. All three conditions are met. Yet one might observe that the bystander, having no special relationship to the stranger and no particular role that creates a duty of assistance, does not have a moral obligation to contribute — however praiseworthy such generosity might be.
abductive_insight: The background cases share a feature absent from the bystander case: in each, the agent stands in a special relationship to the person in need — the physician possesses relevant expertise, the lifeguard occupies a defined role, the parent bears a relationship of care. These relationships generate obligations that go beyond what is owed to any stranger. The definition captures the conditions under which assistance is possible and not unduly costly but cannot distinguish obligatory assistance from supererogatory generosity, because it omits the relational ground from which genuine obligations spring.
passage:
When one examines the cases in which moral obligation is most clearly present, a pattern emerges that the standard formulations do not fully capture. The physician who stops to help an injured stranger acts from a duty grounded in his special competence. The lifeguard who dives into the surf acts from a duty grounded in his professional role. The parent who feeds her child acts from a duty grounded in the relationship of dependency. In each case, the obligation arises not merely from the possibility of preventing harm at low cost but from a special connection between the agent and the person in need.

One might attempt to define moral obligation as follows: an agent has a moral obligation to act when failure to act would produce serious harm, the act is within the agent's power, and performing it would not require a disproportionate sacrifice. This definition captures the consequentialist dimension of obligation — the importance of preventing harm — and the fairness dimension — the requirement that the sacrifice not be excessive. Yet a case which reveals its insufficiency arises with a wealthy bystander who witnesses a stranger's house in flames. The fire brigade is en route. The bystander could contribute generously toward the cost of rebuilding, an act well within his means and imposing no disproportionate burden. The stranger's financial ruin would constitute a serious harm. All three conditions are met. Yet one might observe that the bystander, who has no special relationship to the stranger, is not under a moral obligation to contribute, however admirable such generosity might be. What the background cases possess and the bystander case lacks is a relational ground — expertise, role, or bond of dependence — that transforms the possibility of beneficent action into a genuine obligation. It appears that the definition, by attending only to the possibility and cost of preventing harm, cannot distinguish what is morally obligatory from what is merely morally commendable.

background_cases: The punishment of a thief by imprisonment is widely regarded as justified because the thief acted with full awareness that theft was prohibited and chose to violate the norm nonetheless; the punishment responds to a genuine exercise of wrongful choice.

The punishment of a fraudster who deliberately deceives his victims is regarded as justified because the fraud was the product of calculated intention; the wrongdoer planned the deception and executed it with clear understanding of its wrongfulness.

The punishment of a person who recklessly drives at dangerous speed and causes an accident is regarded as justified because, although the harm may not have been intended, the driver consciously chose to take an unreasonable risk with others' safety.
definition: The punishment of a person P for an act A is morally justified if and only if (i) A violates a legitimate moral norm, (ii) P performed A voluntarily, and (iii) the punishment is proportionate to the gravity of A.
conditions: A violates a legitimate moral norm
P performed A voluntarily
The punishment is proportionate to the gravity of A
counterexample: Consider a person living with a severe compulsive disorder who takes items from shops. The person acts voluntarily in the narrow sense — no one physically compels him — and the act violates a legitimate moral norm. A proportionate fine is imposed. All three conditions are satisfied. Yet one might observe that the person's compulsion robs him of the capacity for genuine choice; he experiences an irresistible urge that he cannot overcome despite sincere and repeated effort.
abductive_insight: The background cases reveal that justified punishment characteristically responds to an act that springs from the agent's genuine capacity for rational self-governance — the thief chose freely, the fraudster planned deliberately, the reckless driver consciously accepted a risk. The compulsive shoplifter acts voluntarily in the thin sense of not being physically restrained, but lacks the deeper capacity for rational control that made the background agents genuinely culpable. The definition captures voluntariness but not the richer notion of rational self-governance that the paradigmatic cases presuppose.
passage:
When one considers the cases in which punishment is most clearly justified, a common feature stands out. The thief who takes another's property acts from a genuine exercise of choice — he weighs the risks, decides to proceed, and is therefore appropriately held to account. The fraudster who constructs an elaborate deception acts with calculated intention — every step is planned and executed with full awareness. The reckless driver who speeds through a crowded street consciously accepts an unreasonable risk. In each paradigmatic case, the wrongdoer exercises a capacity for rational deliberation and choice that grounds his culpability.

One might codify this by defining justified punishment as follows: punishment is morally warranted when the act violates a legitimate moral norm, was performed voluntarily, and the punishment is proportionate. This definition captures the retributive structure: a norm is broken, a choice is made, and a proportionate response is given. Yet a case which reveals its insufficiency involves a person who has a severe compulsive disorder who takes items from shops. He walks into a shop, picks up an item, and leaves without paying — all without physical compulsion. In the narrow sense, his act is voluntary. The norm against theft is legitimate. A proportionate penalty is imposed. All three conditions are met. Yet one might observe that this person's 'choice' is qualitatively different from the thief's or the fraudster's. The compulsion strips him of the capacity for rational self-governance that the paradigmatic cases presuppose. He does not weigh risks and decide; he is driven by an urge he cannot master. What the background cases possess and the compulsive case lacks is not mere voluntariness but the deeper capacity for rational self-direction. It appears that the definition, by requiring only voluntariness in the thin sense, cannot distinguish those who deserve punishment from those who need treatment.

background_cases: A physician who treats a poor patient without charge, knowing that she will receive no payment and no public recognition, acts from a desire to relieve suffering; we regard her action as benevolent.
A farmer who shares his harvest with a neighbouring family struck by misfortune, expecting nothing in return, acts from sympathy; we regard his generosity as benevolent.
A stranger who stops to help a traveller whose carriage has overturned on a lonely road, at some inconvenience to himself, acts from spontaneous goodwill; we regard the act as benevolent.
definition: An action is benevolent if and only if (i) the agent performs the action with the intention of benefiting another, (ii) the action produces a benefit for the intended recipient, and (iii) the agent performs the action freely, without coercion or compulsion.
conditions: The agent performs the action with the intention of benefiting another
The action produces a benefit for the intended recipient
The agent performs the action freely, without coercion or compulsion
counterexample: A merchant gives food to a starving man at his door. The action is free, intended to benefit the man, and does benefit him. All three conditions are met. Yet the merchant acts solely because a crowd has gathered and he fears that refusing will damage his commercial reputation. His motive is entirely self-protective. One might observe that while the starving man has been helped, the merchant's action lacks the quality we associate with genuine benevolence.
abductive_insight: The background cases share a feature absent from the merchant's case: in each, the agent acts from a motive of genuine concern for the welfare of the other person — the physician from sympathy, the farmer from fellow-feeling, the stranger from spontaneous goodwill. The merchant acts from calculated self-interest. The definition captures the outward structure of benevolent action — free, intentional, beneficial — but omits the motivational quality that distinguishes genuinely benevolent acts from those performed for self-interested reasons that happen to benefit another.
passage:
When one surveys the cases in which we most readily ascribe benevolence, a pattern emerges that goes beyond the mere production of benefit by a free agent. The physician who treats a poor patient without charge acts from sympathy for suffering. The farmer who shares his harvest with a stricken neighbour acts from fellow-feeling. The stranger who stops to assist an overturned carriage acts from spontaneous goodwill. In each case, the agent's motive is directed towards the welfare of the other rather than towards his own advantage.

One might attempt to codify this pattern: an action is benevolent if the agent acts freely, intends to benefit another, and succeeds in doing so. This characterisation captures the structural features common to the background cases. Yet a case which reveals its insufficiency arises with a merchant who gives food to a starving man at his door, not from any concern for the man's welfare but solely because a crowd of onlookers has gathered and a refusal would injure his commercial reputation. The action is free, the intention is to benefit the man (at least instrumentally), and the man is in fact benefited. All three conditions are met. Yet one might observe that the merchant's action, however helpful in its effect, differs in an important respect from the physician's or the farmer's. What the background cases possess and the merchant's case lacks is a motive of genuine concern for the welfare of the recipient. It appears that the definition, by attending only to the freedom, intentionality, and beneficial effect of the action, cannot distinguish between benevolence and calculated self-interest that happens to produce a benefit.

background_cases: A boy who pulls the wings from a fly and watches it struggle, deriving amusement from its suffering, acts cruelly; the cruelty lies in the gratuitous infliction of pain for its own sake.
A master who beats his servant for a trivial offence, far exceeding any corrective purpose, acts cruelly; the cruelty lies in the disproportionate severity applied to a powerless victim.

A judge in a despotic regime who sentences a prisoner to prolonged humiliation before a public audience, intending to degrade rather than to punish, acts cruelly; the cruelty lies in the deliberate destruction of human dignity.
definition: An action is cruel if and only if (i) the action causes suffering to a sentient being, (ii) the agent is aware that the action causes suffering, and (iii) the suffering serves no legitimate purpose.
conditions: The action causes suffering to a sentient being
The agent is aware that the action causes suffering
The suffering serves no legitimate purpose
counterexample: A bureaucrat administers a regulation that he is aware causes considerable suffering to those affected — denying relief to families in desperate need. The suffering serves no legitimate purpose; the regulation is an outdated formality that no one has troubled to repeal. All three conditions are met. Yet the bureaucrat acts from indifferent compliance with procedure rather than from any impulse to cause pain. One might observe that while the suffering is real and purposeless, the bureaucrat's action lacks the quality of wilful disregard or positive satisfaction in suffering that we associate with cruelty.
abductive_insight: The background cases share a feature absent from the bureaucrat's case: in each, the agent stands in a direct relationship to the suffering, either taking pleasure in it (the boy), inflicting it in excess of any justification (the master), or deliberately instrumentalising it (the judge). The bureaucrat's relationship to the suffering is mediated by institutional procedure — he neither enjoys it nor deliberately inflicts it but merely fails to prevent it. The definition captures the objective conditions — suffering, awareness, absence of legitimate purpose — but cannot distinguish between the active, engaged infliction of suffering that characterises cruelty and the passive, indifferent perpetuation of it through institutional inertia.
passage:
When one examines the cases in which the charge of cruelty is most clearly warranted, a pattern emerges that goes beyond the mere knowing production of purposeless suffering. The boy who tears the wings from a fly takes a direct pleasure in the creature's distress. The master who beats his servant beyond any corrective need inflicts pain that is grossly disproportionate to the occasion. The despotic judge who humiliates a prisoner before an audience deliberately degrades another human being. In each case, the agent stands in an active, engaged relationship to the suffering: he either enjoys it, deliberately amplifies it, or instrumentalises it for the purpose of degradation.

One might attempt to capture this pattern with the following definition: an action is cruel if it causes suffering to a sentient being, the agent is aware of the suffering, and the suffering serves no legitimate purpose. This characterisation captures the objective features common to the background cases. Yet a case which reveals its insufficiency arises in the domain of bureaucratic administration. Suppose a civil servant administers an outdated regulation that denies relief to families in desperate need. He is aware that the regulation causes suffering. The suffering serves no legitimate purpose — the regulation is an anachronism. All three conditions are met. Yet the civil servant does not delight in the suffering, nor does he deliberately amplify it; he merely follows procedure with indifferent compliance. One might observe that while his conduct is objectionable, it lacks the quality that distinguishes cruelty from mere callousness or negligence. What the background cases possess and the bureaucrat's case lacks is an active, engaged relationship to the suffering — whether through pleasure, disproportionate infliction, or deliberate degradation. It appears that the definition, by attending only to the objective conditions, cannot distinguish cruelty proper from the passive perpetuation of suffering through institutional indifference.

background_cases: A soldier who remains with his regiment through a disastrous retreat, enduring hardship and danger rather than deserting, is regarded as loyal; his loyalty consists in steadfastness under extreme adversity.

A friend who defends an absent companion against unjust gossip, knowing that the defence may cost him social standing, is regarded as loyal; his loyalty consists in maintaining allegiance when it is costly to do so.

A servant who declines a more lucrative position elsewhere in order to remain with an ageing master who needs his care is regarded as loyal; his loyalty consists in placing the relationship above personal advantage.
definition: A person P is loyal to Q if and only if (i) P maintains allegiance to Q over time, (ii) P does not betray Q's trust, and (iii) P acts in Q's interest even when doing so is contrary to P's own interest.
conditions: P maintains allegiance to Q over time
P does not betray Q's trust
P acts in Q's interest even when contrary to P's own interest
counterexample: Consider a person who maintains allegiance to a business partner over many years, never betrays the partner's trust, and consistently acts in the partner's interest at personal cost. All three conditions are met. Yet suppose the man's motive is entirely strategic: he has calculated that the partnership will eventually yield him a fortune, and his apparent sacrifices are investments in future gain. Should the calculation change, he would abandon the partner without hesitation. One might observe that this is not loyalty but a simulation of loyalty sustained by self-interest.
abductive_insight: The background cases share a feature absent from the calculating partner's case: in each, the agent's allegiance is sustained by a genuine attachment to the person or institution — the soldier values his regiment, the friend values the absent companion, the servant values the ageing master. The calculating partner values only the expected return. The definition captures the behavioural pattern of loyalty — steadfastness, fidelity, self-sacrifice — but omits the motivational ground that makes the pattern an expression of loyalty rather than a strategic imitation of it.
passage:
When one considers the cases in which loyalty is most clearly displayed, a common feature emerges that transcends mere behavioural regularity. The soldier who endures a disastrous retreat rather than deserting does not merely remain — he remains because he values his regiment and his comrades. The friend who defends an absent companion against gossip does not merely speak — he speaks because the relationship matters to him. The servant who declines a better position does not merely stay — he stays because he is attached to his master's welfare. In each case, the behaviour of steadfastness and self-sacrifice is sustained by a genuine concern for the object of loyalty.

One might attempt to codify this pattern: a person is loyal when he maintains allegiance over time, does not betray trust, and acts in the other's interest even at personal cost. This characterisation captures the external manifestations of loyalty. Yet a case which reveals its insufficiency arises when one considers the calculating partner. Suppose a man maintains unwavering allegiance to a business associate, never betrays his trust, and regularly acts against his own short-term interest to further the associate's projects. All three conditions are met. Yet the man's motive is entirely strategic: he has determined that the partnership will eventually yield immense personal gain, and every apparent sacrifice is an investment. Should circumstances change, he would abandon the association without a moment's regret. One might observe that this is not loyalty but its calculating counterfeit. What the background cases possess and the strategic partner lacks is a genuine attachment to the other party — a valuation of the relationship that is not reducible to expected personal advantage. It appears that the definition, by specifying only the pattern of conduct, cannot distinguish loyalty from its strategic simulation.

background_cases: A firefighter who enters a burning building to rescue a trapped child, knowing the risks, is regarded as courageous; his courage serves the preservation of innocent life.
A whistle-blower who reports corruption within a powerful institution, knowing that she will face retaliation, is regarded as courageous; her courage serves the cause of honest dealing.
A physician who volunteers to treat patients during a plague, knowing that he may contract the disease, is regarded as courageous; his courage serves the relief of suffering.
definition: An action is courageous if and only if (i) the agent faces a significant and recognised danger, (ii) the agent acts despite the danger rather than retreating from it, and (iii) the action is voluntary.
conditions: The agent faces a significant and recognised danger
The agent acts despite the danger rather than retreating from it
The action is voluntary
counterexample: A man, for the amusement of onlookers, voluntarily swims across a river known to be infested with dangerous currents. He faces a significant danger, acts despite it, and does so voluntarily. All three conditions are met. Yet one might observe that this is recklessness rather than courage, for the risk is borne not for any end worthy of the danger but merely for entertainment.
abductive_insight: The background cases share a feature absent from the swimmer's case: in each, the danger is confronted in the service of a purpose that is commensurate with the risk — the firefighter risks his life to save a child, the whistle-blower risks her career to expose corruption, the physician risks his health to relieve suffering. The swimmer risks his life for entertainment. The definition captures the confrontation with danger but not the purposiveness that elevates such confrontation from recklessness to courage. What distinguishes the background cases from the swimmer is the presence of an end worthy of the risk undertaken.
passage:
When one examines the paradigmatic cases of courage, a feature emerges that goes beyond the mere voluntary confrontation with danger. The firefighter who enters a burning building risks his life to save a child. The whistle-blower who reports corruption risks her livelihood to serve honest dealing. The physician who treats plague patients risks his health to relieve suffering. In each case, the danger is confronted not for its own sake but in the service of a purpose that is, in the judgement of the moral community, commensurate with the risk.

One might attempt to characterise courage by requiring that the agent face a significant danger, act despite it, and do so voluntarily. This definition captures the structural features common to the background cases. Yet a case which reveals its insufficiency arises with a person who, for the entertainment of onlookers, voluntarily swims across a stretch of river known to harbour dangerous currents. He recognises the danger and acts despite it. His action is voluntary — no one compels him. All three conditions are satisfied. Yet one might observe that we do not commend this as courage but condemn it as recklessness. The firefighter's entry into the burning building and the swimmer's plunge into the river share the same structural features — danger, confrontation, voluntariness — but differ decisively in purpose. What the background cases possess and the swimmer's case lacks is an end that justifies the risk. It appears that the definition, by attending only to the relation between the agent and the danger, cannot distinguish the courageous from the merely reckless, and that a further condition — that the danger be confronted for a purpose worthy of the risk — is required.

background_cases: When a physician treats a patient through the night, remaining at the bedside despite exhaustion, we recognise her conduct as the fulfilment of duty not merely because she is present but because her sustained attention and responsive care reflect an unbroken exercise of professional agency — she monitors, adjusts, and decides throughout the vigil.

When a soldier holds a defensive position under bombardment, his fulfilment of duty consists not in the mere fact of his physical presence at the post but in his continued readiness to act — to fire, to signal, to relay information — sustained by his own volition through the duration of the engagement.

When a lighthouse keeper maintains the light through a storm, his duty is fulfilled not by the lamp's burning alone but by his active tending of the mechanism — trimming the wick, clearing the lens, ensuring the rotation — so that the light serves its navigational purpose throughout the night.
definition: An action A performed by an agent S constitutes the fulfilment of a duty if and only if (i) S is under an obligation to perform A, (ii) S performs A voluntarily, and (iii) S performs A because S recognises the obligation to do so.
conditions: S is under an obligation to perform A
S performs A voluntarily
S performs A because S recognises the obligation to do so
counterexample: Consider a night watchman who recognises his obligation to guard a warehouse and voluntarily takes up his position. Shortly thereafter, he is overcome by fumes from a chemical leak and rendered unconscious for the remainder of his shift. A colleague, unaware of his condition, props him in his chair so that he appears to be on duty. All three conditions are formally satisfied by the initial act, yet no duty of vigilance has been fulfilled.
abductive_insight: What the background cases share, and what the definition fails to require, is that the agent's own volition and responsive agency be sustained throughout the period of obligation — that duty be fulfilled not by a single initiating act but by the continuous exercise of the capacities that the obligation demands.
passage:
The cases in which we most readily recognise the fulfilment of duty share a common structure that repays careful examination. When a physician tends a patient through the night, her dutiful conduct consists not in the single act of arriving at the bedside but in the sustained exercise of medical judgement — monitoring vital signs, adjusting dosages, responding to changes in the patient's condition — that continues until her watch is over. When a soldier holds a position under fire, his duty is fulfilled not by his mere physical presence but by his continued readiness to act in defence of the position. When a lighthouse keeper maintains the light through a storm, it is his active tending of the mechanism, not the lamp's passive burning, that constitutes the performance of his obligation.

These cases invite the formulation of a principle: duty is fulfilled when an agent, recognising an obligation, voluntarily performs the required action from a sense of that obligation. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this formulation may be constructed as follows. Suppose a night watchman, fully aware of his obligation, voluntarily takes up his position at a warehouse. Within the hour, he is rendered unconscious by fumes from a chemical leak in an adjacent building. A well-meaning colleague, discovering him slumped at his post, props him upright in his chair so that he appears to be on duty. The watchman remains at his post for the duration of his shift, but he guards nothing — he cannot see, hear, or respond. The initial conditions of the definition are satisfied: he was under an obligation, he took up his position voluntarily, and he did so because he recognised his duty. Yet it would be a distortion of the concept to say that the duty was fulfilled. One might observe that what the background cases share is the sustained exercise of responsive agency throughout the period of obligation. The definition, by attending only to the initiation of the action, fails to require that the agent's own capacities remain engaged for the duration that duty demands.

background_cases: When a firefighter enters a burning building to rescue a trapped child, we call her courageous not merely because she faces danger for a noble purpose but because she acts against the natural pull of fear — she feels the heat, recognises the risk of collapse, and nevertheless presses forward, mastering her apprehension through an act of will.

When a whistleblower exposes corruption within a powerful institution, knowing that retaliation is likely, his courage consists not in the bare fact of risk-taking but in the inner struggle he undergoes — the anxiety about his career, the dread of ostracism — and his deliberate decision to act despite these feelings.

When a civilian in wartime shelters persecuted strangers at risk to herself and her family, her courage lies in the fact that she feels the danger keenly — the terror of discovery, the imagined consequences — and yet chooses to act in accordance with her moral convictions.
definition: An agent S displays courage in performing an action A if and only if (i) A involves a significant risk of harm to S, (ii) S is aware of the risk, and (iii) S performs A despite the risk in pursuit of a worthy end.
conditions: A involves a significant risk of harm to S
S is aware of the risk
S performs A despite the risk in pursuit of a worthy end
counterexample: Consider a test pilot who volunteers to fly an experimental aircraft under conditions of extreme danger, fully aware that the probability of a fatal malfunction is high, and motivated by the worthy end of advancing aviation safety. Yet the pilot has a congenital neurological condition that renders him incapable of experiencing fear or anxiety. He approaches the flight with the same calm detachment with which he might read a newspaper. All three conditions are met, but the performance lacks the inner dimension we associate with courage.
abductive_insight: What the background cases share is not merely the presence of risk and noble purpose but the experience of fear or apprehension and its deliberate mastery — courage involves an inner struggle between the pull of self-preservation and the demands of a worthy end, and the definition's silence on this affective dimension leaves it unable to distinguish true courage from constitutional fearlessness.

A pattern emerges when one examines the cases that most compellingly exemplify courage. The firefighter who enters a burning building feels the heat upon her face and the instinctive urge to retreat, yet masters that impulse and presses forward. The whistleblower who exposes institutional corruption lies awake at night, anxious about the consequences, yet resolves to speak. The civilian who shelters the persecuted during wartime feels a visceral terror at the thought of discovery, yet opens her door. In each case, the agent is aware of a significant risk, pursues a worthy end, and — crucially — experiences a natural aversion to the danger that she must overcome through an exercise of will.

These cases invite the formulation of a general principle: an agent displays courage when she knowingly undertakes a risky action in pursuit of a worthy end. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this formulation may be imagined without extravagance. Suppose a test pilot volunteers to fly an experimental aircraft under conditions of extreme peril. He is fully aware that the probability of a fatal malfunction is substantial. His purpose is admirable: the flight will generate data essential to the safety of future aviation. However, owing to a congenital neurological condition, he is entirely incapable of experiencing fear. The prospect of death produces in him no more discomfort than the prospect of a routine sortie. He does not struggle against an impulse to withdraw; he feels no anxiety to be governed by reason. All three conditions of the definition are satisfied, yet one might observe that something essential to courage is absent. What the background cases share — and what this case conspicuously lacks — is the experience of fear and its deliberate mastery. It appears that the definition, by attending only to risk, awareness, and purpose, fails to require the affective dimension that distinguishes courage from mere fearlessness.

background_cases: When a village distributes its communal harvest, the distribution is regarded as just not merely because each family receives a share proportionate to its labour, but because every family's agreement to the arrangement was freely given — no family was coerced into accepting less than its due, and each had a genuine opportunity to contest the terms.

When a partnership dissolves and its assets are divided, the division is considered just not merely because the proportions reflect each partner's contribution, but because the agreement governing the division was entered into without duress — each partner negotiated freely and with full information.

When a cooperative allocates profits among its members, the allocation is regarded as just not only because it is proportionate to each member's work, but because the rules governing the allocation were adopted through a democratic process in which each member participated voluntarily and without intimidation.
definition: A distribution D of goods among a group of persons is just if and only if (i) D accords to each person what is proportionate to that person's contribution, (ii) the procedure by which D is determined is impartial, and (iii) no person is left without the means to satisfy basic needs.
conditions: D accords to each person what is proportionate to that person's contribution
The procedure by which D is determined is impartial
No person is left without the means to satisfy basic needs
counterexample: Consider a mining cooperative in which the ore is divided strictly in proportion to each miner's output, by an impartial committee, with a guaranteed minimum for every worker. One miner, however, has been threatened by a foreman and coerced into signing over a portion of his share. The committee, unaware of the coercion, processes the transfer as a voluntary reallocation. All three conditions of the definition are satisfied, yet the distribution is manifestly unjust.
abductive_insight: What the background cases share is that each participant's acceptance of the distributive arrangement was freely and voluntarily given — the definition captures proportionality, impartiality, and a humanitarian minimum, but it fails to require that the terms upon which each person participates be uncoerced, leaving it unable to distinguish genuine fairness from the mere appearance of fairness imposed by duress.

When one examines the distributions that we most readily call just, a pattern emerges that extends beyond proportionality and procedural fairness. In the village that divides its communal harvest, the justice of the arrangement rests not only upon each family's receiving a fair share but upon the fact that every family consented to the terms without coercion. In the dissolved partnership, the division of assets is just because the partners negotiated freely, with full information, and without intimidation. In the cooperative that allocates profits, the allocation is just because the governing rules were adopted through a process in which each member participated voluntarily. In every case, the absence of coercion is a condition upon which the justice of the distribution depends.

These cases invite the formulation of a general principle: a distribution is just when it is proportionate to contribution, determined by impartial procedure, and ensures that basic needs are met. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this formulation may be constructed from the domain of labour. Suppose a mining cooperative distributes its ore in strict proportion to each miner's output, with an impartial committee overseeing the process and a guaranteed minimum for every worker. One miner, however, has been privately threatened by a foreman and coerced into signing a document transferring a portion of his share. The committee, unaware of the coercion, records the reduced allocation as consistent with the miner's stated agreement. All three conditions of the definition are met: the distribution is proportionate to stated contributions, the procedure is impartial, and the minimum is preserved. Yet the distribution is plainly unjust. One might observe that what the background cases share — and what this case conspicuously lacks — is the voluntary and uncoerced character of each participant's acceptance. The definition, by attending to proportionality, impartiality, and need, fails to require that participation itself be free from duress.

background_cases: When a teacher spends her evenings tutoring a struggling pupil without compensation, her benevolence is recognised not merely because she intends to help and succeeds in doing so, but because her concern is directed toward the pupil's own good — she seeks the pupil's flourishing for the pupil's sake, not as a means to any private satisfaction.

When a neighbour quietly pays the medical expenses of an ailing widow, his benevolence lies not only in the intention and the effect but in the fact that the widow's welfare is his genuine end — he does not regard her as an instrument through which to achieve some unrecognised personal aim.

When a stranger rescues a child from a river at considerable personal risk, we call the act benevolent because the stranger's concern is for the child as an end — the child's safety is the object of the action, not a vehicle for the stranger's self-regard.
definition: An action A performed by an agent S is benevolent if and only if (i) A is intended to promote the well-being of another person T, (ii) S acts without expectation of reciprocal benefit, and (iii) A does in fact tend to promote the well-being of T.
conditions: A is intended to promote the well-being of another person T
S acts without expectation of reciprocal benefit
A does in fact tend to promote the well-being of T
counterexample: Consider a retired musician who anonymously funds a scholarship for a young violinist. He expects nothing in return and sincerely intends to advance the student's career. The scholarship does, in fact, enable the student to attend a distinguished conservatoire. Yet the patron's driving motive — largely unconscious — is to recreate in the student a version of his own unrealised career, shaping the student's choices to mirror the path he wished he had taken. The student is, in effect, a proxy for the patron's unfulfilled aspirations. All three conditions are satisfied, but the action has an instrumentalising quality that sits uneasily with genuine benevolence.
abductive_insight: What the background cases share is that the beneficiary is regarded as an end in her own right — the agent's concern is directed toward the other's good for the other's sake. The definition captures intention, disinterestedness, and efficacy, but it fails to require that the beneficiary not be treated as a vehicle for the agent's own unrecognised psychological needs.

The cases we most readily identify as benevolent share a feature that extends beyond intention and effect. The teacher who tutors a struggling pupil without compensation acts not merely with the intention of helping but with a concern directed toward the pupil's own flourishing — the pupil's good is the end, not a means. The neighbour who pays a widow's medical expenses regards the widow's welfare as his genuine object, not as an occasion for self-congratulation. The stranger who rescues a child from a river is moved by concern for the child as a person whose safety matters in its own right.

These cases invite a general characterisation: an action is benevolent when it is intended to promote another's well-being, performed without expectation of return, and genuinely conducive to that end. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this characterisation may be imagined as follows. Suppose a retired musician anonymously funds a scholarship for a young violinist. He expects no reciprocation and sincerely believes himself to be acting for the student's benefit. However, the patron's motivating impulse — of which he is only dimly aware — is to recreate in the student a version of his own unrealised musical career. He steers the scholarship's conditions to favour the repertoire he wished he had mastered, the teachers he wished he had studied under, the trajectory he wished he had followed. The student prospers, but as an instrument of the patron's unresolved ambitions. All three conditions of the definition are met, yet one might observe that something essential to benevolence is absent. What the background cases share — and what this case lacks — is that the beneficiary is regarded as an end in her own right. The definition, by attending to intention, disinterestedness, and efficacy, fails to require that the other's good be pursued for the other's sake, rather than as a means to the agent's unrecognised purposes.

background_cases: When we praise the soldier who advances under fire to rescue a wounded comrade, our admiration attaches not merely to the fact that he acts in the face of danger but to the fact that he does so for the sake of something genuinely worth the risk — the life of a fellow human being; a person who charged into gunfire merely to retrieve a dropped coin would not be called courageous, however dangerous his act.

When we commend the whistleblower who risks her career to expose corruption, the praise is directed not at the mere willingness to face professional ruin but at the recognition that the public good at stake warranted the sacrifice; an employee who courted dismissal by violating trivial office rules for no significant purpose would not earn our moral approbation.

When we honour the physician who volunteers to treat patients during a plague, we do so not merely because she exposes herself to contagion but because she grasps that the alleviation of suffering is a good of sufficient weight to justify the hazard; a physician who exposed herself to disease for no reason beyond the thrill of danger would strike us as reckless rather than courageous.
definition: A person S possesses the virtue of courage if and only if (i) S regularly acts in the face of danger, (ii) S acts from a settled disposition rather than from momentary impulse, and (iii) S's actions in the face of danger are voluntary.
conditions: S regularly acts in the face of danger
S acts from a settled disposition rather than from momentary impulse
S's actions in the face of danger are voluntary
counterexample: Consider a professional base-jumper who regularly leaps from buildings and bridges, does so from a settled and practised disposition, and acts with full voluntariness. All three conditions are satisfied. Yet the base-jumper courts danger purely for the excitement it affords and for the admiration of spectators; she is wholly indifferent to whether any good of moral significance is served by her actions. We should hesitate to call her courageous in the morally commendable sense.
abductive_insight: What the background cases share, and what the definition fails to require, is that the agent's willingness to face danger be governed by a correct apprehension of what is genuinely worth risking harm for — that the danger be undertaken because something of real moral importance is at stake, not merely from appetite for excitement or indifference to risk.

The cases in which we most readily attribute courage reveal a common pattern that rewards examination. When we praise the soldier who advances under fire to rescue a wounded comrade, our admiration attaches not merely to the fact that he acts in the face of danger but to the circumstance that he does so for the sake of something genuinely worth the risk — the life of a fellow human being. A person who charged into gunfire merely to retrieve a dropped coin would not, however bold, be called courageous. When we commend the whistleblower who risks her livelihood to expose corruption, the praise is directed not at the bare willingness to face professional ruin but at her recognition that the public good at stake warranted the sacrifice. When we honour the physician who volunteers to treat patients during a plague, we do so because she grasps that the alleviation of suffering is a good of sufficient weight to justify the hazard she assumes.

These cases invite the formulation of a general account: courage is a settled, voluntary disposition to act in the face of danger. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this characterisation may be drawn from the world of extreme sport. Consider a professional base-jumper who regularly leaps from buildings and bridges, does so from a disposition cultivated over many years, and acts with complete voluntariness. All three conditions are satisfied. Yet the base-jumper courts danger purely for the excitement it affords and for the applause of onlookers; she is wholly indifferent to whether any morally significant good is served by her conduct. We should hesitate to call her courageous in the morally commendable sense. One might observe that what the background cases share, and what the definition omits, is the requirement that the agent's willingness to face danger be governed by a correct apprehension of what is genuinely worth risking harm for. The soldier, the whistleblower, and the physician each face danger because something of real moral importance demands it; the base-jumper faces danger for no such reason.

background_cases: When we regard as just the distribution of medical supplies to a community stricken by disease, the justice of the distribution appears to depend not merely on the procedure by which it was effected but on its responsiveness to the severity of the recipients' needs; a distribution that gave equal shares to the gravely ill and the perfectly healthy would strike us as deficient, however fair the procedure.

When we approve of a progressive taxation scheme that requires the wealthy to contribute a larger proportion of their income, the approval rests on the thought that the burden of maintaining public services ought to fall more heavily upon those who can most easily bear it; a flat levy that ignored disparities of wealth would seem unjust despite its procedural uniformity.

When we praise the allocation of university scholarships to students from disadvantaged backgrounds, the praise reflects the judgement that prior disadvantage constitutes a morally relevant consideration in the distribution of educational opportunity; a lottery that ignored all differences of background would not, by itself, be regarded as just.
definition: A distribution of goods among members of a society is just if and only if (i) the distribution was produced by a procedure to which all members consented, (ii) the procedure treated all members identically, and (iii) every member received an equal share.
conditions: The distribution was produced by a procedure to which all members consented
The procedure treated all members identically
Every member received an equal share
counterexample: Suppose a community unanimously consents to distribute a fixed supply of medicine equally among all its members, treating everyone identically. All three conditions are satisfied. Yet some members are gravely ill and need large doses to survive, while others are perfectly healthy and have no use for the medicine at all. The equal distribution leaves the sick without enough medicine to recover and wastes doses on the healthy. We should hesitate to call this distribution just.
abductive_insight: What the background cases share, and what the definition fails to require, is that a just distribution be responsive to the morally relevant differences among recipients — their needs, their capacities to benefit, or their prior disadvantages — rather than treating all persons identically regardless of their circumstances.

Reflection upon the distributions we most readily regard as just reveals a pattern that merits attention. When medical supplies are distributed to a community stricken by disease, the justice of the distribution appears to depend not merely on the fairness of the procedure but on its responsiveness to the severity of the recipients' needs. When a progressive taxation scheme requires the wealthy to contribute a larger share, its justness rests upon the thought that the burden ought to fall more heavily on those who can most easily bear it. When university scholarships are allocated to students from disadvantaged backgrounds, the approval reflects a judgement that prior disadvantage is a morally relevant consideration. In each case, the distribution is regarded as just precisely because it attends to differences among the recipients that are deemed morally significant.

These cases invite the formulation of a general account: a distribution is just when it is produced by a consented-to procedure that treats all members identically and yields equal shares. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this characterisation may be constructed from medical allocation. Suppose a community unanimously consents to divide a fixed supply of medicine equally, treating every member identically. All three conditions are met. Yet some members are gravely ill and require substantial doses to survive, whilst others are perfectly healthy and have no use for the medicine whatsoever. The equal distribution leaves the sick without enough medicine to recover and squanders doses upon those who derive no benefit from them. One might observe that what the background cases share, and what the definition omits, is the requirement that a just distribution be responsive to morally relevant differences among the recipients — their needs, their capacities to benefit, or the disadvantages they have previously suffered.

background_cases: When a strong swimmer sees a child drowning in a shallow pond and can rescue the child at no serious risk to herself, we regard her as morally obligated to act; yet our judgement rests not merely on her capacity to prevent harm but on the fact that the cost to her is trivially small in comparison to the magnitude of the harm she can prevent.

When a physician possesses the only available dose of a life-saving drug and can administer it to a dying patient at no cost beyond the drug itself, we say the physician is obligated to do so; yet if administering the drug would require the physician to sacrifice her own life, our judgement of obligation becomes far less certain.

When a passer-by can prevent a collision by shouting a warning at no cost to himself, the obligation seems clear; yet if preventing the collision would require him to leap in front of a moving vehicle, we should hesitate to say he is morally required to do so.
definition: A person S has a moral obligation to perform an action A if and only if (i) performing A would prevent harm to another person, (ii) S is aware that A would prevent such harm, and (iii) S has the physical capacity to perform A.
conditions: Performing A would prevent harm to another person
S is aware that A would prevent such harm
S has the physical capacity to perform A
counterexample: Suppose a man is aware that he could prevent a stranger from suffering a broken arm by interposing his own body, and he has the physical capacity to do so. All three conditions are met. Yet interposing his body would result in his own death, crushed by the falling object that threatens the stranger. We should hesitate to say that the man has a moral obligation to sacrifice his life to prevent another's broken arm.
abductive_insight: What the background cases share, and what the definition fails to require, is that the cost to the agent of performing the action not be grossly disproportionate to the harm prevented — that the sacrifice demanded of the agent be reasonable in light of the magnitude of the benefit conferred.
passage:
The cases in which we most confidently ascribe a moral obligation to prevent harm share a feature that the proposed definition does not capture. When a strong swimmer can rescue a drowning child from a shallow pond at no serious risk, the obligation seems indisputable; yet the judgement rests not merely on the swimmer's awareness and capacity but on the triviality of the cost relative to the magnitude of the harm prevented. When a physician can administer a life-saving drug at minimal expense, the obligation is clear; but were administering the drug to require the physician's own death, the judgement would falter. When a passer-by can prevent a collision by shouting a warning, the obligation is plain; but if prevention required him to throw himself before a moving vehicle, we should not say he was morally required to do so.

These cases invite an analysis of obligation in terms of awareness, capacity, and the prevention of harm. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this analysis may be readily constructed. Suppose a man is aware that he could prevent a stranger from suffering a broken arm by interposing his own body beneath a falling object, and he has the physical capacity to do so. All three conditions are met. Yet interposing his body would result in his own death. We should hesitate to say that the man is morally obligated to sacrifice his life to prevent another person's broken arm. One might observe that what the background cases share, and what the definition omits, is the requirement that the cost to the agent not be grossly disproportionate to the harm prevented. The obligation to prevent harm is not absolute; it is conditioned upon the reasonableness of the sacrifice demanded of the agent.

background_cases: When we regard as justified the imprisonment of a person who committed premeditated murder, our approval rests not merely on the severity of the crime and the authority of the court but on the assumption that the man acted with full awareness and deliberation, in circumstances where he could have chosen otherwise; had he been living with a psychotic episode that rendered him incapable of understanding his actions, our confidence in the justice of the punishment would be severely shaken.

When we accept as fair the fining of a merchant who knowingly sold contaminated food, our acceptance depends upon the assumption that the merchant acted with culpable awareness; had the contamination been introduced by a third party without the merchant's knowledge or any possibility of detection, the fine would seem unjust.

When we approve of the disciplining of a soldier who deserted his post, the approval presupposes that the soldier had a genuine choice; had he fled because enemy forces had overrun the position and remaining would have meant certain death with no tactical benefit, the punishment would appear disproportionate to his actual culpability.
definition: The infliction of a penalty P upon a person S is morally justified if and only if (i) S has committed a wrongful act, (ii) P is imposed by a competent authority, and (iii) P is proportionate to the gravity of the act.
conditions: S has committed a wrongful act
P is imposed by a competent authority
P is proportionate to the gravity of the act
counterexample: Suppose a woman takes food from a shop without paying — a wrongful act. A competent court imposes a fine proportionate to the offence. All three conditions are satisfied. Yet suppose the woman took the food because she and her children were starving, no charitable assistance was available, and stealing was the only means of preventing their deaths. Her culpability is so diminished by the extremity of her circumstances that the punishment, though proportionate to the abstract gravity of the offence, seems unjust when applied to this particular offender.
abductive_insight: What the background cases share, and what the definition fails to require, is that the offender's moral culpability be sufficient to warrant the punishment — that the conditions under which the wrongful act was committed be such as to sustain the degree of blame that the penalty presupposes, rather than merely that the act itself be wrongful.

The punishments we most readily regard as justified share a structural feature that repays examination. When we approve of the imprisonment of a premeditated murderer, our approval rests upon the assumption that the offender acted with full awareness and deliberation, in circumstances permitting a genuine choice. Had he been in the grip of a psychotic episode, our confidence in the justice of the punishment would be severely shaken. When we accept the fining of a merchant who knowingly sold contaminated food, the acceptance depends upon the merchant's culpable awareness; had the contamination been introduced without any possibility of detection, the fine would seem unjust. When we approve of disciplining a soldier who deserted, the approval presupposes a genuine choice; had remaining at his post meant certain death for no tactical gain, the punishment would appear disproportionate.

These cases suggest a general characterisation: punishment is justified when a wrongful act has been committed, a competent authority imposes a proportionate penalty, and the offender's culpability warrants the sanction. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of the proposed definition may be constructed from situations of extreme necessity. Suppose a woman takes food from a shop without paying. A competent court imposes a fine proportionate to the gravity of the theft. All three conditions are satisfied. Yet the woman stole because she and her children were starving, no charitable assistance was available, and theft was the sole means of preventing their deaths. Her moral culpability is so diminished by the extremity of her circumstances that the punishment, though proportionate in the abstract, seems unjust when applied to her. One might observe that what the background cases share, and what the definition omits, is the requirement that the offender's culpability be sufficient to warrant the punishment — that the conditions under which the act was committed sustain the degree of blame the penalty presupposes.

background_cases: When we admire the courage of the soldier who risks death to rescue a wounded comrade, our admiration is directed not merely at the beneficial outcome of the rescue but at the inner orientation of the agent — the willingness to face danger for the sake of another, held steadily in the face of powerful contrary impulses, and animated by a genuine concern for the comrade's welfare rather than by the prospect of decoration or advancement.

When we praise the temperance of a person who refrains from excessive drinking despite strong temptation, we praise not merely the outward behaviour of abstention but the inner mastery that sustains it — the capacity to recognise the pull of appetite, to weigh it against the good of health and rational self-governance, and to act from a settled commitment to the latter.

When we regard the generosity of a philanthropist as genuinely virtuous, we do so because the giving is animated by a concern for the recipients' well-being rather than by the desire for public recognition, and because the philanthropist gives from a stable disposition rather than on a passing whim.
definition: A character trait T constitutes a moral virtue if and only if (i) T is a stable and enduring disposition, (ii) T leads the agent to perform actions that promote the well-being of others, and (iii) the agent possesses T voluntarily rather than through compulsion.
conditions: T is a stable and enduring disposition
T leads the agent to perform actions that promote the well-being of others
The agent possesses T voluntarily rather than through compulsion
counterexample: Suppose a person, through years of voluntary effort, has cultivated a stable disposition to donate large sums to charitable causes, and these donations reliably promote the well-being of the recipients. The trait is stable, beneficial, and voluntarily acquired. All three conditions are met. Yet the person's motive is entirely self-interested: the donations are calculated to secure social prestige and political influence, and the person privately regards the beneficiaries with indifference or contempt. We should hesitate to call this trait a moral virtue, for the inner life of the agent is entirely at odds with the outward beneficence of the acts.
abductive_insight: What the background cases share — and what the definition fails to capture — is that genuine virtue requires not merely the outward production of beneficial effects from a stable and voluntary disposition, but an appropriate inner orientation: the agent must act from a concern for the right ends, not merely produce the right results as a by-product of self-serving motives.

When we reflect upon the character traits that we most readily call virtues, a pattern emerges that rewards careful attention. When we admire the courage of a soldier who risks death to rescue a comrade, our admiration is directed not merely at the beneficial result — the comrade saved — but at the inner orientation of the agent: the willingness to face mortal danger for the sake of another, sustained against powerful contrary impulses, and animated by genuine concern rather than the prospect of reward. When we praise the temperance of one who refrains from excess despite strong temptation, we praise the inner mastery — the capacity to weigh appetite against the good of rational self-governance and to act from a settled commitment to the latter. When we regard the philanthropist's generosity as virtuous, we do so in part because the giving flows from a concern for the recipients' welfare rather than from the desire for public recognition.

These cases invite a general characterisation: a character trait is a moral virtue if it is stable, voluntarily acquired, and productive of actions that promote the well-being of others. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this formulation may be constructed without difficulty. Suppose a person of considerable wealth has, through years of disciplined effort, cultivated a stable and enduring disposition to donate substantial sums to charitable causes. The donations are regular, generous, and reliably promote the welfare of their recipients. The disposition is voluntary, not imposed by external compulsion. Every condition of the proposed definition is met. Yet suppose the person's sole motive is the calculation that conspicuous philanthropy will secure social prestige and political influence. The person privately regards the beneficiaries with indifference, even contempt. One might observe that what the background cases share — and what the definition fails to require — is that the agent act from an appropriate inner orientation: a genuine concern for the ends that the virtuous action serves, rather than a merely instrumental use of beneficial effects as a means to self-interested goals.

background_cases: When the Athenian jury system required citizens to judge one another's cases by lot, the arrangement was valued not merely because the procedure treated all citizens alike but because it was embedded in a broader framework of civic equality — each citizen bore the reciprocal risk of being judged as well as judging, and the rotation of roles ensured that no permanent class of adjudicators could emerge to entrench its own interests.

When a modern employment tribunal applies standardised criteria to assess candidates for promotion, the procedure is regarded as just not merely because it treats each candidate according to the same rules but because the criteria themselves are related to the requirements of the role — they measure relevant competence rather than irrelevant characteristics, and they distribute the benefit of advancement in a manner that reflects genuine merit.

When a hospital allocates scarce organ donations according to a transparent waiting list ordered by medical urgency, the arrangement is regarded as just because the criterion of urgency bears a substantive relation to the purpose of the allocation — saving the lives most immediately at risk — and not merely because the list treats all patients according to the same rule.
definition: A procedure for distributing a benefit B among persons is just if and only if (i) the procedure applies the same rules to all persons, (ii) the rules are publicly known in advance, and (iii) the procedure is administered by an impartial authority.
conditions: The procedure applies the same rules to all persons
The rules are publicly known in advance
The procedure is administered by an impartial authority
counterexample: Suppose a university distributes scholarships according to a publicly announced rule, administered by an impartial committee, that awards funds to students on the basis of their height. The tallest students receive the largest scholarships. The procedure applies the same rule to all, the rule is publicly known, and the committee is impartial. All three conditions are met. Yet the distribution is manifestly unjust, for height bears no rational connection to academic need, merit, or any other consideration relevant to the purpose of scholarships.
abductive_insight: What the background cases share — and what the definition fails to require — is that the rules governing the distribution bear a substantive and rational connection to the purpose of the benefit being distributed. Procedural uniformity, publicity, and impartiality are insufficient without the further requirement that the criteria of distribution be relevant to the nature and aim of the benefit.
