When one examines the procedures that are most widely regarded as paradigms of just distribution, a structural feature emerges that repays close analysis. The Athenian jury system was valued not merely because it applied the same rules to every citizen but because the rotation of roles ensured that no permanent class could entrench its interests at the expense of others; the procedural uniformity was embedded in a broader framework of civic equality. Modern employment tribunals are regarded as just not merely because they treat each candidate by the same criteria but because those criteria bear a rational relation to the demands of the position — they measure competence, not irrelevant characteristics. A hospital's organ-allocation waiting list is considered just because the criterion of medical urgency bears a substantive connection to the purpose of the allocation: saving the lives most immediately at risk.

These cases invite a general characterisation: a distributive procedure is just when it applies the same rules to all, makes those rules publicly known in advance, and is administered by an impartial authority. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this formulation may be constructed from the allocation of educational resources. Suppose a university, through a duly constituted and impartial committee, announces that scholarships will henceforth be awarded according to the height of the applicants: the tallest students receive the most generous funding. The procedure applies the same rule to every applicant. The rule is publicly announced well in advance. The committee is scrupulously impartial, measuring each student with the same instrument and without favouritism. Every condition of the proposed definition is met. Yet the distribution is manifestly unjust, for height bears no rational connection to academic achievement, financial need, or any other consideration germane to the purpose of a scholarship. One might observe that what the background cases share — and what the definition fails to require — is that the distributing criteria bear a substantive and rational relation to the nature and purpose of the benefit being distributed. Without this, procedural uniformity, publicity, and impartiality are mere formalities that may cloak arbitrary and irrational distinctions.

background_cases: When a court sentences a burglar to a term of imprisonment, the punishment is regarded as just not merely because it is proportionate to the offence and imposed by a legitimate authority, but because the burglar had the capacity to understand the law, the opportunity to comply with it, and chose to violate it — the punishment responds to a genuine exercise of moral agency.

When a military tribunal disciplines a soldier for desertion, the punishment is regarded as appropriate not merely because the offence is serious and the tribunal legitimate, but because the soldier understood the obligation to serve, was capable of honouring it, and freely chose to abandon the post.

When a school imposes a penalty upon a student for deliberate cheating, the penalty is regarded as fair because the student understood the prohibition, had the ability to complete the work honestly, and chose instead to deceive — the penalty responds to a culpable exercise of will.
definition: A punitive measure M imposed upon a person P is morally justified if and only if (i) P has committed a specific wrongful act, (ii) M is proportionate to the gravity of the wrongful act, and (iii) M is imposed by a legitimate authority.
conditions: P has committed a specific wrongful act
M is proportionate to the gravity of the wrongful act
M is imposed by a legitimate authority
counterexample: Suppose that a person, due to a sudden and entirely unforeseeable psychotic episode, commits an act of criminal damage. The act is objectively wrongful. A legitimate court imposes a proportionate sentence. All three conditions are met. Yet the person, at the time of the act, was wholly incapable of understanding what was being done or of conforming behaviour to the requirements of the law. We should hesitate to regard the punishment as morally justified, for the person lacked the capacity for moral agency that makes punishment an appropriate response.
abductive_insight: What the background cases share — and what the definition fails to require — is that the person punished possessed, at the time of the wrongful act, the capacity to understand the relevant norm and to choose whether to comply with it. Without this condition of moral agency, punishment ceases to be a response to culpable choice and becomes the infliction of suffering upon one who could not have acted otherwise.
passage:
When we examine the cases in which punishment is most clearly regarded as morally justified, a structural feature emerges that the most straightforward definitions tend to overlook. When a court sentences a burglar to imprisonment, the justice of the punishment depends not merely upon the proportionality of the sentence and the legitimacy of the authority imposing it, but upon the fact that the burglar understood the law, had the opportunity to obey it, and chose to violate it. When a military tribunal disciplines a deserter, the appropriateness of the penalty rests upon the soldier's understanding of the obligation and free decision to abandon the post. When a school penalises a student for deliberate cheating, the fairness of the penalty depends upon the student's awareness of the prohibition and capacity to have acted honestly.

These cases invite a general characterisation: punishment is morally justified when a person has committed a wrongful act, the punishment is proportionate, and it is imposed by a legitimate authority. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this formulation may be drawn from the criminal law. Suppose a person, due to a sudden and entirely unforeseeable psychotic episode, smashes the windows of several shops. The act is objectively wrongful — it causes damage and violates the law. A duly constituted court imposes a sentence that is, by any measure, proportionate to the gravity of the offence. The authority is legitimate. Every condition of the proposed definition is satisfied. Yet at the time of the act, the person was wholly incapable of understanding what was being done, of recognising the wrongfulness of the conduct, or of conforming behaviour to the requirements of the law. It appears that we should hesitate to regard the punishment as morally justified. One might observe that what the background cases share — and what the definition fails to require — is that the person punished possessed, at the time of the act, the capacity for moral agency: the ability to understand the relevant norm and to choose whether to comply with it. Without this, punishment is not a response to culpable choice but the infliction of suffering upon one who could not have done otherwise.

background_cases: When we praise a soldier who overcomes his fear to rescue a comrade under fire, we recognise his courage as a virtue because his action is guided by a correct perception of the danger and a sound judgement that the good to be achieved — saving a life — warrants the risk. His fear is real, but his determination to act rightly in the face of it is what marks his conduct as courageous.

When we praise a firefighter who enters a burning building to save a child, we do so because she recognises the gravity of the peril, weighs it against the value of the life at stake, and acts from a settled disposition to choose rightly under pressure. Her courage consists not in the absence of fear but in the mastery of it by practical judgement.

When we praise a whistleblower who exposes corruption at great personal cost, we admire his willingness to endure social ostracism and professional ruin for the sake of what he correctly judges to be a serious wrong. His courage is manifested in his readiness to suffer for a genuine good.
definition: A person S possesses the virtue of courage if and only if (i) S habitually acts in the face of danger or difficulty, (ii) S acts from a settled disposition rather than momentary impulse, and (iii) S acts voluntarily, without external compulsion.
conditions: S habitually acts in the face of danger or difficulty
S acts from a settled disposition rather than momentary impulse
S acts voluntarily, without external compulsion
counterexample: Suppose a stunt performer habitually and voluntarily undertakes dangerous feats — leaping from buildings, driving through explosions — from a settled disposition cultivated over years of training. He acts without external compulsion and his conduct in the face of danger is entirely habitual. All three conditions are satisfied. Yet his actions are undertaken not in pursuit of any genuine good but solely for entertainment and financial gain, and he is indifferent to whether his feats serve any purpose beyond spectacle. We should hesitate to attribute the virtue of courage to a person whose habitual confrontation with danger is unconnected to any sound judgement about what is worth risking danger for.
abductive_insight: What the background cases share — and what the definition fails to capture — is that in every instance of genuine courage, the agent's willingness to face danger is guided by a correct apprehension of a good that warrants the risk. The definition, by requiring only habitual, settled, and voluntary action in the face of danger, omits the evaluative component: the agent must perceive correctly that the end pursued is genuinely worth the peril, and must act from that perception. Without this condition, the definition cannot distinguish courage from mere daring or recklessness.
passage:
Reflection upon the most instructive cases of courageous conduct reveals a structural feature that the most natural definitions of courage fail to articulate. When a soldier overcomes his fear to rescue a comrade under fire, we admire his courage because his action is guided by a sound judgement that the good at stake — a human life — warrants the risk. When a firefighter enters a burning building, her courage consists not in the mere willingness to face danger but in the mastery of fear by practical wisdom directed towards a genuine end. When a whistleblower endures professional ruin to expose corruption, his courage is manifested in the readiness to suffer for what he correctly judges to be a serious wrong.

These cases invite a general characterisation: a person possesses courage if he habitually and voluntarily acts in the face of danger from a settled disposition. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this formulation may be constructed from the world of professional stunt performance. Suppose a stunt performer has, over many years, cultivated a settled disposition to undertake dangerous feats — leaping from tall structures, driving vehicles through explosions, and the like. He does so habitually and voluntarily, without any external compulsion. All three conditions of the proposed definition are satisfied. Yet one might observe that his habitual confrontation with danger is undertaken not in pursuit of any genuine good but solely for entertainment and remuneration, and he is wholly indifferent to whether his feats serve any purpose beyond spectacle. We should hesitate to attribute the virtue of courage to such a person. What the background cases reveal — and what the definition fails to require — is that the courageous agent must be guided by a correct apprehension of a good that genuinely warrants the risk. Without this evaluative component, the definition cannot distinguish the truly courageous from those who merely court danger for its own sake or for trivial ends.

background_cases: When a hospital allocates scarce organs for transplantation on the basis of medical urgency and likelihood of benefit, we regard the allocation as just because it attends to the morally relevant features of each patient's situation — the severity of the need and the prospect of restoring health — rather than to irrelevant characteristics such as wealth, social standing, or personal connections.

When a university awards scholarships on the basis of academic merit and financial need, we regard the allocation as just because the criteria bear a rational and morally defensible relation to the purpose of the award: to foster learning among those who are both capable and in need of support.

When a court distributes the assets of a bankrupt estate among creditors in proportion to the sums owed to each, we regard the distribution as just because each creditor's claim is measured by a morally relevant factor — the magnitude of the debt — and the same standard applies to all.
definition: A distribution of scarce resources is just if and only if (i) the distribution is governed by criteria that are applied uniformly to all claimants, (ii) the criteria bear a rational relation to the purpose for which the resources are being distributed, and (iii) no claimant is favoured or disfavoured on the basis of morally irrelevant characteristics.
conditions: The distribution is governed by criteria that are applied uniformly to all claimants
The criteria bear a rational relation to the purpose for which the resources are being distributed
No claimant is favoured or disfavoured on the basis of morally irrelevant characteristics
counterexample: Suppose a municipality distributes access to clean drinking water on the basis of a single criterion applied uniformly to all residents: those who can pay the assessed fee receive water; those who cannot, do not. The criterion — ability to pay — is applied uniformly and bears a rational relation to the purpose of financing the water system. No resident is favoured on the basis of morally irrelevant characteristics; the rule treats all equally in that anyone who pays receives service. All three conditions are satisfied. Yet we should hesitate to call this distribution just when it results in the poorest residents being denied access to a resource necessary for survival. The uniformity of the criterion and its rational connection to financing do not, by themselves, ensure that the distribution is morally acceptable.
abductive_insight: What the background cases share — and what the definition fails to capture — is a sensitivity to the basic needs of the claimants. In each case, the distributive criteria are not merely uniform and rationally related to a purpose but are also constrained by a concern that no person be denied what is essential for a minimally decent existence. The definition, by requiring only uniformity, rational relation, and the absence of morally irrelevant favouritism, permits distributions that are procedurally impeccable but substantively cruel.
passage:
Reflection upon the most illuminating cases of just distribution reveals a feature that the most straightforward definitions of distributive justice fail to require. When a hospital allocates scarce organs on the basis of medical urgency and likelihood of benefit, the justice of the allocation derives not merely from the uniformity of the criteria but from their sensitivity to the gravity of each patient's need. When a university awards scholarships on the basis of merit and financial need, the criteria bear a rational relation to the purpose and attend to the circumstances of the applicants. When a court distributes the assets of a bankrupt estate in proportion to the debts owed, each creditor's claim is measured by a morally relevant standard applied equally to all.

These cases invite a general characterisation: a distribution is just when it is governed by uniform criteria that bear a rational relation to the distributive purpose and that exclude morally irrelevant favouritism. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this formulation may be drawn from the provision of essential public services. Suppose a municipality distributes access to clean drinking water on the basis of a single criterion applied uniformly to all residents: those who can pay the assessed fee receive water, and those who cannot are denied access. The criterion is applied uniformly. It bears a rational relation to the purpose of financing the water system. No resident is favoured on the basis of morally irrelevant characteristics; anyone who pays receives the service. All three conditions of the proposed definition are met. Yet one might observe that the distribution is not just when it condemns the poorest residents to go without a resource necessary for survival. What the background cases share — and what the definition fails to require — is a substantive concern for basic needs. A just distribution must not merely be procedurally uniform and rationally grounded; it must also ensure that no person is denied what is essential for a minimally decent existence.

background_cases: When a strong swimmer encounters a child drowning in a shallow pond, we hold that the swimmer is morally obligated to rescue the child, because the cost to the swimmer is trivial — wet clothing and a few minutes' delay — while the benefit is the preservation of a human life. The disproportion between the ease of rescue and the gravity of the harm grounds the obligation.

When a physician encounters a stranger who has collapsed in the street and requires simple first aid, we hold that the physician is morally obligated to provide assistance, because the physician's training equips her to help at minimal risk and inconvenience, while the stranger faces serious harm without intervention.

When a bystander witnesses a traffic accident and the injured party is unconscious, we hold that the bystander is obligated at least to summon emergency services, because the cost of making a telephone call is negligible while the failure to act may result in death.
definition: A person S is morally obligated to assist another person P in distress if and only if (i) S is aware that P is in distress, (ii) S has the capacity to provide effective assistance, and (iii) the cost to S of providing assistance is not disproportionate to the benefit to P.
conditions: S is aware that P is in distress
S has the capacity to provide effective assistance
The cost to S of providing assistance is not disproportionate to the benefit to P
counterexample: Suppose a wealthy philanthropist is made aware of a famine in a distant country. She has the financial capacity to provide effective assistance by donating funds to relief organisations. The cost to her — a small fraction of her fortune — is trivially disproportionate to the benefit of saving many lives. All three conditions of the proposed definition are satisfied. Yet if the definition is correct, the philanthropist is morally obligated to assist not only this famine but every distress of which she becomes aware, wherever it occurs, so long as she retains the capacity and the cost remains proportionate. The obligation, thus understood, has no natural limit: it extends to every suffering person on the planet of whose plight the philanthropist is informed, and it demands continuous sacrifice until the cost finally becomes disproportionate to the benefit. We should hesitate to accept a definition of obligation that demands, in effect, the dedication of the greater part of one's resources to alleviating the distress of strangers.
abductive_insight: What the background cases share — and what the definition fails to capture — is that the obligation of rescue arises in situations of immediate proximity and specific encounter, where a particular person confronts a particular emergency. The definition, by abstracting away from proximity and the specificity of the encounter, generates obligations of unlimited scope. It cannot distinguish the moral urgency of the drowning child before one's eyes from the moral claims of every suffering person of whom one is merely informed.
passage:
Reflection upon the clearest cases of moral obligation to assist others in distress reveals a structural feature that the most natural generalisation from those cases fails to preserve. When a strong swimmer encounters a drowning child in a shallow pond, the obligation to rescue arises from the conjunction of awareness, capacity, and the triviality of the cost relative to the gravity of the harm. When a physician encounters a collapsed stranger in the street, the same conjunction operates: the physician is aware of the distress, is equipped to help, and the cost is minimal. When a bystander witnesses an accident, the obligation to summon help is grounded in the same three factors.

These cases invite a general characterisation: a person is morally obligated to assist whenever he is aware of distress, has the capacity to help, and the cost of helping is not disproportionate to the benefit. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this formulation may be constructed by considering a wealthy philanthropist who learns of a famine in a distant country. She is aware of the distress, she has the capacity to provide effective assistance through charitable donation, and the cost — a small fraction of her fortune — is trivially disproportionate to the benefit of saving many lives. All three conditions are met. Yet the definition, thus applied, generates an obligation that extends to every suffering person on the planet of whose plight the philanthropist is informed, demanding continuous sacrifice until the cost finally becomes disproportionate. One might observe that this implication is difficult to accept. What the background cases share — and what the definition fails to require — is the element of immediate proximity and specific encounter: a particular person confronting a particular emergency before her eyes. The definition, by abstracting away from this feature, transforms a principle of rescue into a principle of unlimited beneficence, and in so doing loses the very specificity that gives the original obligation its force.

background_cases: When a student studies diligently throughout the term, masters the material, and performs well on the examination, we say she deserves the high mark she receives. Her desert is grounded in her effort and the quality of the work she produced: the outcome is the natural result of sustained voluntary exertion directed towards a legitimate end.

When a craftsman devotes years to perfecting his art and produces work of exceptional quality, we say he deserves the recognition and custom he receives. His desert is grounded in the prolonged voluntary effort and the excellence of its product.

When an athlete trains with dedication over many seasons and wins a competition through superior performance, we say she deserves the prize. Her desert rests on the sustained effort she invested and the quality of the performance that resulted from it.
definition: A person S deserves a favourable outcome F if and only if (i) S engaged in sustained voluntary effort directed towards the end that F rewards, (ii) S's effort produced a result of high quality, and (iii) the quality of S's result is superior to that of all competing claimants.
conditions: S engaged in sustained voluntary effort directed towards the end that F rewards
S's effort produced a result of high quality
The quality of S's result is superior to that of all competing claimants
counterexample: Suppose a lone researcher works in a field so specialised that no other person is engaged in it. She devotes years of sustained voluntary effort to the work and produces results of genuine quality and value. But because she has no competitors, condition (iii) — that the quality of her result be superior to that of all competing claimants — is either vacuously satisfied or, on a more natural reading, inapplicable, since there are no competing claimants against whom superiority could be measured. The definition ties desert to competitive superiority, but one might observe that the researcher deserves recognition for her work whether or not anyone else happens to be working in the same field. Desert grounded in effort and quality does not require that there be rivals whom one surpasses.
abductive_insight: The background cases all involved competitive contexts — an examination with multiple students, a market with multiple craftsmen, a contest with multiple athletes — and the definition was induced from those competitive settings. Condition (iii) reflects the accidental feature that each observed case involved comparisons among claimants. But desert can arise from effort and quality alone, without any competitive dimension. A person who works hard and produces excellent results may deserve recognition even if no one else is pursuing the same end. The correct analysis of desert must not require competitive superiority as a necessary condition.
passage:
Reflection upon several paradigmatic cases of desert might lead one to formulate a general criterion. When a student studies diligently and earns a high mark, her desert rests upon sustained effort and the quality of the result. When a craftsman perfects his art and receives recognition, his desert is similarly grounded. When an athlete trains and wins a competition, her desert derives from effort and superior performance. In each case, the deserving party worked hard, produced results of high quality, and surpassed competing claimants.

These cases invite a general characterisation: a person deserves a favourable outcome if she engaged in sustained voluntary effort, produced a result of high quality, and surpassed all competitors. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this formulation may be constructed by imagining a lone researcher working in a field so specialised that no other person is engaged in it. She devotes years of careful, voluntary effort to her work and produces results of genuine quality and significance. But she has no competitors; condition (iii) is either vacuously satisfied or, on the more natural reading, simply inapplicable, since there are no rival claimants against whom superiority might be measured. One might observe that it would be strange to deny that the researcher deserves recognition merely because she lacks competitors. Her desert is grounded in her effort and the quality of her work, and these grounds do not depend upon the existence of others whom she has surpassed. The background cases all happened to involve competitive settings, and condition (iii) was abstracted from that accidental feature. The correct analysis of desert must recognise that competitive superiority is a frequent accompaniment of desert but not a necessary condition of it.

background_cases: When we praise the temperate man, we note that he enjoys food and drink but does not pursue them to excess; his pleasure is governed by a settled disposition that maintains the proper measure. His temperance consists not in the absence of desire but in the right regulation of desire.

When we commend the generous man, we observe that he gives freely of his wealth to those in need, but not so freely as to impoverish himself or his dependants. His giving is guided by a stable character that discerns the fitting occasion and the appropriate amount.

When we admire the patient man, we remark that he endures hardship without complaint, not because he is insensible to suffering but because his settled disposition enables him to bear difficulty in pursuit of a worthy purpose.
definition: A person S possesses a moral virtue V if and only if (i) S has a settled disposition to act in accordance with V, (ii) S's disposition leads to actions that observe a mean between excess and deficiency, and (iii) S's virtuous actions are accompanied by the appropriate affective response.
conditions: S has a settled disposition to act in accordance with V
S's disposition leads to actions that observe a mean between excess and deficiency
S's virtuous actions are accompanied by the appropriate affective response
counterexample: Suppose a man has a settled disposition to tell the truth, and his truth-telling observes a mean between loquacity and secretiveness — he discloses what is fitting and withholds what is properly private. His candour is accompanied by the appropriate affective response: he feels satisfaction in honesty and discomfort at the thought of deception. All three conditions are satisfied. Yet consider a case in which this man, visiting a friend who is gravely ill and whose spirits are fragile, is asked about the friend's prognosis. The truthful answer would be devastating and serve no practical purpose. The man's settled disposition compels him to speak honestly, and he does so, observing his characteristic mean, with his characteristic feeling. His truth-telling, virtuous in ordinary circumstances, becomes a source of unnecessary cruelty in this particular situation.
abductive_insight: What the background cases share — and what the definition fails to capture — is that the virtuous person does not merely act from a settled disposition observing a mean; he exercises practical wisdom to recognise when the characteristic expression of a virtue must be modulated or even withheld in light of the particular circumstances. Without the requirement of situational judgement, a settled and well-regulated disposition may produce actions that are mechanically virtuous in form but practically harmful.
passage:
The cases we most readily cite in praise of moral virtue share a common structure. The temperate man enjoys food and drink but does not pursue them to excess; his pleasure is regulated by a settled disposition that holds to the proper measure. The generous man gives freely but not extravagantly, guided by a stable character that discerns the fitting occasion. The patient man endures hardship not from insensibility but from a disposition that enables him to bear difficulty in pursuit of a worthy end. These cases invite a general account: a person possesses a virtue when he has a settled disposition whose expression observes a mean between excess and deficiency, accompanied by the appropriate affective response.

Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this formulation may be constructed from the virtue of truthfulness. Suppose a man has a settled disposition to speak honestly, and his candour holds to a mean between garrulousness and reticence. He discloses what is fitting, withholds what is properly private, and feels the satisfaction of honesty in his dealings. All three conditions are met. Yet let us imagine that this man visits a gravely ill friend whose spirits are exceedingly fragile. The friend asks about his prognosis, and the truthful answer is devastating. The man's disposition compels him to answer honestly, observing his characteristic mean, with his characteristic feeling of rectitude. His truth-telling, virtuous in ordinary circumstances, becomes a source of unnecessary suffering. What the background cases reveal — and what the definition neglects — is that the virtuous person exercises practical wisdom: he recognises when the habitual expression of a virtue must be modulated in light of particular circumstances. A settled disposition observing a mean is not sufficient; what is required in addition is the capacity for situational judgement that determines when and how the disposition ought to be expressed.

background_cases: When a court sentences two offenders who have committed the same crime under the same circumstances to the same punishment, we regard the outcome as just, for like cases have been treated alike.

When an employer pays two workers who perform the same task with the same competence the same wage, we regard this as fair, for the principle of equal treatment for equal performance has been honoured.
When a university admits two applicants with identical qualifications under the same criteria, we regard the process as equitable, for no arbitrary distinction has been drawn between them.
definition: A procedure or institution treats persons justly if and only if (i) it applies the same rules to all persons in relevantly similar circumstances, (ii) the rules are known and publicly stated in advance, and (iii) no person is subjected to an arbitrary distinction that lacks a rational basis.
conditions: It applies the same rules to all persons in relevantly similar circumstances
The rules are known and publicly stated in advance
No person is subjected to an arbitrary distinction lacking a rational basis
counterexample: Suppose a society applies a uniform rule: all persons above the age of eighteen must pay the same flat tax, regardless of income. The rule is publicly known, applies equally to all adults, and draws no arbitrary distinction — the age threshold has a rational basis. All three conditions are satisfied. Yet a person earning barely enough to survive pays the same amount as a person of enormous wealth. The rule treats them identically, but the burden falls with crushing weight on the poor and trivial weight on the rich. The formal equality of the procedure masks a substantive inequality.
abductive_insight: What the background cases share is that equal treatment was appropriate because the persons involved were substantively similar in the respects relevant to the distribution at issue. The definition fails to require that the equality of treatment correspond to a genuine equality of relevant circumstances, so that a rule which is formally uniform may produce substantively unjust outcomes when applied to persons whose situations differ in morally relevant ways.
passage:
The cases most commonly cited as paradigms of just treatment share a notable feature. When a court sentences like offenders alike, when an employer pays equally competent workers equally, and when a university admits equally qualified applicants under the same standard, the justice of the outcome rests upon the fact that like cases are treated alike. These examples invite a general account: a procedure treats persons justly when it applies the same rules to all in relevantly similar circumstances, the rules are publicly known, and no arbitrary distinction is drawn.

Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this formulation may be drawn from the domain of taxation. Suppose a society imposes a uniform flat tax upon all adults: every person above the age of eighteen pays the same sum. The rule is publicly known, applies equally to all, and draws no arbitrary distinction — the age threshold is rationally grounded. All three conditions are satisfied. Yet consider the person who earns barely enough for subsistence and the person of great wealth. Both pay the same amount, but the burden is crushing for the first and negligible for the second. The formal equality of treatment masks a profound inequality of impact. What the background cases reveal — and what the definition neglects — is that equal treatment was fitting in those cases because the persons involved were substantively similar in the respects relevant to the distribution. When circumstances differ in morally relevant ways, a rule that is formally identical for all may produce outcomes that are substantively unjust. The definition, by requiring only formal uniformity, public notice, and rational distinction, fails to demand that equal treatment be appropriate to the actual situation of those to whom it is applied.

background_cases: When a man deliberately sets fire to a barn, knowing it to be occupied, and the occupants are injured, we hold him responsible because he chose to act, foresaw the harm, and could have refrained.

When a woman knowingly sells contaminated food, foreseeing that buyers will fall ill, and illness results, we hold her responsible because she acted voluntarily, anticipated the consequences, and had the power to act otherwise.

When a soldier obeys an order to destroy a village, knowing that civilians will be harmed, and civilians are killed, we hold the soldier at least partly responsible because he acted with awareness of the consequences and could, in principle, have refused the order.
definition: A person S is morally responsible for an outcome O if and only if (i) S performed a voluntary action that was a cause of O, (ii) S foresaw or could reasonably have foreseen O as a consequence of their action, and (iii) S had the ability to act otherwise than they did.
conditions: S performed a voluntary action that was a cause of O
S foresaw or could reasonably have foreseen O
S had the ability to act otherwise
counterexample: Suppose a factory owner, through years of negligent indifference, allows toxic waste to accumulate in storage tanks without proper safeguards. He could have arranged for safe disposal at any time and can reasonably foresee that the tanks will eventually corrode. One night, an earthquake ruptures the tanks and the waste poisons the local water supply. The owner performed no voluntary action on the night of the spill; the immediate cause was the earthquake. He did not act; he omitted to act over many years. Yet he foresaw the risk, he had the ability to prevent it, and the harm resulted from a pattern of neglect rather than from a single voluntary act.
abductive_insight: What the background cases share is that each involved a discrete voluntary act that caused the harm. The definition, by requiring a voluntary action as a cause, fails to account for responsibility arising from culpable omissions or from sustained patterns of negligent inaction. A person who fails to act when he ought to have acted may be as responsible for the resulting harm as one who actively brings it about, yet the definition excludes him because no single voluntary action of his was a proximate cause of the outcome.
passage:
The cases that most clearly engage our sense of moral responsibility involve discrete acts: a man sets fire to a building, a woman sells tainted provisions, a soldier carries out a destructive order. In each case, the responsible party performed a voluntary action, foresaw the consequences, and could have acted otherwise. These features suggest a general account: a person is morally responsible for an outcome when he performed a voluntary action that caused it, foresaw or could have foreseen it, and had the ability to act differently.

Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this formulation may be drawn from the domain of industrial negligence. Suppose a factory owner, through years of indifferent neglect, allows toxic waste to accumulate in deteriorating storage tanks without arranging for safe disposal. He can foresee that the tanks will eventually fail, and he has the means and the authority to prevent the accumulation. One night, an earthquake ruptures the tanks, and the waste poisons the water supply of a neighbouring village. The owner performed no voluntary action on the night of the catastrophe; the immediate cause was a natural event. What he did — or rather, failed to do — was to maintain safe storage over a period of years. The harm arose not from a discrete act but from a sustained pattern of culpable inaction. What the background cases share, and what the definition takes as essential, is a voluntary action as the cause. But the factory owner's responsibility is no less real for the absence of such an act. The definition, by requiring a voluntary action as a necessary condition, fails to accommodate responsibility for harms that result from culpable omissions extended over time.

background_cases: When a student studies diligently for an examination and earns the highest mark, we say he deserves recognition because his achievement resulted from sustained voluntary effort.
When an athlete trains for years and wins a championship, we say she deserves the prize because her victory was the fruit of deliberate, arduous preparation.

When a craftsman spends months perfecting a piece of furniture and produces a work of exceptional quality, we say he deserves commendation because the excellence of the product is attributable to his skill and labour.
definition: A person S deserves a favourable outcome F if and only if (i) F resulted from S's voluntary effort, (ii) S's effort was sustained and significant, and (iii) the connection between S's effort and F is direct, not mediated by luck or accident.
conditions: F resulted from S's voluntary effort
S's effort was sustained and significant
The connection between S's effort and F is direct and not mediated by luck or accident
counterexample: Suppose a farmer works tirelessly for an entire season, tending his fields with extraordinary care and skill. His effort is voluntary, sustained, and significant. Yet whether his harvest is abundant or meagre depends substantially upon the weather — a factor entirely beyond his control. In a year of favourable rains, the harvest is bountiful, and all three conditions appear satisfied: the good outcome resulted from his effort, the effort was sustained, and the connection seems direct. In a year of drought, the harvest fails despite identical effort. The farmer in the bountiful year is said to deserve his good fortune; the farmer in the drought year is denied the same verdict. Yet the only difference between them is the weather, and neither farmer controlled it. The condition requiring a direct and unmediated connection between effort and outcome is never truly satisfied in any agricultural endeavour, since the outcome always depends in part upon circumstances beyond the farmer's control.
abductive_insight: What the background cases share is that the connection between effort and outcome appeared direct because the domains — examination, athletics, craftsmanship — offered relatively controlled conditions in which the influence of external fortune was minimised. The definition assumes that effort and outcome can be connected without the mediation of luck, but in most human endeavours this assumption is false. The definition fails to recognise that desert may attach to effort and intention independently of whether the outcome, shaped as it inevitably is by factors beyond the agent's control, is favourable.
passage:
The cases we most naturally invoke as paradigms of desert share a structural feature that may mislead us into treating it as necessary. When a student earns the highest mark, an athlete wins a championship, or a craftsman produces a masterpiece, the favourable outcome appears to flow directly from sustained voluntary effort, with minimal interference from fortune. These cases suggest a general account: a person deserves a favourable outcome when it resulted from sustained voluntary effort and the connection between effort and outcome is direct and unmediated by luck.

Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this formulation may be drawn from the life of agriculture. Suppose a farmer works tirelessly for an entire season, tending his fields with extraordinary diligence and skill. His effort is voluntary, sustained, and significant. In a year of favourable weather, the harvest is bountiful, and the definition's conditions appear satisfied. In a year of drought, the harvest fails despite identical effort, and condition (iii) is exposed as unsatisfied, since the outcome was mediated by a factor — the weather — entirely beyond the farmer's control. Yet the farmer's effort was the same in both years; if he deserves reward in the first, it is difficult to see why he does not deserve it in the second. What the background cases reveal is that the connection between effort and outcome appeared direct only because the domains in question — examinations, athletics, craftsmanship — afforded relatively controlled conditions. In most human endeavours, outcomes are inevitably shaped by fortune. The definition, by requiring an unmediated connection, fails to recognise that desert may properly attach to effort and intention quite apart from whether the outcome, influenced as it always is by circumstance, proves favourable.

background_cases: A soldier who endures great physical danger to protect his comrades in battle is widely regarded as courageous, for he faces peril willingly in the service of others.
A whistleblower who risks career ruin to expose corporate fraud is likewise deemed courageous, for she confronts serious personal loss in order to serve a higher moral purpose.
A mountaineer who climbs a treacherous peak despite extreme danger is often called courageous, for he voluntarily accepts grave risk in pursuit of a challenging goal.
definition: A person acts courageously if and only if (i) the person voluntarily confronts a significant risk of harm, (ii) the person is aware of the danger, and (iii) the person acts in pursuit of a goal that the person regards as valuable.
conditions: The person voluntarily confronts a significant risk of harm
The person is aware of the danger
The person acts in pursuit of a goal that the person regards as valuable
counterexample: Suppose a person who is pathologically addicted to gambling voluntarily enters a dangerous underground gambling den in a district notorious for violent crime. He is fully aware of the danger to his person. He acts in pursuit of a goal he regards as immensely valuable — the thrill of wagering large sums. All three conditions are met, yet one should hesitate to call his conduct courageous, for the goal, though subjectively valued, is not one that merits the risk, and the voluntariness of his action is compromised by his compulsive condition.
abductive_insight: The background cases share a feature that the inferred definition fails to capture: in each, the goal pursued is one that an impartial observer would recognise as genuinely worthy, and the agent acts from rational self-governance rather than compulsion. The inferred definition, by relying on the agent's own valuation of the goal, cannot distinguish courage from reckless indulgence of appetite, revealing that inductive generalisation from paradigm cases may overlook the normative constraints that implicitly govern our selection of those cases.
passage:
When we survey paradigmatic instances of courage, certain common features present themselves. The soldier who holds his position under fire, the whistleblower who sacrifices her career for the public good, and the mountaineer who pits himself against a formidable peak all share a willingness to confront danger in pursuit of something they value. It is tempting to abstract from these cases a general principle: courage consists in the voluntary and knowing acceptance of significant risk in the service of a valued end.

Yet this generalisation proves vulnerable when extended beyond the cases from which it was drawn. Let us imagine a compulsive gambler who, fully aware of the violent dangers attending an illicit gambling establishment, enters it voluntarily in pursuit of the excitement he prizes above all else. Each of the inferred conditions is satisfied. He confronts danger knowingly and willingly, and the goal he pursues is one he regards as supremely valuable. Yet one might observe that calling this conduct courageous would be to misuse the term. The difficulty lies in the fact that our paradigm cases were tacitly selected for the worthiness of their ends and the rational autonomy of their agents, features that the inferred definition does not capture. We are thus led to recognise that an abductive generalisation drawn from commendable instances may silently presuppose normative criteria that are not encoded in the surface conditions it identifies, and that the extension of such a generalisation to cases lacking those tacit criteria exposes its insufficiency.

background_cases: In a factory, workers who produce more units receive higher wages, and this arrangement is generally regarded as fair.
In a university, students who submit superior work receive higher marks, and this system is considered just.
In an athletic competition, runners who finish fastest receive the highest prizes, and spectators accept this as equitable.
definition: A distribution is just if and only if (i) rewards are allocated in proportion to individual performance, (ii) the criteria of performance are applied uniformly to all participants, and (iii) no participant is arbitrarily excluded from the opportunity to perform.
conditions: Rewards are allocated in proportion to individual performance
The criteria of performance are applied uniformly to all participants
No participant is arbitrarily excluded from the opportunity to perform
counterexample: Suppose that in a farming community, land is distributed among families in proportion to the crop yield each family achieves. The criteria are applied uniformly, and no family is excluded from the opportunity to farm. Yet one family, through no fault of its own, has been assigned infertile soil that produces meagre yields regardless of effort, while another family enjoys rich alluvial land. The distribution tracks performance exactly, but the underlying conditions of performance are grossly unequal. It appears unjust that the family on poor soil should receive less when its inferior yield reflects disadvantage rather than deficiency of effort.
abductive_insight: The background cases all involved contexts in which the conditions of performance were roughly equal — factory workers using the same machines, students receiving the same instruction, runners competing on the same track. The inferred definition assumed a level playing field without stating it, because the paradigm cases provided one. The farming case, by introducing unequal starting conditions, reveals that proportionality to performance is just only when the conditions under which performance occurs are themselves fair, a requirement that the abductive inference failed to articulate because it was never tested in the sample.
passage:
The principle that rewards should follow performance enjoys wide acceptance, and paradigmatic cases of just distribution seem to confirm it. Factory workers paid by output, students graded by the quality of their work, and athletes ranked by their finishing times all instantiate a pattern in which higher performance yields greater reward, the criteria are uniformly applied, and participation is open to all. From these cases, one might reasonably infer that a distribution is just when it satisfies these three conditions.

Yet the inference conceals a presupposition that the background cases happened to satisfy. In each paradigmatic case, the conditions of performance were roughly comparable: the workers used the same machines, the students received the same instruction, the runners competed on the same track. Now suppose a farming community in which land is allocated according to crop yield. The criteria are uniform and participation is open, yet one family has been assigned infertile ground while another cultivates rich soil. Yield is proportional to the fertility of the land rather than to the diligence of the farmer, and the resulting distribution, though it tracks performance faithfully, strikes us as unjust. One might observe that the difficulty lies in the inequality of the conditions under which performance occurs, a factor that the background cases neutralised without ever making explicit. The abductive generalisation, having been drawn from a sample in which starting conditions were fair, failed to encode fairness of starting conditions as a separate requirement, and its inadequacy becomes manifest only when this tacit assumption is violated.

background_cases: A bystander who sees a child drowning in a shallow pond and can rescue the child at trivial personal cost is widely held to be morally obliged to act.
A physician who encounters a person in cardiac arrest in a public place and possesses the skills to perform resuscitation is regarded as morally obliged to intervene.
A passer-by who witnesses a traffic accident and can call for emergency services at no personal risk is considered morally obliged to summon help.
definition: A person has a moral obligation to aid another if and only if (i) the other person faces a serious threat to life or well-being, (ii) the potential rescuer can provide effective aid, and (iii) the cost to the rescuer is minor relative to the harm averted.
conditions: The other person faces a serious threat to life or well-being
The potential rescuer can provide effective aid
The cost to the rescuer is minor relative to the harm averted
counterexample: Suppose a surgeon walking through a hospital corridor encounters a patient in critical condition whom she could save by performing an emergency procedure. The patient faces a mortal threat, the surgeon can provide effective aid, and the personal cost to the surgeon is trivial. All three conditions are satisfied. Yet the patient is a convicted criminal who has escaped from lawful custody, and the authorities are moments away from recapturing him. The surgeon's intervention would enable the patient to flee and endanger others. It appears that the surgeon's obligation to aid is at least suspended, if not entirely defeated, by the foreseeable consequences of her intervention for the safety of third parties.
abductive_insight: The background cases all involved situations in which aiding the imperilled person carried no adverse consequences for anyone else. The drowning child, the cardiac arrest victim, and the accident victim could all be helped without creating danger to third parties. The inferred definition, drawn from these benign scenarios, contains no condition addressing the effects of rescue on others. The surgeon's case reveals that the obligation to aid is not determined solely by the dyadic relationship between rescuer and victim but is sensitive to the broader consequences of intervention, a dimension that the background cases never tested because it was never at issue.
passage:
Paradigmatic cases of the obligation to rescue share a clear structure: someone faces grave peril, a bystander can help at modest cost, and the aid would be effective. The child in the shallow pond, the cardiac arrest victim in the public square, and the accident on the highway all conform to this pattern. From these cases, one might reasonably generalise that the obligation to aid arises whenever serious harm threatens, effective assistance is available, and the cost of providing it is small.

Yet the generalisation proves incomplete when applied beyond its original domain. Suppose a surgeon in a hospital corridor encounters an escaped prisoner in critical condition. The threat to the prisoner's life is genuine, the surgeon's skill is adequate, and the personal cost of intervention is negligible. All three inferred conditions are satisfied. But suppose that the prisoner, if stabilised, will exploit the opportunity to flee and pose a serious danger to others. It appears that the surgeon's obligation to aid is complicated, if not overridden, by the foreseeable consequences of rescue for innocent third parties. One might observe that in every background case the rescue was wholly benign in its wider effects — saving the drowning child endangered no one, resuscitating the stranger threatened no further harm. The abductive inference, shaped by these uncomplicated scenarios, failed to incorporate any condition relating to the broader consequences of the rescuer's intervention. This omission illustrates a recurring limitation of inductive generalisation from morally simple cases: the conditions that emerge may be necessary but not sufficient, because the simplicity of the paradigm cases ensured that complicating factors were systematically absent.

background_cases: A patient who is clearly informed of the risks of surgery, deliberates for several days, and signs a consent form is regarded as having given valid consent.
A homeowner who, after careful consideration, agrees to sell her property at a price she considers fair is taken to have consented validly to the transaction.
A research subject who reads a detailed information sheet, asks questions, and voluntarily signs a participation agreement is treated as having given valid consent.
definition: A person's consent to an arrangement is valid if and only if (i) the person is informed of the material facts, (ii) the person deliberates and reaches a considered judgement, and (iii) the person expresses agreement without coercion.
conditions: The person is informed of the material facts
The person deliberates and reaches a considered judgement
The person expresses agreement without coercion
counterexample: Suppose an elderly woman is fully informed of the terms of a financial agreement, deliberates carefully over several days, and voluntarily signs the contract without any pressure. Yet suppose further that she is in the early stages of a degenerative cognitive condition that impairs her capacity to appreciate the long-term consequences of her decision, though she remains capable of understanding the immediate facts and deliberating in the short term. All three conditions are satisfied, yet her consent seems compromised, for her capacity to evaluate the significance of the arrangement for her own future welfare is materially diminished.
abductive_insight: The background cases all involved persons of full cognitive capacity — a patient capable of weighing long-term surgical consequences, a homeowner able to appreciate the financial implications of selling property, a research subject competent to assess future risks. The inferred definition captured the informational and procedural dimensions of consent but took cognitive capacity for granted because it was uniformly present. The case of the elderly woman with diminished capacity reveals that information, deliberation, and freedom from coercion do not suffice when the person's ability to appreciate the significance of the facts is impaired. Abductive generalisation from a sample of fully competent consenters will systematically overlook the capacity condition because it is never tested.
passage:
When we examine paradigmatic instances of valid consent — the surgical patient who deliberates and signs, the homeowner who agrees to sell after due reflection, the research subject who reads and questions before participating — we find a common structure. Each person is informed of the relevant facts, engages in genuine deliberation, and expresses agreement freely. From these cases, one might infer that these three conditions jointly constitute valid consent.

Yet the inference presupposes something that the background cases all satisfied without making explicit. Suppose an elderly woman who is fully informed of the terms of a complex financial arrangement, who takes several days to deliberate, and who signs the agreement entirely of her own free will. She is, however, in the early stages of a degenerative cognitive condition that, while leaving her short-term understanding intact, significantly impairs her ability to appreciate the long-term consequences of her decision. Each of the three inferred conditions is met, yet one should hesitate to regard her consent as fully valid. The difficulty is that the background cases all involved persons whose cognitive capacities were unimpaired, and the inferred definition, having been drawn from this sample, encoded no condition pertaining to the depth of the consenter's capacity to appreciate what she is agreeing to. We are thus led to observe that abductive inference from cases of competent consent will inevitably overlook the requirement of adequate decisional capacity, for the very competence of the paradigm agents ensures that this requirement is never put to the test.
